Under cross-examination he added that he had been detained and searched at a place called Jayasiri hotel. His wife testified under oath that police came to their house looking for him and that he never trafficked or used drugs, but the courts found this defence evidence not credible and insufficient to raise a reasonable doubt.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal re-evaluated the evidence and concluded that the prosecution had proved possession of heroin by the appellant beyond reasonable doubt, relying principally on the consistent testimony of PW-01 and PW-03 and the unchallenged chain of productions. It held that the failure to call PC Milroy did not create a vital missing link, since he merely received the tip-off and pointed out the appellant, while the searching officers testified fully, and that minor discrepancies did not affect trustworthiness. The court stressed that the defence failed to confront prosecution witnesses with the allegation that heroin was planted and that the appellant's version and his wife's supporting evidence were afterthoughts which the High Court had properly rejected. Applying authorities on the effect of not calling every witness, the significance of cross-examination and the treatment of trivial inconsistencies, it found no misdirection of law or fact and affirmed the conviction and sentence of life imprisonment.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This appeal arose from the conviction of the appellant in the High Court of Chilaw for possession of 2.25 grams of heroin and the imposition of life imprisonment, while he was acquitted of trafficking. On appeal he argued that the prosecution detection story was fabricated, that contradictions and omissions undermined the credibility of the police witnesses, that the failure to call PC Milroy was fatal, and that the trial judge erred in rejecting his and his wife's evidence. The Court of Appeal held that PW-01 and PW-03 were credible, that any inconsistencies were minor and non-material, that the non-calling of Milroy did not create a vital gap since the searching officers testified, and that the defence version was an unconvincing afterthought not put to the prosecution in cross-examination. Finding no legal or procedural error in the High Court's reasoning, the Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal and affirmed the conviction and sentence of life imprisonment.
prosecution_counsel: Anoopa de Silva, SSC for the Respondent
defence_counsel: Amila Palliyage with Ms. S. Udugampola for the Accused-Appellant
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court of Chilaw accepted the evidence of the police officers regarding the receipt of information, the raid, the search and the recovery of heroin, and found the possession charge proved beyond reasonable doubt while acquitting on trafficking for lack of specific evidence. It rejected the appellant's and his wife's testimony as not credible and insufficient to undermine the prosecution narrative. The Court of Appeal endorsed this approach, holding that the trial judge had correctly considered the totality of the evidence and committed no misdirection in preferring the prosecution version over the defence.
precedents_cited_list: Walimunige John v. State – 76 NLR 488 | Sarwan Singh v. State of Punjab – 2002 AIR SC 3652 | State of Uttar Pradesh v. Anthony – 1985 AIR SC 48 | Attorney General v. Sandanam Pitchi Mary Theresa – (2011) 2 SLR 292 | Bogmbhai Hirjibhai v. State of Gujarat – AIR (1983) SC 753 | Jayasena v. The Queen – 72 NLR 313 | Pilippu Mandige Nalaka Krishantha Thisera v. The Attorney General – CA 87/2005, decided on 17-05-2007 | Don Samantha Jude Anthony v. The Attorney General – CA 303/2006, decided on 11-07-2012
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal re-assessed the totality of the evidence for credibility, material contradictions and sufficiency of proof beyond reasonable doubt, applying principles on failure to call witnesses, effect of trivial inconsistencies and the significance of unchallenged testimony.

judges: K. Priyantha Fernando J. (P/CA) | Wickum A. Kaluarachchi J.
offence_sections: Section 140 of the Penal Code; Section 300 read with Section 146 and Section 32 of the Penal Code; Section 323 read with Section 146 and Section 32 of the Penal Code; Section 4 of the Offences against Public Property Act read with Sections 146, 32 and 380 of the Penal Code; Section 344 read with Sections 146 and 32 of the Penal Code
offence_category: Attempted murder, unlawful assembly, robbery of public property, assault on police officers
brief_facts_summary: On 2006-03-30, the day of a provincial council election, police officers from Rasnayaka Police Station went to investigate illegal sand mining being carried out with heavy machinery. After detaining two trucks, they pursued a backhoe whose driver fled, leaving the machine in a shrub. When officers tried to start the backhoe with the help of mechanics, the 11th accused led a gang, including the other accused, who assaulted the officers and robbed PW2's official firearm. PW2 suffered severe head injuries, including a fractured skull, loss of vision in one eye and facial disfigurement, resulting in the loss of his employment as a police officer.
evidence_type_primary: Prosecution narrative of events and accounts of injured police officers who were present at the scene
evidence_type_secondary: Medical information regarding the serious and permanent injuries suffered by PW2 and other documentary material placed before the sentencing court
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Conviction based on guilty plea on counts 1 to 5 namely: being a member of an unlawful assembly contrary to Section 140 of the Penal Code; attempted murder contrary to Section 300 read with Section 146 of the Penal Code; two counts of voluntarily causing hurt to deter police officers from their duty contrary to Section 323 read with Section 146 of the Penal Code; and robbery of an official firearm contrary to Section 4 of the Offences against Public Property Act read with Sections 146 and 380 of the Penal Code, with counts 7 to 11 treated as alternatives and not separately sentenced
hc_sentence_type: Suspended rigorous imprisonment on several counts with fines and compensation
hc_judgment_summary: Upon the accused respondents (except the 3rd accused) pleading guilty to counts 1 to 5, the High Court imposed three months' rigorous imprisonment on counts 1, 2 and 5, and fines on all five counts, but suspended the imprisonment terms for ten years. The court also ordered each accused to pay compensation to PW2, PW3 and PW4, and did not impose sentences on counts 6 to 11 considering them embedded or alternative counts. The Attorney General appealed on the basis that these sentences were manifestly inadequate and contrary to the statutory minimum and suspension provisions.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Attorney General appealed contending that the High Court erred in imposing a suspended sentence on count 5, an offence under the Offences against Public Property Act carrying a mandatory minimum term of imprisonment. It was argued that the trial judge failed to impose appropriate terms of imprisonment overall, especially in light of the aggravating circumstances such as the brutal assault on police officers and the robbery of a police firearm. The State further complained that the trial judge did not properly consider the relevant legal principles and aggravating factors when deciding the amount of compensation and the decision to suspend imprisonment.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Complaint that the suspended sentence imposed for count 5, which attracts a mandatory minimum term of imprisonment, was erroneous and contrary to law and fact. | Allegation that the High Court failed to impose appropriate and sufficiently severe terms of imprisonment on the accused respondents in light of the seriousness of the offences. | Contention that the High Court failed to consider the aggravating circumstances, including the nature of the assault on police officers and the robbery of a police firearm, when fixing compensation and deciding on a non-custodial sentence. | Argument that the High Court misapplied or ignored the mandatory minimum sentencing provisions and the statutory restriction on suspending imprisonment where a minimum term is prescribed.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal did not re-evaluate trial testimony in detail because the accused had pleaded guilty, but it relied on the prosecution's facts and the serious injuries to PW2, PW3 and PW4 when assessing aggravation. It treated PW2's permanent disabilities and the fact that the officers were performing their lawful duties at the time of the attack as key factors justifying an enhanced custodial sentence. The court also considered fingerprint reports establishing prior convictions of some accused when individualising sentences.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal did not base its decision on competing probabilities of guilt, as guilt was admitted, but rather on the seriousness of the conduct and the need for deterrent punishment; it did not apply a benefit of doubt analysis.
defence_version_summary: At the appeal, counsel for the respondents did not dispute the facts but relied on mitigation, emphasising that the offences occurred about fifteen years earlier, that the accused had families who would suffer from imprisonment, and that substantial compensation had already been ordered in civil proceedings. They relied on Supreme Court authorities suggesting that courts may deviate from statutory minimum sentences and argued for continued non-custodial treatment, also pointing out that some accused were first offenders and that the 9th accused had spent time in remand. The Court of Appeal held that these factors, including lapse of time, did not outweigh the need for immediate custodial sentences given the gravity of the offences and the harm caused.
legal_errors_description: The Court of Appeal held that the High Court erred by imposing a term of imprisonment below the statutory minimum for count 5 and by suspending that term in direct contravention of Section 303(2)(a) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act. It also criticised the trial judge for failing to give any reasons for deviating from the mandatory minimum sentence and for not explaining the basis for the suspended sentences in the sentencing judgment.
procedural_defects_description: No distinct procedural defects in the conduct of the trial were identified; the errors found related to sentencing discretion and failure to follow the statutory framework for minimum sentences and suspension of imprisonment.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal analysed the statutory minimum sentence for robbery of public property and the limitations on suspending sentences under Section 303 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, concluding that the High Court's lenient and suspended sentences were grossly inadequate and unlawful. It emphasised the aggravating features, including the organised gang assault on police officers on duty, the brutal injuries and permanent disability of PW2, the robbery of a police firearm, and prior convictions of some accused, and stressed the need for deterrent sentences to denounce such attacks on law enforcement. While acknowledging mitigating factors such as guilty pleas, first-offender status for some and lapse of time, the court held these did not justify a non-custodial outcome and substituted custodial rigorous imprisonment terms with fines and compensation, ordered to run concurrently subject to default terms.
coa_sentence_type: Enhanced rigorous imprisonment terms between two and five years on main counts, with fines and compensation orders, to be served as immediate custodial sentences rather than suspended terms
brief_judgment_file_summary: This appeal by the Attorney General challenged the lenient, largely suspended sentences imposed by the High Court of Kurunegala on twenty accused who had pleaded guilty to unlawful assembly, attempted murder, causing hurt to deter police officers and robbing a police firearm arising from a 2006 gang assault on officers investigating illegal sand mining. The High Court had imposed three-month rigorous imprisonment terms on principal counts, suspended for ten years, together with fines and compensation, and treated alternative counts as embedded. The Court of Appeal held that the High Court had ignored the mandatory minimum sentence and the statutory bar on suspending imprisonment for the robbery of public property, and had failed to give reasons for its departure from the statutory framework, while also underestimating the gravity of the assault and the permanent injuries to PW2. Applying principles of deterrence and denunciation, and considering aggravating and mitigating factors, the Court of Appeal set aside the suspended terms and substituted immediate custodial rigorous imprisonment sentences of varying lengths, with fines and compensation retained, ordering imprisonment terms to run concurrently and default sentences consecutively.
prosecution_counsel: Shaminda Wickrema, State Counsel for the Complainant–Appellant
defence_counsel: Palitha Fernando PC; Lakmal Karunaratne; Ranil Samarasooriya; Nalaka Samarakoon; Duminda De Alwis; Charuni De Alwis
cause_of_death_exact_text: Not applicable as the victim did not die; PW2 sustained serious permanent injuries including a fractured skull, loss of vision in one eye and facial disfigurement.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court accepted the accuseds' guilty pleas and imposed short terms of rigorous imprisonment on key counts, suspended for ten years, together with fines and substantial compensation, reasoning implicitly that the suspension was appropriate despite the serious offences and minimum sentence provisions. It treated some counts as alternative or embedded and therefore did not impose separate sentences on them. The Court of Appeal found that the High Court had failed to articulate reasons, had imposed imprisonment below the statutory minimum and had unlawfully suspended a sentence for an offence attracting a mandatory minimum, thereby misdirecting itself on sentencing law and underestimating the seriousness of attacks on police officers.
precedents_cited_list: SC Reference No. 03/2008 | SC Appeal No. 89A/2009 | SC Appeal No. 17/2013 | Regina v. Colin Dickson [2005] EWCA 1826 | R v. Zamagias [2002] NSWCCA 17 (as cited in O'Malley on Sentencing, 3rd Ed) | Dulla and Others v. The State AIR 1958 All 198 | Regina v. Darran Marshall [2015] EWCA Crim 474
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal treated the case as one of misdirection and misapplication of sentencing law and a manifestly inadequate sentence, warranting appellate intervention to substitute an appropriate custodial sentence in line with statutory minimums and sentencing principles.
sentencing_principles_applied: The court considered statutory minimum sentencing provisions, the bar on suspending imprisonment where a mandatory minimum applies, the need for general and specific deterrence and denunciation in cases of attacks on police officers, the impact on victims, prior convictions, first-offender status, discounts for early guilty pleas, lapse of time since the offence and the balancing of aggravating and mitigating factors when fixing custodial terms, fines and compensation.
final_charge_after_appeal: Same as the original convictions on counts 1 to 5 for unlawful assembly, attempted murder, causing hurt to deter police officers and robbery of an official firearm; only the sentences were enhanced.

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 308(A)(2) of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The appellant, who was the stepmother of the child victim, was alleged to have subjected the child to persistent physical and mental cruelty between about 2001 and 2002 while the child was living with her father and the appellant. The assaults with sticks, broomsticks and other objects and the imposition of excessive household chores caused the victim's schooling to stop and left approximately 36 external injuries on her body. Complaints were made to the police, and after the victim was eventually removed to a children's home, she revealed the full extent of the ill-treatment, leading to the indictment under Section 308(A)(2) of the Penal Code.
evidence_type_primary: Eyewitness testimony of the victim (child witness)
evidence_type_secondary: Medical evidence of injuries and corroborative testimony of the police officer and the victim's father
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 308(A)(2) of the Penal Code
hc_sentence_type: Rigorous Imprisonment 3 years plus fine and compensation
hc_judgment_summary: After trial in the High Court of Gampaha, the judge accepted the testimony of the victim, supported by the medical officer, the investigating police officer and the victim's father, and found that the appellant had wilfully ill-treated the child within the meaning of Section 308(A)(2). The appellant was convicted as charged and sentenced to three years rigorous imprisonment, a fine and a substantial compensation order in favour of the victim.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: On appeal, the appellant argued first that the adoption of evidence following transfers of High Court judges had not been properly done in terms of Section 48 of the Judicature Act. She further contended that the trial judge had failed to evaluate the evidence of all witnesses properly and had not given due consideration to the defence version.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Alleged improper adoption of evidence by successor High Court judges under Section 48 of the Judicature Act. | Alleged failure by the High Court judge to properly evaluate the evidence of all prosecution witnesses. | Alleged failure by the High Court judge to properly consider and give weight to the defence version.
gnd_other_description: Alleged failure by the High Court judge to evaluate all witnesses and to properly consider the defence version.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal carefully reviewed the victim's account of long-term ill-treatment, noting that despite extensive cross-examination over several years she remained consistent and explained earlier omissions as arising from fear of the appellant. It emphasised the corroboration provided by the police officer, the victim's father and the medical officer, and observed that defence suggestions such as scarring from chickenpox were not properly put to key witnesses, thereby diminishing their value.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal found the victim's consistent and corroborated testimony far more probable than the appellant's bare denial and defence suggestions, and held that no reasonable doubt was created as to the appellant's guilt.
defence_version_summary: The appellant gave evidence from the witness box, vehemently denying the charge of cruelty, and the defence called two witnesses to support her position, including a suggestion that the scars on the victim's body were due to chickenpox rather than assault. The Court of Appeal noted that these alternative explanations were not properly put to the medical officer or the victim's father and concluded that the defence evidence did not create a reasonable doubt in the prosecution case.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: In addressing the complaint about Section 48 of the Judicature Act, the Court of Appeal held that there is no statutory requirement for a formal adoption of evidence and that it was sufficient that successor judges continued the proceedings, leaving it open to the accused to demand that witnesses be re-summoned, which had not been done in this case. It then reviewed the victim's evidence in detail, found it to be credible and consistent despite lengthy cross-examination, and noted that her explanations for earlier incomplete statements due to fear were plausible and supported by the police officer's and father's testimony as well as the medical findings of 36 blunt force injuries. The court considered the defence evidence, including the appellant's denial and the two defence witnesses, but held that they failed to undermine the prosecution case or to explain away the injuries and long-term deprivation of schooling. Concluding that the High Court had correctly evaluated the evidence and applied Section 308(A)(2), the Court of Appeal found no basis to interfere with either conviction or sentence and dismissed the appeal.
coa_sentence_type: Rigorous Imprisonment 3 years plus fine and compensation (unchanged)
brief_judgment_file_summary: This Court of Appeal judgment concerns an appeal by a stepmother convicted in the High Court of Gampaha for cruelty to her stepdaughter under Section 308(A)(2) of the Penal Code for prolonged ill-treatment between about 2001 and 2002. The appellant challenged the manner in which evidence was adopted following judicial transfers and claimed that the trial judge failed to properly evaluate the witnesses and the defence version. After reviewing Section 48 of the Judicature Act and the sequence of successor judges, the Court of Appeal held that there was no procedural irregularity since the accused did not demand that witnesses be re-summoned, and that the High Court had correctly continued the proceedings. Analysing the victim's consistent testimony, corroborated by the police officer, the victim's father and medical evidence of 36 blunt force injuries, and noting the weakness of the defence explanations such as scarring from chickenpox, the court affirmed that the prosecution had proved the charge beyond reasonable doubt. The appeal was therefore dismissed and the original conviction and sentence of three years rigorous imprisonment, a fine and compensation to the victim were affirmed, with directions to secure the appellant's presence as she had been on bail pending appeal.
defence_counsel: Sumith Senanayake, P.C. with Damitha Wickrama Arachchi
cause_of_death_exact_text: Not applicable – case concerns cruelty to a child with multiple blunt force injuries; no death was alleged.
hc_analysis_summary: According to the Court of Appeal, the High Court judge accepted the child's account of repeated assaults and deprivation, found it corroborated by police and medical evidence and by the father's testimony, and rejected the appellant's denial and defence evidence as insufficient to raise a reasonable doubt. The High Court concluded that the elements of the offence of cruelty to a child under Section 308(A)(2) were proved beyond reasonable doubt and imposed a sentence of three years rigorous imprisonment together with a fine and compensation. The Court of Appeal endorsed this analysis and found no misappreciation of evidence or error warranting interference.
precedents_cited_list: Panchhi and Others v. The State of U.P and Others – [1998] 7 SCC 177 | Kumara De Silva and 2 Others v. Attorney General – [2010] 2 SLR 169
standard_of_review_applied: Whether there was any misapplication of Section 48 of the Judicature Act or failure to properly evaluate the evidence amounting to a misdirection on law or misappreciation of evidence that would justify appellate interference with the trial judge's findings.
final_charge_after_appeal: Cruelty to a child under Section 308(A)(2) of the Penal Code (unchanged)

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna J. | R. Gurusinghe J.
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
offence_category: Murder (later reduced on appeal to culpable homicide not amounting to murder)
location_of_offence: Near the deceased's house and Zarook's boutique in the area under the jurisdiction of the High Court of Kalutara
brief_facts_summary: The accused-appellant Aluthgedarage Ratnasiri alias Susil was indicted in the High Court of Kalutara for murdering Mohamed Izzadeen Mohamed Izzad on or about 7 January 1999 by stabbing him in the chest following a dispute arising from the deceased assaulting the accused's younger brother Saman for plucking coconuts. The prosecution relied mainly on the testimony of the deceased's brother (PW 2) and other neighbours, together with medical evidence from the Judicial Medical Officer. The defence contended that the deceased was the aggressor, that there was a sudden fight and that the fatal injury was caused in the course of the accused exercising his right of private defence after grave and sudden provocation. The High Court nevertheless convicted the accused of murder and imposed the death sentence, leading to this appeal.
evidence_type_secondary: Medical evidence and surrounding circumstantial evidence
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code (murder)
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Kalutara, after a non-jury trial in which eight prosecution witnesses and two defence witnesses testified, accepted the prosecution version that the accused intentionally stabbed the deceased in the chest following a confrontation about the assault on his brother. The learned trial judge rejected the defence of private defence and grave and sudden provocation, treated the inconsistencies in the prosecution evidence as immaterial, and found the accused guilty of murder under section 296 of the Penal Code. The accused was thereupon sentenced to death on 15 December 2015.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: On appeal, counsel for the accused-appellant challenged the credibility of the prosecution witnesses, highlighting numerous contradictions and omissions within and between their testimonies and prior police statements, including on where people were, how many stab blows were delivered, and what weapon was used. It was argued that PW 2 and other purported eyewitnesses could not properly remember events that had occurred many years earlier and that some of them admitted giving police statements based on hearsay. The defence also contended that the non-production of the alleged weapon, inconsistencies between lay witness evidence and medical findings, and the police officer's incomplete records rendered the prosecution case unsafe. Further, the trial judge was said to have erred in failing to properly consider grave and sudden provocation, the right of private defence and the possibility of a sudden fight, warranting a reduction of the offence from murder to culpable homicide.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Alleged numerous material contradictions and omissions within the testimony of PW 1, PW 2, PW 3, PW 4, PW 6 and PW 7 and between their evidence and police statements, undermining credibility. | Contended that key witnesses, particularly PW 2, conceded poor memory of the incident due to lapse of time and admitted that some prior statements were inaccurate or based on hearsay. | Argued that the prosecution failed to produce the alleged weapon, and that medical evidence about the nature and number of injuries and the likely weapon conflicted with lay witness accounts. | Submitted that the trial judge failed to appreciate the impact of these contradictions and omissions and misdirected himself by treating them as immaterial. | Contended that the trial court failed to properly consider the accused's right of private defence, grave and sudden provocation and sudden fight and thereby wrongly refused to reduce the charge to culpable homicide not amounting to murder. | Argued that delays and the long lapse of time between the incident and trial prejudiced the accuracy of witness recollection.
gnd_other_description: Failure to give due weight to grave and sudden provocation, private defence and sudden fight raised by the defence and supported by parts of the prosecution evidence.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal carefully examined the evidence of PW 1, PW 2, PW 3 and PW 4 and found numerous inter se contradictions and omissions on material points such as who was at the scene, whether the accused entered the house, how many stab blows were delivered, and where witnesses went before and after the incident. It observed that PW 2, the principal eyewitness, had significant inconsistencies between his police statement and testimony and repeatedly admitted poor memory of the events. PW 3 and PW 4 were also found to be unreliable, with PW 3 acknowledging that his police statement was not truthful and largely based on what he heard from others, leading the appellate court to treat the prosecution evidence as unsafe to sustain a conviction for murder.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal held that in light of the substantial contradictions, omissions and unreliability of key prosecution witnesses, it was unsafe to uphold a conviction for murder beyond reasonable doubt. It considered the accused's version of a sudden confrontation following the assault on his brother, coupled with grave and sudden provocation and an exercise of private defence, as reasonably probable. On that footing, the court concluded that the more appropriate finding was that the accused caused the death in a sudden fight with some excess in self-defence, amounting to culpable homicide not amounting to murder.
defence_version_summary: The accused-appellant testified on oath that his younger brother Saman, who had been beaten by the deceased over plucking coconuts, came to him with a bleeding ear and complained, and that later when they were passing the deceased's house the deceased approached aggressively with a knife. The accused claimed that he had no prior enmity with the deceased, that a struggle ensued when he tried to ward off the attack, and that the deceased sustained the fatal injury during this scuffle while the accused was exercising his right of private defence under grave and sudden provocation. His brother gave supporting evidence as a defence witness, and the Court of Appeal found that this version, though involving some excess, attracted the special exceptions of self-defence and sudden fight.
legal_errors_description: The Court of Appeal held that the trial judge misdirected himself and failed to properly evaluate material exculpatory factors, including grave and sudden provocation, the right of private defence and sudden fight, and the impact of numerous contradictions and omissions in the prosecution evidence. It found that the non-consideration of these extenuating circumstances and the treatment of the contradictions as immaterial resulted in a non-direction and miscarriage of justice, leading to an erroneous conviction for murder instead of culpable homicide.
procedural_defects_description: The appellate court identified a failure by the High Court to adequately consider and analyse the material contradictions and omissions in prosecution testimony and the deficiencies in the police evidence, as well as a failure to direct itself properly on the application of the special exceptions to murder relating to provocation, sudden fight and self-defence. This amounted to a non-direction on critical issues that prejudiced the accused in the conduct of his trial.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal undertook a fresh evaluation of the evidence, emphasising the cumulative effect of the many contradictions, omissions, lapses of memory and hearsay elements in the testimonies of PW 1, PW 2, PW 3, PW 4 and the police officer PW 7, along with the non-production of the alleged weapon and inconsistencies between eyewitness accounts and the medical findings. It concluded that the prosecution evidence was unreliable to prove an intentional murder beyond reasonable doubt, while the accused's account of a sudden fight after grave and sudden provocation and in the course of private defence was reasonably probable. Applying case law on the impact of material contradictions and the legal principles of self-defence, grave and sudden provocation and sudden fight, the court held that the trial judge had misdirected himself and recast the conviction from murder under section 296 to culpable homicide not amounting to murder under section 297, imposing a sentence of seven years' rigorous imprisonment instead of the death penalty.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This Court of Appeal judgment concerns an appeal from the High Court of Kalutara where the accused-appellant was convicted of murder under section 296 of the Penal Code and sentenced to death for fatally stabbing the deceased following a dispute about the deceased beating the accused's younger brother. The appellate court reviews the testimony of several prosecution witnesses, highlighting numerous material contradictions, omissions, admissions of poor memory and hearsay, as well as the non-production of the alleged weapon and inconsistencies between lay evidence and the medical findings of two stab injuries caused by a heavy sharp weapon. Considering these infirmities, the accused's version of a sudden fight after his brother had been assaulted, and the legal principles relating to grave and sudden provocation and private defence, the court finds that the trial judge misdirected himself by failing to evaluate exculpatory material and extenuating circumstances. It sets aside the conviction and sentence for murder, substitutes a conviction for culpable homicide not amounting to murder under section 297 of the Penal Code based on sudden fight and self-defence, and imposes a sentence of seven years' rigorous imprisonment with effect from 15 December 2015.
weapon_type: A long sharp and heavy cutting weapon such as a sword, longer than a normal knife
cause_of_death_exact_text: Haemorrhage due to severe bleeding caused by the stab injury to the left side of the chest was identified by the Judicial Medical Officer as the cause of death.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court accepted PW 2 and other prosecution witnesses as credible, disregarded the extensive contradictions and omissions highlighted by the defence and treated them as peripheral, and thus concluded that the accused had intentionally stabbed the deceased after confronting him about the assault on Saman. It rejected the accused's version that the deceased came at him with a knife and that the fatal injury occurred during a struggle in the course of private defence and grave and sudden provocation. The Court of Appeal criticised this approach as a misdirection and non-direction, holding that the trial judge failed to consider exculpatory evidence and special exceptions that should have reduced the offence to culpable homicide not amounting to murder.
precedents_cited_list: Thantiriee Plyasena vs. Bribery Commission CA/31/98 dated 29.01.2001 | Padmatileke (SGT) vs. Director General, Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery and Corruption SC/99/2007 | AG v Manjula Priyantha HA (PHC) APN 19/99 dated 26.09.2000 | Hewage Chandarapala vs. AG CA/131/2001 dated 05.08.2005 | Kanattha Gamage Wijetilaka and two others vs. AG CA/189/2000 dated 02.10.2009 | Chacko Mathai v State of Kerala AIR 1964 KER 222 | Russell on Crime and K.D. Gaur, The Text Book on the Indian Penal Code (discussion on right of private defence)
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal applied an appellate review for misdirection and non-direction in law and for failure to properly evaluate evidence, particularly material contradictions and omissions, and considered whether the trial court's findings were unsafe or perverse in light of the totality of the evidence.
sentencing_principles_applied: In fixing a sentence of seven years' rigorous imprisonment, the court took into account extenuating circumstances such as the absence of premeditation, the grave and sudden provocation given by the deceased's assault on the accused's brother, the sudden fight and the fact that the accused acted partly in private defence, thereby warranting a substantial reduction from the death penalty for murder.
final_charge_after_appeal: Culpable homicide not amounting to murder under section 297 of the Penal Code based on sudden fight and self-defence

judges: K. Priyantha Fernando J (P/CA) | Wickum A. Kaluarachchi J
offence_sections: Section 54A(d) and Section 54A(b) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance
brief_facts_summary: The accused-appellant, Daulkarage Dhanushika Lakmali, was charged in the High Court of Balapitiya with trafficking and possessing 4.90 grams of heroin. The prosecution case was that during a police raid at the appellant's house, heroin was recovered from her person in a pink coloured bag associated with her upper body clothing, and the productions were later sent to the Government Analyst who confirmed the substance as heroin. The defence position was that the police came to her house and that a parcel of heroin was found on top of a fan in the living room rather than on her person, implying it was not in her possession. The High Court rejected the defence version and convicted her on both counts.
evidence_type_primary: Police eyewitness testimony of the recovery of heroin from the accused-appellant
evidence_type_secondary: Government Analyst forensic report confirming the substance as heroin and related documentary evidence
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 54A(d) and Section 54A(b) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Balapitiya found that the prosecution had proved beyond reasonable doubt that the accused-appellant trafficked and possessed 4.90 grams of heroin. It accepted the police testimony as to the recovery of the drugs and the Government Analyst report as establishing the nature of the substance. The court rejected the defence version that the heroin was found on top of a fan and held that the heroin was in the appellant's possession, convicting her on both counts and sentencing her to life imprisonment.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: On appeal, the accused-appellant challenged the conviction and sentence primarily on evidentiary and evaluative grounds. She argued that the prosecution had failed to properly prove the chain of productions and that the trial judge ignored or failed to fairly assess inconsistencies and contradictions in the prosecution evidence. It was further contended that the High Court judgment contained wrong observations, that the judge failed to apply the test of probability particularly in relation to the alleged recovery from her brassiere, and that due consideration was not given to the accused's own evidence. One previously pleaded ground was expressly abandoned at the hearing of the appeal.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Alleged failure to prove the chain of productions, including not calling the production keeper of the Magistrate's Court. | Alleged failure by the trial judge to take into account inconsistencies and contradictions in prosecution evidence (children present, weather conditions, precise location on body where production was found, number of search rounds). | Complaint that judgment was entered on wrong observations, particularly about what was or was not suggested in cross-examination regarding the location of recovery (inside brassiere versus top of fan). | Allegation that the learned High Court Judge failed to apply the test of probability to the facts, such as the likelihood of the appellant wearing a brassiere shortly after waking. | Allegation that the trial judge failed to properly evaluate and give due consideration to the evidence of the accused.
gnd_other_description: Alleged failure by the trial judge to apply the test of probability and to properly evaluate and give due consideration to the accused-appellant's evidence.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal noted that several police witnesses, including PW1, PW4 and PW5, corroborated each other regarding the recovery of a pink coloured bag from the appellant's person and the subsequent handling of the productions. PW7, who searched the appellant, did not identify the production in court, but the court held that this caused no prejudice since she had not examined the contents and the other witnesses clearly identified the bag and its association with the appellant. PW14's unchallenged evidence established the movement of productions from the seizure to the Magistrate's Court and then to the Government Analyst, and the retired Government Analyst's testimony and report were effectively undisputed. The Court of Appeal concluded that the alleged inconsistencies highlighted by the defence were minor and did not undermine the credibility of the prosecution case, whereas the appellant's own version contained infirmities and did not create reasonable doubt.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal rejected the argument that it was improbable for the appellant, as a mother of an infant, to have been wearing a brassiere soon after waking and emphasised that the issue was what the credible evidence revealed rather than what is usual. It found no reason to disbelieve the mutually corroborative testimony of the prosecution witnesses and held that the alleged improbabilities and contradictions did not give rise to reasonable doubt.
defence_version_summary: The accused-appellant gave evidence asserting that although police officers came to her house, searched the premises and a parcel of heroin was found, it was discovered on top of a fan in the living room rather than on her person, suggesting it was not in her exclusive possession. Through cross-examination, her counsel also suggested a version inconsistent with recovery from inside her brassiere and attempted to cast doubt on the prosecution narrative. The High Court, as endorsed by the Court of Appeal, found that the appellant's version suffered from infirmities, that she had left room for subsequent changes when putting suggestions to prosecution witnesses, and that her evidence could not be believed. Consequently, her testimony did not generate any reasonable doubt about the prosecution case.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal carefully examined the alleged break in the chain of productions and concluded that PW14's unchallenged evidence, along with the memorandum from the Government Analyst, proved that the same productions taken from the appellant ultimately reached the analyst without room for tampering. It held that the failure to call the production keeper and the non-identification of the productions by PW7 did not prejudice the prosecution because other witnesses clearly identified the pink bag and its association with the appellant. The court considered the highlighted inconsistencies (such as weather conditions, presence of children, and precise body area of recovery) to be minor and immaterial, particularly given the appellant's own admission that the police came to her house and that a parcel of heroin was found on top of the fan. It also rejected complaints about wrong observations and probability, found that the High Court had properly evaluated and rejected the defence evidence, and ultimately affirmed that no reasonable doubt arose in the prosecution case.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This Court of Appeal judgment concerns an appeal by Daulkarage Dhanushika Lakmali against her conviction and life sentence from the High Court of Balapitiya for trafficking and possessing 4.90 grams of heroin under sections 54A(d) and 54A(b) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance. The appellant challenged the proof of the chain of productions, pointed to alleged inconsistencies in prosecution evidence, complained of wrong observations by the trial judge, argued that the test of probability was not applied, and claimed that her evidence had not been properly evaluated. The Court of Appeal held that the chain of custody was satisfactorily proved through PW14 and the Government Analyst documentation, that the alleged contradictions were minor and did not affect credibility, and that the Government Analyst evidence was effectively unchallenged. It concluded that the High Court had correctly rejected the defence version, that no legal or procedural error vitiated the trial, and accordingly affirmed the conviction and life sentence, dismissing the appeal.
prosecution_counsel: Janaka Bandara SSC for the Respondent
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court had reasoned that the heroin recovered during the police raid was in the possession of the appellant and accepted the testimony of several police officers, together with the Government Analyst report, as proving the charges beyond reasonable doubt. It rejected the defence version that the heroin was merely found on top of a fan and not on the appellant's person, finding that version unreliable and insufficient to create reasonable doubt. The Court of Appeal agreed with this reasoning and only noted, as unnecessary but harmless, an observation by the trial judge suggesting that even on the appellant's version there was exclusive possession.
precedents_cited_list: Witharana Doli Nona v The Republic of Sri Lanka – C.A. 19/99 | Perera v Attorney General – (1998) 1 Sri L.R. 378
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal examined whether the High Court had properly evaluated the evidence, in particular the proof of the chain of custody of the productions, the materiality of alleged contradictions, and the assessment of the defence version, and whether the findings were supported by the evidence rather than being perverse or unreasonable.
final_charge_after_appeal: Trafficking and possession of 4.90 grams of heroin under Section 54A(d) and Section 54A(b) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance

judges: Sampath B Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 364(2)(e) punishable under Section 364(2) Penal Code (as amended by Act No. 22 of 1995)
brief_facts_summary: The appellant, a neighbour, repeatedly raped a 12-year-old girl between September and December 2008 when she was called to his house to look after his young child while his wife was away. The victim did not disclose the incidents due to fear and the matter came to light only when she sought medical treatment for a genital rash. The appellant was convicted after trial on three counts of rape.
evidence_type_secondary: Medical evidence (genital rash leading to disclosure)
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 364(2)(e) Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the appellant on three counts of rape of a minor and sentenced him to 18 years rigorous imprisonment on each count to run concurrently, plus Rs. 250,000 compensation to the victim with a default term of 2 years imprisonment.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant initially appealed both conviction and sentence but later withdrew the appeal against conviction, seeking only to challenge the sentence on the basis that the trial judge failed to consider he was a first-time offender and other mitigating circumstances.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Appellant is a first-time offender | Request for lenient sentence considering mitigating circumstances
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The judgment does not re-analyse witness evidence in detail because the conviction appeal was withdrawn; the court accepted the High Court's findings based on the victim's testimony and medical evidence leading to disclosure.
defence_version_summary: No defence version discussed in the Court of Appeal judgment as the conviction appeal was withdrawn and no dock statement or defence evidence is mentioned.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal against sentence, finding 18 years RI appropriate given the gravity of repeated rape of a 12-year-old child, breach of trust, young age of victim, and aggravating factors from English and Sri Lankan precedents. The court noted the sentence was near the maximum of 20 years but justified, and concurrent terms plus no fine showed the trial judge had considered proportionality.
brief_judgment_file_summary: Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal against an 18-year rigorous imprisonment sentence (plus compensation) for three counts of raping a 12-year-old girl, holding the sentence appropriate despite the appellant being a first offender, citing gravity of offence, breach of trust, young victim, and precedents emphasising deterrence and public protection.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court correctly considered all relevant sentencing factors for multiple child rape counts and imposed 18 years RI concurrent with compensation; the Court of Appeal found no reason to interfere with this reasoned sentence.
precedents_cited_list: Attorney-General V. H.N de Silva 57 NLR 121 | M. Gomes W. V. D Leelaratne 66 NLR 233 | Roberts (1982) Vol 74 CAR 242 | Keith Billam (1986) Vol 82 CAR 347
standard_of_review_applied: Appropriateness of sentence in light of gravity, aggravating factors and precedents
sentencing_principles_applied: Gravity of offence, deterrence, public protection, breach of trust, young victim as aggravating factors; first-offender status subordinate where public interest prevails

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Poison, Opium and Dangerous Drugs (Amendment) Act No.13 of 1984, Section 54(a) and (b)
brief_facts_summary: The accused, Samaraweera Arachchige Hashan Rodrego, was arrested on 2018-04-25 for allegedly possessing 2.23 grams of heroin and was later indicted in the High Court of Panadura under the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs (Amendment) Act. The indictment was served in October 2020 and the case was fixed for trial on several dates, but the proceedings were repeatedly postponed due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the High Court judge being on leave, and failures by remand officials to produce the accused. Bail applications made in February and June 2021 were refused by the High Court on the basis that there were no exceptional circumstances under Section 54(a) and (b). After more than four years on remand without his trial being taken up, the petitioner invoked the revisionary jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal alleging that the prolonged detention violated his constitutional right to personal liberty.
evidence_type_secondary: Medical evidence (JMO report on injuries to the accused)
hc_judgment_summary: On 2021-06-22 the High Court of Panadura refused the accused's application for bail made by way of motion in the pending heroin case. The High Court appears to have taken the view that there were no exceptional circumstances to justify departure from the remand requirement under Section 54(a) and (b) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs (Amendment) Act.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: In the revision application, counsel for the petitioner complained that the accused had been in remand since 2018 without his trial being taken up despite the indictment having been served and several trial dates being fixed. He argued that the prolonged detention without adjudication, together with the fact that the accused had no previous convictions and had even complained to the Human Rights Commission in 2017 about an impending arrest, amounted to exceptional circumstances warranting bail. Counsel further relied on the JMO's report recording injuries on the accused and cited several authorities on delay, personal liberty and the concept of exceptional circumstances. The principal complaint was that the High Court erred in refusing bail in these circumstances.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Accused has been in remand since 2018 without the trial being taken up despite the indictment and several trial dates. | The prolonged and uncertain delay in adjudicating the charge violates the accused's constitutional right to personal liberty and should be treated as an exceptional circumstance for bail. | The accused has no previous convictions and had lodged a prior complaint with the Human Rights Commission about an impending arrest, suggesting harassment. | The JMO report notes injuries on the accused, supporting the contention of ill-treatment during or after arrest. | The High Court erred in its application of the 'exceptional circumstances' test under Section 54(a) and (b) in refusing bail.
gnd_other_description: Reliance on a prior complaint to the Human Rights Commission about an impending arrest and on the JMO report indicating injuries to show harassment and to support the plea for bail.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal did not undertake a detailed assessment of trial evidence because the case was still pending and no witnesses had been called. The only specific item of evidence mentioned was the JMO report, which recorded injuries on the accused and was used by defence counsel to support allegations of ill-treatment and to bolster the plea for bail. The Court's analysis instead focused on the statutory bail restrictions and constitutional protections against oppressive pre-trial detention rather than on the strength of the prosecution case.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal did not analyse probabilities of guilt but proceeded on the basis that, even assuming the charge stood, the prolonged delay and continued remand rendered further detention oppressive and unjustifiable in the circumstances.
defence_version_summary: At this interlocutory stage the defence did not present a detailed alternative narrative to the heroin charge but focused on the circumstances of the accused's detention. Counsel highlighted that the accused had no previous convictions, had made a prior complaint to the Human Rights Commission in 2017 about an impending arrest, and had sustained injuries noted in the JMO report, suggesting possible harassment or ill-treatment. No defence witnesses were called and no oral evidence was led because the trial had not commenced; the accused was represented through counsel on the bail revision. The Court of Appeal treated these matters, together with the lengthy remand without trial, as supporting the contention that exceptional circumstances existed to justify bail.
legal_errors_description: The Court of Appeal effectively held that the High Court erred in refusing bail by failing to recognize that the prolonged delay in adjudicating the charge, combined with the circumstances of the accused's detention, amounted to exceptional circumstances within the meaning of Section 54(a) and (b) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs (Amendment) Act. The High Court's approach was inconsistent with constitutional protections of personal liberty and the principles in prior authorities on oppressive delay and pre-trial detention.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal examined the statutory framework which permits bail for offences under Section 54(a) and (b) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs (Amendment) Act only on exceptional grounds and stressed that this regime cannot be used to keep accused persons in remand for indefinite periods in violation of their fundamental right to personal liberty. Relying on Carder v OIC Narcotics Bureau, Attorney General v Ediriweera and Sumanadasa v Attorney General, the Court underscored that excessive or oppressive delay in bringing an accused to trial can itself amount to an exceptional circumstance. It accepted that, although many adjournments were due to the COVID-19 pandemic and other factors beyond the control of the court and prosecution, the fact that the accused had remained on remand for about four years without his trial being taken up rendered continued detention unjustifiable. While the Court did not assess the merits of the prosecution evidence, it regarded the delay and the defence material as sufficient to revise the High Court order and direct that the accused be enlarged on bail subject to conditions.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This revision application arose from the High Court of Panadura's refusal to grant bail to an accused charged with possessing 2.23 grams of heroin under Section 54(a) and (b) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs (Amendment) Act No.13 of 1984. The Court of Appeal traced the procedural history, noting that the indictment was served in October 2020 but the trial dates were repeatedly postponed due to the COVID-19 pandemic, judicial unavailability and failures by remand officials to produce the accused, leaving him on remand from April 2018 onwards. After discussing the constitutional guarantee of personal liberty and authorities such as Carder v OIC Narcotics Bureau, Attorney General v Ediriweera and Sumanadasa v Attorney General, the Court held that the prolonged and uncertain delay in adjudicating the charge had become an exceptional circumstance justifying bail notwithstanding the statutory restrictions. The revision application was therefore allowed and the High Court was directed to enlarge the accused, Samaraweera Arachchige Hashan Rodrego, on suitable conditions of bail.
prosecution_counsel: Priyani Abeygunawardena, SC for the state.
defence_counsel: Geeth Karunarathna instructed by Thilini Karunarathna for the Petitioner.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court of Panadura had refused bail to the accused, applying the strict statutory test that bail may be granted for Section 54(a) and (b) offences only on exceptional grounds and finding none in this case. The Court of Appeal observed that, although many of the adjournments were attributable to the COVID-19 pandemic and other factors beyond the control of the court and prosecution, the cumulative effect was that the accused had remained on remand for about four years without his trial being taken up. It held that the High Court had failed to appreciate that this prolonged delay, in light of constitutional guarantees and jurisprudence on oppressive pre-trial detention, itself constituted an exceptional circumstance requiring the grant of bail.
precedents_cited_list: Carder v OIC Narcotics Bureau – 2006 SLR 74 | Attorney General v Ediriweera – S.C. Appeal No. 100/2005; 2006 B.L.R. 12 | Sumanadasa v Attorney General – 2006 Sri Lanka Law Reports 202
standard_of_review_applied: Failure to recognize and properly evaluate exceptional circumstances and oppressive delay in applying the statutory bail restriction, with emphasis on safeguarding constitutional rights against unlawful or excessive pre-trial detention.
final_charge_after_appeal: Possession of 2.23 grams of heroin under Section 54(a) and (b) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs (Amendment) Act No.13 of 1984

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J | Neil Iddawala J
offence_sections: Section 19(b) and Section 19(c) of the Bribery Act; Section 90 of the Bribery Act
brief_facts_summary: The virtual complainant, an instructor at the Palmyrah Development Board and a subordinate of the respondent, alleged that the respondent solicited a bribe by requiring her to hand over the portion of her monthly salary corresponding to days she did not work, in exchange for allowing her to mark attendance while she attended external degree lectures during office hours. Acting on her complaint, the Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery or Corruption organized a raid on 2008-06-26 during which she handed over 7500 to the respondent. The respondent maintained that this money was not a bribe but an installment towards repayment of a personal loan he had advanced to her. The High Court of Colombo acquitted the respondent of all bribery charges, and the Director General of CIABOC sought revision of that acquittal in the Court of Appeal.
evidence_type_secondary: Raid evidence and official witness testimony
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court held that the prosecution had failed to establish beyond reasonable doubt the purpose for which the 7500 was paid to the respondent. It found the virtual complainant to be evasive and dishonest, noting that her credibility was undermined by her collusion with the respondent to defraud the government and her later grievances following her demotion and salary deductions. The court concluded that bribery charges were not proved and acquitted the respondent, even observing that cheating charges might properly have lain against both the complainant and the respondent.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: In the revision application, the Director General of CIABOC contended that the High Court misdirected itself in law and fact by failing to properly consider the evidence relating to the purpose of the payment and the relevant provisions of the Bribery Act. The petitioner argued that the sum of 7500 constituted a gratification within the wide definition in Section 90, paid to ensure preparation of the complainant's salary and continuation of her employment, and thus fell squarely within Section 19(c). It was further urged that although certain counts were not proved, the learned High Court Judge had failed to recognize that at least the counts based on Section 19(c) were made out on the evidence. On this basis the petitioner sought revision of the acquittal as being the result of a misdirection on a question of law and a failure to adequately analyse the evidence.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Alleged that the High Court failed to appreciate that the payment of 7500 was made as a gratification to secure the preparation of the virtual complainant's salary and continuation of her employment. | Contended that the High Court did not properly apply Section 19(c) of the Bribery Act in light of the broad definition of 'gratification' in Section 90. | Asserted that, when the totality of the prosecution evidence is considered, the respondent's acceptance of the money amounted to a bribery offence, and that the judge failed to adequately analyse and evaluate that evidence. | Claimed that the acquittal in respect of counts framed under Section 19(c) amounted to a misdirection on a question of law warranting revision.
gnd_other_description: Alleged failure by the High Court to properly analyse and evaluate the prosecution evidence concerning the purpose of the payment.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The prosecution relied mainly on the testimony of the virtual complainant, a decoy police constable, the CIABOC investigating officer, and two officials from the Palmyrah Development Board. The Court of Appeal, aligning with the High Court, found the complainant's evidence to be evasive and internally inconsistent, particularly regarding her reasons for complying with the respondent's demands and the purpose of the payment, and noted that her credibility was further weakened by her role in defrauding the government and her grievance over demotion and salary reductions. Evidence from PW04 and the respondent supported the defence version that the money represented repayment of a personal loan, and the respondent's testimony was described as consistent and uncontradicted.
probability_assessment_by_court: Both the High Court and the Court of Appeal held that the prosecution had not established beyond reasonable doubt that the payment was a bribe rather than repayment of a loan, and therefore gave the respondent the benefit of the doubt. The Court of Appeal emphasized that the shaken credibility of the virtual complainant and the supporting evidence for the defence made it unsafe to disturb the acquittal.
defence_version_summary: The respondent's defence was that the 7500 received from the virtual complainant on the day of the raid was not a bribe but an installment towards repayment of a personal loan he had earlier advanced to her. This version was corroborated by PW04, who stated that the complainant handed over the money saying it was what she had taken from the respondent. The Court noted that the respondent's testimony was consistent, uncontradicted, and free from marked omissions, and treated his version as more credible than that of the complainant.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal examined whether the High Court had misdirected itself in law or failed to evaluate the evidence in relation to the alleged bribery. It found that the prosecution's attempt to establish the purpose of the payment as a gratification under Section 19(c) largely depended on a new explanation elicited only in re-examination, which the Court regarded as a weak attempt and inconsistent with the broader evidence. The Court gave weight to PW04's evidence and the respondent's consistent testimony that the money represented repayment of a personal loan, while emphasizing that the virtual complainant's credibility was seriously compromised by her involvement in defrauding the government, her demotion, and her retaliatory complaints. Concluding that the prosecution had failed to prove beyond reasonable doubt that the payment was a bribe, the Court held that the High Court's acquittal was sound and that there was no basis to exercise revisionary jurisdiction to interfere with it.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This revision application by the Director General of CIABOC sought to challenge a High Court judgment that had acquitted a public servant of four bribery counts under Sections 19(b) and 19(c) of the Bribery Act concerning a payment of 7500 allegedly taken from a subordinate employee. The prosecution claimed the money was a gratification to permit the employee to mark attendance and draw salary for days not worked, while the respondent maintained it was repayment of a personal loan, a version supported by an office assistant from the Palmyrah Development Board. Both the High Court and the Court of Appeal found the complainant's evidence evasive and inconsistent, noting her participation in defrauding the government, her demotion, and retaliatory complaints, and concluded that the purpose of the payment as a bribe was not proved beyond reasonable doubt. The Court of Appeal accordingly held that there was no misdirection of law or failure of evaluation warranting interference under its revisionary jurisdiction and dismissed the application, leaving the acquittal undisturbed.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court analysed the complainant's conduct and evidence, noting that she had participated in an arrangement that defrauded the government, had later been demoted with salary deductions, and appeared to lodge complaints as a retaliatory measure. It found that the prosecution had not satisfactorily proved the purpose of the payment and that the complainant's testimony was evasive and unreliable, while the respondent's version of loan repayment was consistent. The Court of Appeal endorsed this reasoning, agreeing that cheating rather than bribery might have been the appropriate charge and that the acquittal on the bribery counts was justified.
standard_of_review_applied: The Court exercised its revisionary jurisdiction over an acquittal, considering whether there had been a misdirection on law or a failure to properly evaluate the evidence, and concluded that no such error or perversity existed to justify interference.
final_charge_after_appeal: Bribery under Section 19(b) and Section 19(c) of the Bribery Act (acquittal affirmed)

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Sections 296, 297, 315 of the Penal Code
offence_category: Culpable homicide not amounting to murder and causing grievous hurt
brief_facts_summary: The accused was originally indicted in the High Court of Chilaw for murder and causing grievous hurt arising from a stabbing incident. The deceased and an injured witness went in search of the accused after learning he had assaulted the deceased's wife, and confronted him while he was holding his two young children. Despite the accused warning them not to approach, the deceased advanced towards him and the accused, armed with a knife, stabbed both men, causing a single fatal stab injury to the deceased.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 297 (culpable homicide not amounting to murder) and Section 315 of the Penal Code
hc_sentence_type: Imprisonment 12 years for count 1; sentence on count 2 unchanged
hc_judgment_summary: In the High Court the accused, having initially pleaded not guilty, later pleaded guilty mid-trial to a lesser charge of culpable homicide not amounting to murder under section 297 on the basis of a sudden fight. The High Court accepted this plea and convicted him on that basis together with the hurt charge, imposing a sentence of 12 years imprisonment on the first count.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: By way of revision the accused-petitioner did not challenge his conviction but argued that the sentence of 12 years imposed on the first count was excessive. He contended that the incident should be viewed as one where he acted with knowledge rather than with murderous intention, given that he was holding his children at the time. He also urged the Court to excuse the delay in filing the revision application, explaining financial hardship and the fact that his two children were in an orphanage. On these humanitarian and evidential grounds he sought a reduction of the custodial term.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Petitioner does not challenge the conviction but contends that the 12-year sentence on count 1 is excessive. | Incident should be treated as one committed with knowledge rather than intention, affecting the appropriate level of sentence. | Delay of about two years in filing the revision application should be excused due to financial hardships. | Petitioner's two children are in an orphanage, and humanitarian considerations should weigh in favour of sentence reduction. | Court should exercise its revisionary powers despite the availability and non-use of the right of appeal.
gnd_other_description: Humanitarian grounds based on the petitioner's financial difficulties and the fact that his two children were in an orphanage, urged to justify reduction of sentence and excusing delay.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal relied primarily on the testimony of the injured witness Sarath Kumara, who described how the deceased initiated the confrontation by going in search of the accused after the alleged assault on his wife. The Court emphasized that the accused was clutching one child and accompanied by another, warned the deceased not to approach, and delivered only a single stab blow which proved fatal. These features led the Court to view the incident as one where the accused acted with knowledge rather than with premeditated murderous intention.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court considered it improbable that the accused, while holding a child and having another beside him, would have stabbed with full murderous intention, and instead concluded that he acted with knowledge of the probable consequences.
defence_version_summary: The accused initially pleaded not guilty but later pleaded guilty in the High Court to culpable homicide not amounting to murder under section 297 and did not adduce a separate defence case in evidence. In the Court of Appeal his counsel argued that he lacked intention to kill and acted only with knowledge, stressing that he was with his two young children and warned the deceased not to advance. Counsel also highlighted his financial hardship and the plight of his children in an orphanage as mitigating circumstances. The Court accepted that his mental state was one of knowledge rather than intention but treated these factors as grounds for reducing sentence rather than disturbing the conviction.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal first considered whether the stringent requirements for exercising revisionary jurisdiction were satisfied, noting the delay and the availability of an appeal but deciding to intervene due to the humanitarian grounds advanced. It reviewed the evidence of the injured eyewitness and highlighted that the deceased and witness went in search of the accused, that the accused was holding his children and implored them not to approach, and that only a single fatal stab blow was delivered. On that basis the Court held that the case was better characterized as culpable homicide committed with knowledge under section 297 rather than on the footing of a sudden fight, and that the original 12-year term was excessive. It therefore reduced the sentence on the first count to six years rigorous imprisonment while leaving the rest of the sentence undisturbed and dismissed the revision subject to that variation.
coa_sentence_type: Rigorous Imprisonment 6 years on count 1; other sentences unchanged
brief_judgment_file_summary: This revision application from the High Court of Chilaw concerned a prisoner convicted on a guilty plea to culpable homicide not amounting to murder under section 297 and causing hurt under section 315, who received 12 years imprisonment on the first count. He did not dispute his conviction but argued that the sentence was excessive, asking the Court of Appeal to excuse the delay in seeking revision in light of his financial hardship and the fact that his children were in an orphanage. The Court examined the evidence of the injured eyewitness and found that the deceased had gone in search of the accused, that the accused was holding his children and warned the deceased not to come near, and that there was only a single fatal stab blow. Holding that the incident reflected knowledge rather than murderous intention, the Court reduced the sentence on the first count to six years rigorous imprisonment while otherwise affirming the High Court order.
cause_of_death_exact_text: Death resulted from a single stab injury which proved fatal.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court proceedings, as reflected in the Court of Appeal judgment, show that the accused was originally indicted for murder and grievous hurt but was ultimately convicted on his mid-trial guilty plea to culpable homicide not amounting to murder under section 297, on the footing of a sudden fight, together with the hurt charge. The High Court imposed a sentence of 12 years imprisonment on the first count. The Court of Appeal did not expressly fault the High Court's reasoning but reassessed the factual circumstances and mental element, concluding that a lesser custodial term was appropriate.
precedents_cited_list: CA/PHC/APN/21/2021 | Rustom v. Hapangama – 1978 2 SLR 225 | Rashid Ali v. Mohammed Ali – 1936 6 CLW
standard_of_review_applied: Revisionary jurisdiction is to be exercised only in the presence of exceptional or special circumstances and ordinarily not where an alternative remedy by way of appeal is available and unexplained delay exists.
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court calibrated sentence by assessing the offender's mental state as knowledge rather than intention under section 297 and by taking into account humanitarian and personal mitigating factors while maintaining proportionality to the gravity of the offence.
final_charge_after_appeal: Section 297 (culpable homicide not amounting to murder) and Section 315 of the Penal Code (unchanged).

judges: M. Sampath K. B. Wijeratne J. | Dr. Ruwan Fernando J.
brief_facts_summary: The appellant, Lanka Marine Services (Pvt) Ltd, is a company engaged in importing and supplying marine lubricants and fuel which filed its return for the year of assessment 2011/2012 claiming tax exemption and concessionary rates under the Inland Revenue Act No. 10 of 2006. The Assessor rejected the return, issued an assessment and the Commissioner General of Inland Revenue confirmed the assessment on appeal. The appellant then appealed to the Tax Appeals Commission, which also confirmed the assessment. A stated case was thereafter referred to the Court of Appeal on questions of law concerning time bars on the Commissioner General and Tax Appeals Commission and whether bunker fuel supplies constituted exports entitling the appellant to exemptions.
evidence_type_primary: Documentary evidence (tax returns, assessments, appeal papers, correspondence, statutory materials)
evidence_type_secondary: Legal submissions and statutory interpretation arguments
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the Tax Appeals Commission determination was time barred under section 10 of the Tax Appeals Commission Act and that, in any event, the Commissioner General of Inland Revenue had made a time-barred determination under section 165(14) of the Inland Revenue Act. It further contended that the Tax Appeals Commission erred in law in holding that bunker fuel supplied to non-resident ships out of bonded warehouses did not constitute exports within section 13(b)(iii). In the alternative, the appellant claimed entitlement to the concessionary tax rate under section 52 on the basis that the income was qualified export profits and income. It also raised a general ground that, having regard to all the facts and circumstances, the Tax Appeals Commission erred in law in coming to its conclusions.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Allegation that the Tax Appeals Commission determination was time barred under section 10 of the Tax Appeals Commission Act | Allegation that the Commissioner General of Inland Revenue determination was time barred under section 165(14) of the Inland Revenue Act | Challenge to the Tax Appeals Commission finding that bunker fuel supplies were not exports under section 13(b)(iii) of the Inland Revenue Act | Alternative claim that the appellant was entitled to the concessionary tax rate under section 52 of the Inland Revenue Act as qualified export profits and income | General assertion that in all the facts and circumstances the Tax Appeals Commission erred in law in reaching its conclusions
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The case was determined almost entirely on documentary material and legal argument rather than oral testimony, with the Court scrutinising the statutory framework governing time limits and tax exemptions. The Court emphasised that the taxpayer claiming an export-based exemption bore the burden of producing supporting documents, such as CUSDEC forms, marine delivery notes and invoices showing a foreign destination, and noted that such documents were not produced. It also examined the legality of the assessor acknowledging the appeal on behalf of the Commissioner General, applying public law principles on delegation, and concluded that this administrative step was valid.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court held that a person invoking a tax exemption must clearly establish eligibility and that in cases of doubt or ambiguity the benefit goes to the revenue rather than the taxpayer, so the appellant could not succeed without proving that its bunker fuel supplies were exports. It found that the appellant failed to discharge this burden because it did not adduce documentary proof that the fuel was supplied to ships leaving Sri Lankan territory for foreign destinations.
defence_version_summary: The appellant maintained that it was an exporter of petroleum products because fuel was supplied from bonded warehouses to non-resident ships, and that such supplies were treated as re-exports under customs procedure codes and conducted in foreign currency. It argued that the Tax Appeals Commission was functus officio after exceeding 270 days, that the Commissioner General was time barred under section 165(14), and that it qualified either for full exemption under section 13(b)(iii) or a concessionary rate under section 52 as qualified export profits and income. The Court of Appeal rejected these contentions, finding the time limits to be directory, the acknowledgement by the assessor valid, and the documentary record insufficient to prove that the transactions were exports in the sense required by the Inland Revenue Act.
