procedural_defects_description: The Court acknowledged that the Tax Appeals Commission had in fact exceeded the 270 day statutory time frame in section 10 of the Tax Appeals Commission Act and that the Commissioner General issued his determination close to the two year limit, but held that these time limits were directory rather than mandatory and that an acknowledgement of appeal signed by an assessor acting under the Commissioner General was valid, so no defect warranted setting aside the assessment.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: On the first question, the Court held that the 270 day limit in section 10 of the Tax Appeals Commission Act is directory, not mandatory, based on legislative history, the absence of an express sanction, and principles against defeating substantive rights due to delays by adjudicative bodies. On the second question, it found that an assessor could validly acknowledge an appeal on behalf of the Commissioner General under the Carltona principle and relevant provisions of the Inland Revenue Act, and that the Commissioner General's determination was made within the effective two year period. On the export issue, the Court analysed definitions of 'export' in case law and statutes and concluded that, for bunker fuel, the taxpayer must prove supply to ships leaving Sri Lankan territory for a foreign destination, which the appellant failed to do due to lack of documentary proof, so it was not entitled to exemption under section 13(b)(iii) or the concessionary rate under section 52. Although the Court disagreed with some reasoning of the Tax Appeals Commission, it ultimately affirmed the assessment and dismissed the appeal.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This was a stated case appeal by Lanka Marine Services (Pvt) Ltd against a tax assessment for 2011/2012, raising questions of law on whether the Tax Appeals Commission and the Commissioner General of Inland Revenue were time barred and whether bunker fuel supplied from bonded warehouses to non-resident ships qualified as exports. The Court of Appeal held that the 270 day time limit in section 10 of the Tax Appeals Commission Act is directory and that delays beyond this period do not nullify determinations, and further held that appeals can be validly acknowledged by an assessor acting under the Commissioner General. On the merits, it ruled that to claim export-based exemptions under sections 13(b)(iii) and 52 of the Inland Revenue Act, the taxpayer must prove that bunker fuel is supplied to vessels leaving Sri Lankan territory for foreign destinations, supported by documents such as CUSDECs, marine delivery notes and invoices, which the appellant had not produced. Accordingly, the Court affirmed the Tax Appeals Commission determination and the assessment, answering all questions of law in the negative and dismissing the appeal.
prosecution_counsel: Farzana Jameel, SASG, P.C. with Nirmalan Wigneswaran, DSG, for the Respondent
defence_counsel: Dr. Shivaji Felix with Nivantha Satharasinghe for the Appellant
appeal_type: Other - Stated case on questions of law in a tax appeal
hc_analysis_summary: There was no High Court judgment; instead the Commissioner General of Inland Revenue first confirmed the assessment and the Tax Appeals Commission then dismissed the appellant's appeal, holding that the determinations were not time barred and that bunker fuel supplies were not exports qualifying for concessions. The Court of Appeal generally agreed with the outcome, though it refined the legal tests on time limits and the notion of export while ultimately affirming the Tax Appeals Commission determination and the assessment.
precedents_cited_list: Amadeus Lanka (Private) Limited v. Commissioner General of Inland Revenue | D.M.S. Fernando and Another v. Mohideen Ismail | K. Nagalingam v. Lakshman de Mel | Ramalingam v. Thangarajah | Mohideen v. Commissioner General of Inland Revenue | Walker Sons & Co. (U.K.) Ltd. v. Gunathilake and Others | Bandahamy v. Senanayake | Kegalle Plantations PLC v. Commissioner General of Inland Revenue | Stafford Motor Company (Private) Limited v. Commissioner General of Inland Revenue | CIC Agri Businesses (Private) Limited v. Commissioner General of Inland Revenue | Ramanathan Chettiar v. Wickramarachchi and Others | Wickremaratne v. Samarawickrema and Others | Perera and Silva Ltd v. Commissioner General of Inland Revenue | Burmah Shell Oil Storage & Distributing Co. Ltd v. The Commercial Tax Officer and others | Government of Kerala v. Mother Superior Adoration Convent | Novopan India Ltd v. Collector of Central Excise and Customs | Commissioner of Central Excise v. Hari Chand Shri Gopal | Bank of Commerce v. Tennessee | Carltona Ltd v. Commissioner of Works | Kuruppu v. Keerthir Rajapakse, Conservator of Forests | M.S. Perera v. Forest Department and another | Lanka Ashok Leyland PLC v. Commissioner General of Inland Revenue | Polycrome Electrical Industries (Pvt) Ltd v. Commissioner General of Inland Revenue | Nanayakkara v. University of Peradeniya | Sidhartha Sarawagi v. Board of Trustees for the Port of Kolkata and others | Director of Public Prosecutions v. Haw | R (Chief Constable of West Midlands Police) v. Birmingham Justices | Gajraj Singh v. State Transport Appellate Tribunal
standard_of_review_applied: Review confined to questions of law, including interpretation of statutory time limits in tax appeal procedures, the legality of administrative delegation and acknowledgement of appeals, and the proper construction of export-based exemption provisions under the Inland Revenue Act, without re-evaluating primary factual findings except to assess whether the appellant had legally sufficient proof.

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna, J. | R. Gurusinghe, J.
offence_sections: Section 54 A of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance
brief_facts_summary: The accused was arrested during an undercover police raid after a tip-off, allegedly clenching a bag of heroin in his fist while standing under a lamp post. Following the arrest, he allegedly led police to a nearby house belonging to a third party (Asthambi), where more heroin and money were found under a cupboard. The accused was charged with possession and trafficking of both quantities of heroin.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 54 A of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court found the appellant guilty on all four charges involving possession and trafficking of heroin. He was sentenced to life imprisonment for the first two counts and seven years rigorous imprisonment for the third and fourth counts, to run concurrently.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the evidence of the main police witness (PW1) was not credible and contained improbabilities. Further grounds included the failure to prove exclusive possession of drugs found in a house belonging to another person and that the failure to call the informant denied a fair trial.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Evidence of PW1 is not credible enough to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt. | The learned Trial Judge failed to give due consideration to the improbabilities of the evidence of PW1. | The prosecution failed to prove exclusive possession of articles detected in a house belonging to another person. | The failure of the prosecution to call the officer who received the information denied a fair trial.
gnd_other_description: Judgment written by a judge who did not hear the evidence.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found the police evidence (PW1) highly improbable, noting unnatural behavior by the accused (not seeing police, clenching drugs) and the police (entering a house, leaving doors open, not contacting the owner). The Court emphasized that the trial judge who wrote the judgment did not observe the witnesses' demeanor, weakening the credibility findings.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court assessed the probability of the events described by PW1 as very low, citing the accused's lack of vigilance despite being a distinct 'drug seller' and the police's casual manner in searching a third party's house.
defence_version_summary: The accused pleaded not guilty and made a dock statement. The defence contended that the police evidence was fabricated and improbable, and that the drugs found in the third party's house could not be attributed to the accused's exclusive possession.
legal_errors_description: The Court identified errors in classifying possession as trafficking without evidence of commercial intent. It also noted the error of attributing possession of items in a shared house to the accused without proof of control.
procedural_defects_description: The judgment was written and delivered by a judge who did not hear the evidence or observe the witnesses.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court allowed the appeal on all grounds. It acquitted the appellant of trafficking due to lack of evidence of commercial purpose. It acquitted on possession regarding the house search because exclusive possession wasn't proved. Finally, it acquitted on the personal possession count because the police testimony was inherently improbable and the convicting judge had not seen the witnesses.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The Court of Appeal set aside the High Court's conviction for heroin possession and trafficking. The Court found the police testimony regarding the arrest improbable and ruled that possession of drugs in a third party's house was not exclusively linked to the accused. The fact that the judgment writer did not hear the evidence was a key factor in overturning the credibility findings.
hc_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal noted that the High Court Judge failed to consider the improbabilities in the police evidence and erred in law by assuming trafficking without specific proof. The High Court also incorrectly imputed possession of items found in a house accessible to others.
precedents_cited_list: State vs Devunderage Nihal SC Appeal 154/2 2010 | King vs Chalo Singho 42 NLR 269 | Walimunige John vs State 76 NLR 488 | Mohomed Iqbal Mohomed Saddath vs Honorable Attorney General SC appeal 110/15 | Martin Fernando v Inspector of Police, Minuwangoda 46 NLR 210 | Munasinghe v Vidanage 69 NLR 97 | Watt or Thomas v. Thomas (1947 A. C. 484)
standard_of_review_applied: Review of evidence credibility and probability, particularly where the trial judge did not have the advantage of observing witnesses.

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna, J. | R. Gurusinghe, J.
offence_sections: Section 54 A of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance, as amended by Act No 13 of 1984
brief_facts_summary: Following a tip-off, PNB officers raided a location in Orugodawatte and arrested the accused, who was allegedly found with 4.378 grams of heroin in his fist. Based on the accused's statement, police searched a nearby house belonging to one Asthambi, finding another 1.77 grams of heroin and Rs. 318,520 under a cupboard. The house was open and unoccupied at the time of the search.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 54 A of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance, as amended by Act No 13 of 1984
hc_judgment_summary: The Trial Judge convicted the accused on all four counts (possession and trafficking of two separate quantities of heroin). The court imposed life imprisonment for the first two counts and seven years of rigorous imprisonment for the third and fourth counts to run concurrently.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the evidence of the main police witness (PW1) was not credible and contained inherent improbabilities. The defence also contended that the prosecution failed to prove exclusive possession of the drugs found in a third party's house and that the failure to call the informant prejudiced the defence.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The evidence of PW1 is not credible enough to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt. | The learned Trial Judge has failed to give due consideration to the improbabilities of the evidence of PW1. | The prosecution has failed to prove the exclusive possession of the articles detected, according to the statement of the appellant, from a house that belonged to some other person. | The failure of the prosecution to call the officer who had received the information or other witnesses to affirm the position of the prosecution, has curtailed the ability of the defense to attack the veracity of the witnesses and thereby denied a fair trial.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found the evidence of PW1 improbable, citing unnatural behaviors such as the accused clenching drugs in his fist for a long time and not noticing police approaching over 40 meters. The court also criticized the police for entering a third party's house without the owner's presence or recording a statement, labeling it as trespassing.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court found reasonable doubt due to the improbabilities in the police version and the fact that the judgment writer did not observe the witnesses.
defence_version_summary: The accused pleaded not guilty and made a dock statement. The defence argued that the police version was fabricated and unnatural, and that the drugs found in the house could not be attributed to the accused as it was not in his exclusive possession.
legal_errors_description: The High Court erred in convicting for trafficking without evidence of commercial intent and for possession in a house where exclusive possession was not established.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal held that trafficking requires evidence of commercial purpose beyond mere possession, which was absent. Regarding the drugs found in Asthambi's house, the court ruled that exclusive possession was not proved as the house was accessible to others. For the drugs in the accused's hand, the court found the police testimony inherently improbable and unreliable, especially given that the trial judge did not hear the evidence personally. Consequently, the conviction was unsafe.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused appealed a High Court conviction for possession and trafficking of heroin. The Court of Appeal acquitted the accused on all charges, finding that the prosecution failed to prove the elements of trafficking and exclusive possession for items found in a third party's house. Furthermore, the court deemed the police testimony regarding the arrest and recovery inherently improbable and unreliable.
hc_analysis_summary: The COA criticized the HC for failing to appreciate the improbabilities in PW1's evidence and for convicting on trafficking without sufficient proof of commercial intent. The COA noted the HC judge relied on the written record without observing witness demeanor.
precedents_cited_list: State vs Devunderage Nihal SC Appeal 154/2 2010 | King vs Chalo Singho 42 NLR 269 | Walimunige John vs State 76 NLR 488 | Mohomed Iqbal Mohomed Saddath vs Honorable Attorney General SC appeal 110/15 | Martin Fernando v Inspector of Police, Minuwangoda 46 NLR 210 | Munasinghe v Vidanage 69 NLR 97 | Watt or Thomas v. Thomas (1947 A. C. 484)
standard_of_review_applied: Appellate review of facts and law, specifically testing evidence extrinsically and intrinsically where the trial judge did not observe witnesses.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Act No. 13 of 1984
brief_facts_summary: The accused was arrested on 19.07.2019 for possession and trafficking of 8.5 grams of Heroin. An indictment was forwarded on 28.04.2021, and the trial was scheduled for 08.04.2022. The petitioner filed a revision application seeking bail on the ground of delay in filing the indictment, arguing it constituted an exceptional circumstance.
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court made an order dated 27.08.2021 regarding the remand of the accused, which the petitioner sought to set aside. The High Court effectively refused bail, maintaining the accused in remand pending trial.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner's main grievance is the delay in filing the indictment even after receiving the government analyst report. The petitioner argues this delay constitutes an exceptional circumstance warranting bail.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Delay of filing the indictment even upon receipt of the government analyst report
gnd_other_description: Exceptional circumstances for bail under the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Act
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The court did not analyze witness testimony as this was a revision application concerning bail and delay, not a trial appeal. The focus was on the procedural timeline and the definition of exceptional circumstances.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court assessed whether the delay was 'excessive or oppressive' and found it was not, given the COVID pandemic and the fact that the trial was fixed for the near future.
defence_version_summary: The defence argued that the delay of 1 year and 9 months in filing the indictment was excessive. They posited this delay as an exceptional circumstance to justify releasing the accused on bail.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal analyzed the alleged delay of 1 year and 9 months in filing the indictment. The court noted that delay is relative and must be excessive or oppressive to warrant relief. Considering the COVID pandemic and the fact that the trial was already fixed for 08.04.2022, the court found no oppressive delay. Consequently, no exceptional circumstances were found to grant bail.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The petitioner, accused of possessing and trafficking 8.5g of Heroin, sought revision of a High Court order refusing bail. The petitioner argued that the delay of 1 year and 9 months in filing the indictment constituted exceptional circumstances. The Court of Appeal dismissed the application, holding that the delay was not oppressive given the pandemic and the imminent trial date.
prosecution_counsel: Chathuranga Bandara, SC for the State
hc_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found no reason to interfere with the High Court's process, noting that the administration of justice had started and the trial was scheduled.
precedents_cited_list: Cader v OIC narcotics Bureau 2006 3 SLR 74 | Abdul Hamidkari Path and etc v State of Gujarat and other 15 476 | Attorney General v Ediriweera (2006) BLR pg. 12
standard_of_review_applied: The court applied the test of 'exceptional circumstances' required for bail under the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Act.

judges: K. Priyantha Fernando, J. | Wickum A. Kaluarachchi, J.
offence_sections: Section 365B 2(b) of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The appellant, who was 16 years old at the time, was indicted for grave sexual abuse of a 10-year-old mentally subnormal schoolmate. He was acquitted on a charge of oral/anal sex but convicted for squeezing the victim's penis. The medical evidence showed no physical injuries to the victim.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 365B 2(b) of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court acquitted the appellant on Count 1 but convicted him on Count 2. The judge imposed a 7-year custodial sentence, citing the need to deter bullies in schools and protect vulnerable children, considering the victim's tender age.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant withdrew the appeal against conviction and only challenged the sentence. The main argument was that the High Court failed to consider the appellant was a juvenile (16 years old) at the time and a first offender, making the 7-year sentence disproportionate.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Appellant was 16 years old (juvenile) at the time of the offence | Appellant is a first offender | Sentence of 7 years is not proportionate to the sexual act (squeezing penis) | High Court Judge failed to consider the appellant's age
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The court relied on the medical officer's testimony which stated no injuries were detected on the victim's penis, contradicting the mother's claim of redness. This medical evidence was used to mitigate the gravity of the offence for sentencing purposes.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court accepted the conviction based on trial evidence but re-evaluated the sentence based on the lack of physical injury and juvenile status.
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty at trial. In appeal, the defence accepted the conviction but argued for a reduced sentence based on the appellant's youth (16 years) and lack of prior convictions.
legal_errors_description: The High Court Judge failed to consider that the appellant was a juvenile (16 years old) at the time of the offence when imposing the mandatory minimum sentence.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal held that sentencing for juveniles should focus on rehabilitation. Citing SC Reference 3/2008, the court noted it has discretion to deviate from mandatory minimum sentences. Considering the appellant was 16, a first offender, and the act caused no physical injury, the 7-year sentence was deemed wrong in principle and excessive. It was reduced to 18 months.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The appellant appealed against a 7-year sentence for grave sexual abuse (squeezing the penis) of a 10-year-old schoolmate. The appellant was 16 at the time of the offence. The Court of Appeal found the High Court failed to consider the appellant's juvenile status and the lack of physical injury. The sentence was reduced to 18 months rigorous imprisonment, maintaining the fine and compensation.
defence_counsel: Amila Palliyage with R. Doralagoda and S. Udugampola
hc_analysis_summary: The COA found the HC Judge rightly considered aggravating factors but erred by failing to mention or consider the appellant was a juvenile. This omission made the sentence wrong in principle.
precedents_cited_list: Archbold 2019 Sentencing Guidelines | SC Reference 3/2008 | Rohana Alias Loku v Hon. Attorney-General 2011 2 Sri.L.R 174
standard_of_review_applied: Review of sentencing discretion regarding juvenile offenders
sentencing_principles_applied: Rehabilitation of juveniles; Discretion to deviate from mandatory minimums; Proportionality of sentence to gravity of act.

judges: Sampath B Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The deceased, a hotel caretaker, was found injured in the hotel garden and later succumbed to injuries. Prosecution alleged the accused, a three-wheeler driver, assaulted the deceased. Evidence relied on a witness seeing the accused's rented vehicle leaving the scene and a Section 27 recovery of a stool.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court found the accused guilty based on circumstantial evidence, concluding he was the only person who could have committed the crime. The court relied on the vehicle's presence and the recovery of a stool.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The items of circumstantial evidence relied on by the prosecution were highly insufficient to prove the case beyond a reasonable doubt. Therefore, the conviction was bad in law.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Circumstantial evidence insufficient to prove case beyond reasonable doubt | Conviction bad in law
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found the circumstantial evidence insufficient to form an irresistible inference of guilt, noting other suspects were initially involved. The recovery of the stool (P-03) was deemed 'clouded with doubt' as the initial investigator missed it, but it was found 10 days later by another officer.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court held that circumstantial evidence must be consistent with guilt and inconsistent with innocence; here, alternative possibilities existed.
defence_version_summary: The accused admitted driving the vehicle but claimed it was damaged in a brawl at the hotel when he went for a hire. He did not admit to assaulting the deceased.
legal_errors_description: The High Court Judge erred in concluding the accused was the only possible perpetrator despite evidence of a brawl and other initial suspects.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court held that the circumstantial evidence (vehicle seen leaving, appellant's admission of being there) raised suspicion but did not prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt, especially given the possibility of others' involvement. The recovery of the stool was considered unreliable due to the delay and failure of the first investigator to find it. Suspicion alone does not equal proof.
brief_judgment_file_summary: Accused was convicted of murder by the High Court based on circumstantial evidence involving his presence at the scene in a three-wheeler. The Court of Appeal set aside the conviction, ruling that the evidence did not irresistibly point to the accused's guilt alone, as others were involved in a brawl. The court also rejected the recovery of a weapon as doubtful.
defence_counsel: Palitha Fernando, P.C. with Neranjan Jayasinghe, Harshana Ananda and Dulshan Katugampola
hc_analysis_summary: The COA found the HC Judge factually wrong in inferring only the accused could have caused the injuries. The HC failed to consider the involvement of others and relied on doubtful recovery evidence.
precedents_cited_list: The King Vs. Abeywickrama 44 NLR 254 | Don Sunny Vs. The Attorney General (1998) 2 SLR 01 | Regina Vs. Exall (176 English Reports, Nisi Prius at page 853) | The King Vs. Gunaratne 47 NLR 145 | Pulukuri Kottaya Vs. Emperor A.I.R. (1947) P.C. 67 | The Queen Vs. M.G.Sumanasena 66 NLR 350
standard_of_review_applied: Review of whether circumstantial evidence proved guilt beyond reasonable doubt.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs (Amendment) Act No. 13 of 1984
brief_facts_summary: The 2nd accused-petitioner was arrested on 25/11/2017 for possession and trafficking of 93.43g of heroin. Despite the Government Analyst report being received in late 2017 or early 2018 and the indictment being served on 25/08/2020, the trial had not commenced by 2022. The accused remained in remand custody for nearly five years.
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court made an order on 05/07/2021 refusing bail and keeping the accused in remand pending trial. The trial had not been fixed despite the case being called numerous times.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner argued that she has been in remand since 2017 without trial commencement, which constitutes excessive and oppressive delay. This delay is presented as an 'exceptional circumstance' under the Act to warrant bail.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Accused in remand since 25/11/2017 without trial commencement | Indictment served on 25/08/2020 but case called over 12 times without fixing trial | Excessive delay constitutes exceptional grounds for bail
gnd_other_description: Revision application for bail based on exceptional circumstances due to delay
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: No witness evidence analysis was conducted as the trial has not commenced. The court focused solely on the procedural history and delay.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court did not assess guilt but focused on the delay in the administration of justice and the presumption of innocence.
defence_version_summary: The defence contended that the prolonged remand without trial (approx. 5 years) violated the accused's rights and constituted exceptional circumstances justifying bail.
legal_errors_description: The Court of Appeal found that keeping the accused in remand for an uncertain lengthy period without trial violates basic rights and is a mockery of justice.
procedural_defects_description: Excessive delay in fixing trial after indictment.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court observed that the accused had been in remand for nearly five years (since 2017) with the indictment served in 2020, yet no trial date was fixed. Citing 'Attorney General v Ediriweera', the Court held that such excessive delay is oppressive. The Court determined that this delay constitutes an 'exceptional circumstance' under Section 83 of the Dangerous Drugs Act, warranting the accused's release on bail despite the seriousness of the charge.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The 2nd accused filed a revision application against a High Court order refusing bail. She was indicted for possession and trafficking of heroin and had been in remand since November 2017 without trial commencement. The Court of Appeal found the delay of nearly five years to be excessive and oppressive, constituting exceptional circumstances. The application was allowed, and the High Court was directed to release the accused on bail.
prosecution_counsel: Sudharshana de Silva, DSG with Kanishka Rajakaruna, SC
hc_analysis_summary: The COA criticized the High Court process where the case was called numerous times without being fixed for trial, calling it a mockery of justice given the 5-year remand period.
precedents_cited_list: Carder vs. OIC Narcotics Bureau 2006 3 SLR 74 | Abdul Hamidkari Path and etc vs the State of Gujarat and other 15 476 | Anuruddha Ratwatte and others v The Attorney general (2003) 2 Sri L.R. 39 | Attorney General v Ediriweera [S.C. Appeal No.100/2005] (2006 B.L.R 12)
standard_of_review_applied: Revision jurisdiction under Article 138 of the Constitution regarding exceptional circumstances for bail.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Section 365 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The accused was indicted in the High Court under Section 365 of the Penal Code. He pleaded guilty to the charge and was convicted and sentenced to ten years rigorous imprisonment, a fine, and compensation. A revision application was filed two years later seeking a reduction of the sentence.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 365 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the accused based on his plea of guilty. The court sentenced him to ten years rigorous imprisonment, along with a fine and compensation.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner sought to exercise the revisionary powers of the Court to reduce the sentence from ten years to seven years. The application did not explain the failure to exercise the statutory right of appeal or the delay of two years in filing the revision application.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Reduction of sentence from 10 years to 7 years
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The court did not analyze witness evidence as the conviction was based on a guilty plea. The judgment focuses solely on the procedural requirements for a revision application.
defence_version_summary: The accused pleaded guilty at the High Court trial. In the revision application, he sought only a reduction of his sentence from ten years to seven years.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal emphasized that revision is a discretionary and exceptional remedy. The court found that the petitioner failed to explain why the statutory right of appeal was not exercised and failed to explain the fatal two-year delay in filing the application. Since no exceptional illegality in the sentence was shown and no explanation for the delay was provided, the court refused to intervene.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused pleaded guilty to Section 365 of the Penal Code and was sentenced to ten years rigorous imprisonment. A revision application was filed two years later seeking to reduce the sentence to seven years. The Court of Appeal dismissed the application, citing the unexplained failure to appeal and the fatal delay in filing for revision, noting no exceptional circumstances warranted intervention.
hc_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal did not identify any errors in the High Court's judgment, noting that the petitioner failed to adduce any exceptional illegality in the sentence or conviction.
precedents_cited_list: Ameen vs. rasheed 3CLW 8 | Rustom v Hapangama (1978 SLR Vol.2 PAGE No.225) | Rasheed Ali vs. Mohamed Ali (1936 6 CLW) | CA/PHC/APN 78/2021 | Ilangakoon v OIC Eppawala Police Station 2007 1SLR Page 398
standard_of_review_applied: Discretionary revisionary powers only in exceptional circumstances

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 364(3) of the Penal Code as amended
brief_facts_summary: The Accused-Appellant was indicted for committing incest/rape on his biological daughter, who was under 16 years of age at the time. The victim subsequently gave birth to a child. Although the victim became hostile during the trial, the prosecution relied on DNA evidence confirming the Appellant as the biological father of the child, supported by medical evidence regarding the victim's age and childbirth.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 364(3) of the Penal Code as amended
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the Appellant on the first count of the indictment based on the DNA evidence proving paternity and medical evidence, despite the victim's hostile testimony. The Appellant was acquitted on the 2nd and 3rd charges.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Appellant challenged the court's jurisdiction, arguing the lack of the Attorney General's sanction required for incest charges. He further argued that the case rested solely on DNA expert evidence which is not conclusive, and that the victim's age and date of birth were not properly proved.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Patent lack of jurisdiction of the court. | That the whole case for the prosecution is based on DNA evidence which is at best can only be considered as mere expert evidence and cannot be considered as conclusive evidence especially in the absence of other probative evidence. | That the victim was not 16 years old at the time and the date of birth was not proved by the prosecution.
gnd_other_description: Challenge to weight and conclusiveness of DNA evidence; Challenge to jurisdiction regarding AG sanction.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The victim (PW1) turned hostile, necessitating reliance on other evidence. The court placed significant weight on the DNA report (P2) and the expert testimony of PW11, which established paternity with 99.999% accuracy. Medical evidence (PW9) regarding the victim's childbirth and age was also accepted as it was not challenged by the defence at trial.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court found the DNA evidence, supported by medical testimony, sufficient to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt.
defence_version_summary: The Appellant pleaded not guilty and consented to the appeal arguments in his absence. The defence argued that DNA evidence alone is insufficient for conviction and that procedural requirements regarding sanction were not met.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal dismissed the jurisdictional objection, clarifying that charges under Section 364(3) do not require the Attorney General's sanction, unlike Section 364A. Regarding evidence, the Court affirmed that DNA evidence is a powerful scientific tool and can be relied upon, especially when the expert (PW11) vouched for 100% accuracy and the sample collection process. The Court also noted that the defence failed to cross-examine the medical evidence regarding the victim's age at trial, precluding them from raising it on appeal.
coa_sentence_type: Rigorous Imprisonment 18 years and Fine of Rs. 10,000
brief_judgment_file_summary: The Appellant appealed against his conviction for raping his daughter (under 16), arguing lack of jurisdiction due to no AG sanction and challenging the reliance on DNA evidence. The High Court had convicted him based on DNA proving he fathered the victim's child, despite the victim turning hostile. The Court of Appeal upheld the conviction, ruling that Section 364(3) does not require sanction and that DNA evidence is scientifically reliable and conclusive in this context. The sentence of 18 years RI was affirmed.
hc_analysis_summary: The COA found the High Court Judge meticulously considered the acceptable evidence along with DNA evidence. The HC correctly used the concept of divisibility of evidence to separate the hostile witness's false testimony from the scientific truth.
precedents_cited_list: Bandara v. The State C.A. 27/99 | Ukkuwa v. The Attorney General [2002] 3 SLR 279 | Gunasiri and Two Others v. Republic of Sri Lanka C.A. 116/13 | The Attorney General v. M. N. Naufer alias Potta Naufer and Others [2007] 2 SLR 144 | C. Premjibhai Bachubhai Khasiya v. State of Gujarat and another [2009] CRI.L.J. 2888 | Samaraweera v. AG [1990] 1 SLR 256 | Mark Antony Fernando v. AG CA/84/97
standard_of_review_applied: Review of evidence evaluation and legal applicability of sanctions and DNA evidence.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
brief_facts_summary: The petitioner filed a revision application to set aside an order of the High Court dated 06/01/2021 which refused bail pending appeal. The petitioner failed to exercise their right of appeal against this order and instead invoked the revisionary jurisdiction. The respondent raised a preliminary objection regarding the petitioner's failure to appeal.
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court Judge refused the petitioner's application for bail pending appeal. The petitioner challenged this refusal order via revision.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner argues that there was an illegality in the High Court order violating the Evidence Ordinance, creating an exceptional situation that shocks the conscience of the court. They contend this justifies revision despite not exercising the right of appeal.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Illegality in the order of the High Court which violated the provisions of the Evidence Ordinance | Exceptional situation which shocks the conscious of this Court
gnd_other_description: Maintainability of revision when right of appeal exists but was not exercised
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: Not applicable as this judgment concerns a preliminary objection in a revision application regarding bail procedure, not an evaluation of trial evidence.
defence_version_summary: The petitioner admitted to not exercising the right of appeal but argued that the illegality in the High Court's order constituted exceptional circumstances warranting revision.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court examined the preliminary objection that the petitioner failed to explain why the right of appeal was not exercised. Citing precedents, the Court noted that revision is exceptional. However, accepting the petitioner's submission that the impugned order contained an illegality regarding the Evidence Ordinance, the Court found this could constitute exceptional circumstances. Consequently, the Court overruled the preliminary objection to allow the application to proceed.
coa_sentence_type: Preliminary objection overruled; application fixed for due process.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The Accused-Petitioner filed a revision application against a High Court order refusing bail pending appeal. The Attorney General raised a preliminary objection because the petitioner did not exercise their right of appeal. The Court of Appeal overruled the objection, accepting the argument that an alleged illegality in the order constituted exceptional circumstances warranting revision. The case was fixed for further argument.
defence_counsel: Sarath Jayamanne, PC with Darshana Kuruppu, Vineshka Mendis and Prashan Wickramaratne
hc_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal did not analyze the High Court's substantive reasoning yet, only addressing the procedural objection to the revision application itself.
precedents_cited_list: Martin v Wijewardana 1989 2SLR 409 | Meeriyam Beebee v Seyed Mohammad 68 NLR 36 | Ranasighe v Henry 1 NLR 303 | Athukorala v Samynadan 41 NLR 165 | Abdul Kadar v Suthy Nisar 52 NLR 536 | Appuhamy v Weerathunga 23 NLR 467 | Soomawathie v Madawala 1983 2SLR 15 | Ameen v Rasheed 6 CLW 8 | Dharmarathne and Another v Palm Paradise Cabanas Itd and others 2003 SLR 24
standard_of_review_applied: Revisionary jurisdiction is an exceptional proceeding; where a right of appeal exists, a party must explain why it was not exercised, usually requiring exceptional circumstances.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
brief_facts_summary: The Attorney General filed a revision application seeking to set aside the order of the High Court of Kandy, arguing that the sentence imposed on the Accused-Respondent was inadequate. The application was challenged on preliminary objections regarding the failure to file an affidavit and unexplained delay.
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court delivered an order on 14.12.2015 imposing a sentence which the Attorney General subsequently challenged as being inadequate.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Petitioner (Attorney General) claimed that the sentence imposed by the High Court Judge was inadequate based on the facts of the case and the nature of the offence.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Sentence imposed by the High Court Judge is inadequate
gnd_other_description: Respondent raised preliminary objections regarding the Petitioner's failure to file a mandatory affidavit and unexplained delay in filing the application.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court did not analyze witness evidence as the application was dismissed in limine based on preliminary objections regarding procedure.
defence_version_summary: The Accused-Respondent raised preliminary objections that the Petitioner failed to file a mandatory affidavit under Court of Appeal rules and failed to sufficiently explain the 10-month delay in filing the revision application.
procedural_defects_description: The Petitioner (Attorney General) failed to file an affidavit in support of the revision petition as required by CA Rule 3(1)(a) and failed to substantiate the explanation for the 10-month delay.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court upheld the preliminary objections raised by the Respondent. It held that under CA Rule 3(1)(a), filing an affidavit is mandatory for revision applications, even for the Attorney General, citing 'Attorney General vs. Wilson Silva'. The Court also noted that the 10-month delay in filing was not sufficiently explained or substantiated by affidavit, which is fatal to the application.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The Attorney General filed a revision application against a High Court sentence deemed inadequate. The Respondent raised preliminary objections regarding the AG's failure to file a supporting affidavit and a 10-month unexplained delay. The Court of Appeal upheld the objections, ruling that the affidavit requirement is mandatory and delay is fatal, dismissing the application in limine.
defence_counsel: Nalinda Indatissa, PC with Nuwan D' Alwis instructed by Charith Thuduwage
hc_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal did not review the High Court's reasoning on merits as the application was dismissed on procedural grounds.
precedents_cited_list: Kiriwanthi and another 1990 1 Sri.L.R 2 | Attorney General vs. Wilson Silva 1992 1 Sri L R 44 | CA/PHC/APN78/2021
standard_of_review_applied: Revision application standards regarding mandatory procedural rules and delay.

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 365B (2) (b) of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The victim, a 7-year-old boy, was called into a room by the Appellant (his aunt's daughter), who was 17 years old at the time. The Appellant removed the victim's trousers and committed grave sexual abuse. The incident was revealed later when the victim was involved in an incident with a female student at school.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 365B (2) (b) of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the Appellant and imposed the mandatory minimum sentence of 7 years rigorous imprisonment, along with a fine and compensation. The judge rejected the Appellant's denial of the charge.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Appellant initially raised grounds regarding motive to fabricate, inconsistencies, and failure to consider defence evidence. However, at the hearing, the appeal against conviction was withdrawn, and the counsel restricted the submission to the sentence, arguing for mitigation due to the Appellant's age (17) at the time of the offence.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: PW4 had a motive to induce PW1 to fabricate a false allegation | Relied on uncorroborated and inconsistent evidence | Failed to consider the test of probability and improbability | Failed to offer appropriate prominence and due consideration on the defence evidence | Mitigation of sentence due to Appellant being a child (17 years) at the time of offence
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court did not analyze the witness evidence in detail as the appeal against conviction was withdrawn. The focus remained entirely on the legal principles governing the sentencing of a child offender.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court did not re-evaluate probabilities as the conviction was uncontested at the appeal stage.
defence_version_summary: The Appellant denied the charges at trial. In the appeal, she accepted the conviction but pleaded for a non-custodial sentence, highlighting that she is now a mother of two children and was a minor at the time of the offence.
legal_errors_description: The High Court failed to exercise judicial discretion to impose a lesser sentence than the mandatory minimum, given the Appellant was a child (under 18) at the time of the offence.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal emphasized that the Appellant was 17 years old at the time of the offence. Citing SC Reference No. 03 of 2008 and the Convention on the Rights of the Child, the Court held that it has discretion to deviate from mandatory minimum sentences for child offenders. Considering the Appellant is now a married mother of two and requires rehabilitation rather than incarceration, the Court suspended the sentence.
coa_sentence_type: 2 years Rigorous Imprisonment suspended for 10 years, Fine Rs. 5000/-, Compensation Rs. 100,000/-
brief_judgment_file_summary: The Appellant was convicted of grave sexual abuse committed when she was 17 and sentenced to 7 years rigorous imprisonment. She appealed the sentence, citing her age at the time of the offence and her current status as a mother. The Court of Appeal, applying established precedents regarding the sentencing of children, set aside the custodial term and imposed a suspended sentence of 2 years. The conviction was affirmed as the appeal against it was withdrawn.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court imposed the statutory minimum sentence without considering the Appellant's age as a mitigating factor warranting deviation. The Court of Appeal corrected this by applying the principle of the 'best interests of the child'.
precedents_cited_list: Supreme Court determination No. 03 of 2008 | Dharma Sri Tissa Kumara Wijenaike v. Attorney General SC Appeal 179/2012 | Jusabbhai Ayubbhai v. State of Gujarat CR.MA/623/2012
standard_of_review_applied: Sentencing discretion review
sentencing_principles_applied: Best interests of the child, Judicial discretion over mandatory minimums for minors, Reformation and rehabilitation over punishment

judges: K. Priyantha Fernando, J. | Wickum A. Kaluarachchi, J.
offence_sections: Section 54A(b) and 54A(d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance as amended
location_of_offence: Near the junction where Jalashaya Road and Sri Vajiragnana Mawatha meet, Maradana
brief_facts_summary: On 20.05.2013, Police Narcotics Bureau officers acted on information and set up near a supermarket in Maradana. When the appellant arrived near Jalashaya Road, officers searched him and found a parcel containing 24.89 grams of Heroin in his right trouser pocket. A subsequent search of his house yielded no illicit items.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 54A(b) and 54A(d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance as amended
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the accused of trafficking and possessing heroin and sentenced him to death. The judge rejected the defence's claims of fabrication, noting that the defence failed to prove the police notes were false or that the arrest happened elsewhere.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the main prosecution witness was untrustworthy and that the trial judge erred by shifting the burden of proof to the accused. The defence also contended that the police description of the arrest location was incorrect, implying the arrest took place at the appellant's home.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Evidence of PW 1 is untrustworthy and contradictory | Learned High Court Judge shifted the burden of proof to the accused | Learned High Court Judge came to conclusions on assumptions | Defence evidence has not been properly analyzed | Learned Judge incorrectly concluded that the defence had not challenged prosecution evidence | Learned Judge incorrectly observed appellant failed to prove police notes were false | Place of arrest described by PW 1 and PW 6 is wrong
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found that PW 1 and PW 6 were consistent regarding the physical landmarks of the arrest location (near Ananda College and Vidyodaya Pirivena), despite uncertainty about the specific road name. The Court held their evidence was credible and mutually corroborating.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court found the prosecution case proved beyond reasonable doubt, stating that minor discrepancies in road names did not affect the credibility of the arrest location evidence.
defence_version_summary: The defence claimed the drugs were planted due to animosity with the police witness (PW1) arising from a previous case. The appellant stated from the dock that he was arrested at home, not on the road. Witnesses (wife, Surveyor, GN) were called to challenge the prosecution's description of the arrest location.
legal_errors_description: The Court of Appeal found the High Court Judge incorrectly observed that the appellant failed to prove police notes were false and failed to prove he was not arrested on Jalashaya Road, which erroneously shifted the burden of proof to the accused.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court acknowledged the High Court's errors regarding the burden of proof but determined they did not prejudice the final outcome. The Court found the police witnesses credible regarding the arrest location despite a road name error. Crucially, the Court rejected the defence because it presented two contradictory versions: one suggesting animosity with an informant (put to witnesses) and another alleging animosity with PW1 (in the dock statement), which was never put to the prosecution witnesses.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The appellant appealed a death sentence for heroin trafficking, arguing the trial judge shifted the burden of proof and that the arrest location evidence was false. The Court of Appeal admitted the trial judge made erroneous remarks about the burden of proof but found the evidence independently sufficient. The appeal was dismissed primarily because the defence presented contradictory versions of the events and failed to cross-examine witnesses on the version given in the dock statement.
hc_analysis_summary: The COA criticized the HC for stating the accused failed to prove certain facts, noting the burden lies entirely on the prosecution. However, the COA affirmed the HC's ultimate decision to convict based on the strength of the prosecution evidence and the weakness of the contradictory defence.
precedents_cited_list: Sarvan Singh V. State of Punjab (2002 AIR SC (iii) 3652
standard_of_review_applied: The Court reviewed the evidence independently to determine if the trial judge's defective observations influenced the final decision.
sentencing_principles_applied: The sentence was imposed considering the quantity of Heroin.

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 364(1) of the Penal Code; Section 365(b)(2) of the Penal Code as amended
brief_facts_summary: The victim was at the airport to depart for foreign employment when the appellant, posing as an agent, took her to a room. He allegedly forced her to drink a liquid, threatened her with a knife, and removed her clothes to reveal her genitalia. She lost consciousness and was later put on a flight but returned due to illness.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 365(b)(2) of the Penal Code as amended
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court Judge allowed an amendment to the indictment adding an alternative charge of Grave Sexual Abuse after the prosecution case concluded. The Judge convicted the appellant on this alternative charge and sentenced him to 20 years rigorous imprisonment.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant challenged the legality of the amendment made to the indictment at the late stage of the trial. Additionally, it was argued that the trial judge failed to determine the identity of the appellant beyond reasonable doubt.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The amendment made to the indictment. | The Learned Trial Judge failed to make a determination that the prosecution had proved the identity of the appellant beyond reasonable doubt.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The court relied primarily on the evidence of the victim (PW1). The court analyzed whether the acts described by the victim (removal of clothes, threat with knife) constituted the offence of Grave Sexual Abuse or a lesser offence.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court concluded that the evidence did not support the charge of Grave Sexual Abuse but was sufficient for Sexual Harassment.
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty, gave evidence, and called witnesses on his behalf. The defence objected to the amendment of the indictment.
legal_errors_description: The High Court convicted the appellant under Section 365(b)(2) (Grave Sexual Abuse) when the proven facts (threatening with a knife, removing clothes) did not meet the ingredients of that section but rather Section 345 (Sexual Harassment).
procedural_defects_description: The prosecution amended the indictment adding a distinct offence as an alternative charge at the close of the case, which was legally questionable, though the appeal was decided on the ingredients of the offence.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal held that the acts proven (threatening with a knife, forced drinking, removal of clothes) did not constitute 'Grave Sexual Abuse' under Section 365B(1) as there was no specific sexual act on an orifice or use of genitals. However, these acts clearly constituted 'Sexual Harassment' under Section 345, which covers assault or criminal force causing sexual annoyance. The Court substituted the conviction to Section 345 and reduced the sentence.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The appellant was indicted for Rape, later amended to include Grave Sexual Abuse. The High Court convicted him of Grave Sexual Abuse. The Court of Appeal found the evidence did not support Grave Sexual Abuse but did support Sexual Harassment under Section 345. The conviction was substituted, and the sentence reduced to 5 years RI.
defence_counsel: Anil Silva, P.C. with Isuru Jayawardena
hc_analysis_summary: The COA criticized the High Court for not properly analyzing the ingredients of Section 365B versus Section 345. The HC failed to see that the facts did not fit the grave nature required for Section 365B.
precedents_cited_list: John Perera v. Weerasinghe 53 NLR 158 | Doole v. The Republic of Sri Lanka [1978-1979] 2 SLR 33 | R. T. Wilbert and 3 others v. Newman 75 NLR 138 | Mahalakotuwa v. The Attorney General [2011] 2 B.L.R 406 | Tuck and Sons v. Priester (1887 (19) QBD 629
standard_of_review_applied: Review of legal ingredients against proven facts
sentencing_principles_applied: The court applied the principle that if acts fall between two sections or are uncertain, the more lenient construction should be given (lenity).

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 read with section 32 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The deceased went missing after borrowing a bicycle. His body was found in a well two days later. The prosecution's case rested on circumstantial evidence, including the recovery of the bicycle and a wrist watch allegedly pawned by the appellant. The appellant was arrested and charged with murder along with a second accused who was acquitted.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 read with section 32 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court Judge found the appellant guilty based on circumstantial evidence. The judge concluded that the evidence pointed to the guilt of the accused, despite the defence's arguments regarding the sufficiency of the nexus.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the High Court Judge failed to evaluate the circumstantial evidence in its correct perspective and failed to establish a proper nexus between the evidence and the appellant. Additionally, it was urged that the dock statement was not properly evaluated.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Failure to evaluate circumstantial evidence in correct perspective | Failure to establish proper nexus between available evidence and the appellant | Failure to properly evaluate the dock statement of the appellant
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found the circumstantial evidence to be weak and lacking a proper nexus to the appellant. The recovery of the bicycle and watch was deemed 'sketchy' with no clear timeline or proven connection to the crime. The recovery of glass pieces lacked details on location, and DNA results were inconclusive.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court held that the suspicion raised by the circumstances was insufficient to prove the charge beyond reasonable doubt.
defence_version_summary: The appellant made a dock statement denying involvement, claiming he went to harvest paddy with one Kamal. He alleged Kamal was the one who pawned the bicycle and watch, and he had nothing to do with the murder.
legal_errors_description: The High Court misdirected itself on the facts and the application of the law regarding circumstantial evidence, finding guilt where the evidence did not irresistibly point to the accused.
procedural_defects_description: The Attorney General failed to indict a committed suspect (Kamal) or communicate his discharge to the Magistrate, leaving him potentially still reporting to police under bail conditions.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court accepted the Senior State Counsel's concession that the conviction could not be supported due to poor circumstantial evidence. The Court found no evidence linking the appellant to the disposal of the body or the recovery of the bicycle. The evidence regarding the watch was ambiguous, and the glass recovery lacked specific location details. The Court concluded the chain of circumstances was broken and insufficient for a conviction.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The appellant was convicted of murder by the High Court based on circumstantial evidence involving a bicycle and watch. The Court of Appeal set aside the conviction after the prosecution conceded the evidence was insufficient to support the verdict. The Court highlighted the lack of nexus between the items and the crime, and the failure to prove the chain of circumstances. The appellant was acquitted.
hc_analysis_summary: The COA found the HC Judge's reliance on the available circumstantial evidence was misplaced as it did not exclude reasonable hypotheses of innocence. The HC failed to see the lack of connection between the recovered items and the crime.
precedents_cited_list: The King Vs. Abeywickrama 44 NLR 254 | Don Sunny Vs. The Attorney General (1998) 2 SLR 01 | The Queen Vs. D.G.DE.S.Kularatne and two others 71 NLR 529 | Regina Vs. Exall (176 English Reports, Nisi Prius at page 853)
standard_of_review_applied: Review of whether circumstantial evidence irresistibly points to guilt

judges: M. T. Mohammed Laffar, J. | S. U. B. Karalliyadde, J.
offence_sections: Section 8(1) and Section 13 of the Payment of Gratuity Act, No. 12 of 1983
brief_facts_summary: The Petitioner company sought to quash a certificate issued by the Deputy Commissioner of Labour to the Magistrate's Court for the recovery of gratuity owed to the 4th Respondent, a former General Manager. The Petitioner had forfeited the gratuity alleging the employee was involved in misappropriation of funds, but the employee had resigned and his resignation was accepted. The Petitioner argued the Commissioner lacked jurisdiction and failed to give a proper hearing.
hc_judgment_summary: The Magistrate refused the application of the Company to reject the Certificate issued by the Deputy Commissioner of Labour and decided to recover the amount mentioned in the Certificate. A subsequent revision application to the Provincial High Court of Negombo was dismissed.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Petitioner argued that the Commissioner of Labour had no jurisdiction to decide on the forfeiture of gratuity, asserting that such power lies with the Labour Tribunal. They also claimed they were not given a proper hearing (violation of natural justice) before the certificate was issued.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The Company was never heard by the 2nd Respondent before issuing the Certificate and no reasons were given. | The decision-making power about the question of payment of gratuity is vested with the labour tribunal and not with the Commissioner of Labour.
gnd_other_description: Challenge to jurisdiction of the Commissioner of Labour versus Labour Tribunal
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court reviewed the documentary evidence, specifically letters exchanged between the parties (P-16, P-17, P-18, P-19, P-20, P-21). The Court found that contrary to the Petitioner's claim of not being heard, the documents showed the Petitioner was invited to inquiries and had made representations.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court found the Petitioner misrepresented facts regarding the lack of a hearing.
defence_version_summary: The Petitioner (Company) claimed the employee misappropriated funds between 2007-2011, justifying the forfeiture of gratuity under Section 13 of the Gratuity Act, even though the employee resigned in 2014.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court held that under Section 13 of the Gratuity Act, forfeiture is only valid if services are terminated for specific reasons like fraud or misappropriation. Since the 4th Respondent resigned and the resignation was accepted with EPF payments, the Petitioner could not forfeit gratuity based on past allegations. Consequently, the Commissioner had jurisdiction to issue the certificate for default under Section 8. Furthermore, the Court found the Petitioner had suppressed material facts regarding the inquiries held, as documents showed they were given opportunities to be heard. Due to this suppression/misrepresentation, the Court refused to exercise its discretionary power.
coa_sentence_type: Application Dismissed; Petitioner to pay Rs. 75,000 as costs
brief_judgment_file_summary: Lanka Milk Foods filed a writ application to quash a certificate issued by the Commissioner of Labour for the recovery of gratuity owed to a former General Manager. The Company had forfeited the gratuity alleging misappropriation, despite accepting the employee's resignation. The Court of Appeal dismissed the application, ruling that the Commissioner had jurisdiction since the termination was not for fraud under Section 13, and found the Petitioner had suppressed facts about having been given a hearing.
defence_counsel: Shiran Coorey instructed by Ms. H.K. Bulathwatta
hc_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal implicitly upheld the Magistrate's decision to proceed with the recovery based on the certificate, noting the Provincial High Court had already affirmed the Magistrate's order in a separate revision application.
precedents_cited_list: Baur and Co Ltd Vs Commissioner General of Labour C. A. No:1033/2005 | W. S. Alphonso Appuhamy v. Hettiarachchi 277 N.L.R. 131 | The King v. The General Commissioner for the Purpose of the Income Tax Acts for the District of Kensington-Ex-parte Princess Edmorbd de Poigns | Blanca Diamonds (Pvt) Ltd Vs Wilfred Van Els And Two Others (1997) 1 Sri LR 360 | Laub Vs Attorney General And Another (1995) 2 Sri LR 88 | W.A Bhathiya Indika Wickramasinghe Vs Land Commissioner General & Others CA (Writ) 381/2017 | Sarath Hulangamuwa Vs Siriwardena, Principal, Vishaka Vidyalaya and others (1986) 1 SLR 275
standard_of_review_applied: Writ Jurisdiction (Certiorari and Mandamus)

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Section 20(b) read with 20(a) iv and Section 19 (c) of the Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery or Corruption Act No 19 of 1994
brief_facts_summary: The petitioner, a former Additional Educational Director, was convicted of bribery charges. She applied for bail pending appeal, citing medical grounds and personal hardships, which the High Court refused. She filed this revision application challenging the refusal of bail and raising a legal objection regarding the prosecution's sanction.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 20(b) read with 20(a) iv and Section 19 (c) of the Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery or Corruption Act No 19 of 1994
hc_sentence_type: Rigorous Imprisonment 20 years (concurrent, to be carried out in 10 years)
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the accused and sentenced her to 20 years of Rigorous Imprisonment. In a subsequent order dated 24.11.2020, the High Court refused to grant bail pending appeal, ruling that the petitioner failed to prove exceptional circumstances.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner argued that the High Court erred in refusing bail, citing medical conditions, the welfare of students at her private school, and delays in the appeal. Additionally, a legal objection was raised regarding the validity of the prosecution sanction/directive by the Commissioners.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Medical condition (outdated reports). | Plight of 150 students in petitioner's private school. | Delay in concluding the appeal. | Invalidity of the prosecution sanction (absence of directive by 3 Commissioners).
gnd_other_description: Exceptional circumstances for bail pending appeal.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: Not applicable; this is a revision application regarding bail pending appeal and does not analyze trial evidence.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court assessed whether the grounds presented constituted 'exceptional circumstances' for bail, finding they did not.
defence_version_summary: The defence argued for release on bail based on health issues, the impact of incarceration on the petitioner's music/dancing school, and a fundamental legal defect in the institution of the case.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal affirmed the High Court's refusal of bail. It held that the medical reports provided were outdated (2005-2014) and did not prove a present danger to life. The argument regarding the legal validity of the prosecution sanction (directive by 3 Commissioners) was deemed inappropriate for revision proceedings at this stage, with the Court distinguishing cited precedents that applied to Magistrate Court actions.
coa_sentence_type: Rigorous Imprisonment 20 years (concurrent, to be carried out in 10 years)
brief_judgment_file_summary: The petitioner, convicted of bribery and sentenced to 20 years RI, applied for revision of a High Court order refusing bail pending appeal. She relied on medical grounds, personal hardship, and a legal challenge to the prosecution's sanction. The Court of Appeal dismissed the application, finding no exceptional circumstances and ruling the legal challenge inapplicable in revision.
prosecution_counsel: Wasantha Perera, SSC with Priyangani Jayalath, ADA
hc_analysis_summary: The COA found the High Court correctly insisted on the burden of proving exceptional circumstances and properly dismissed the grounds raised.
precedents_cited_list: Anoma Polwatte v Jayawickrama Director General, Bribery Commission and Others SC (writ) 01/2011 | Nandasena Gotabhaya Rajapakse v CIABOC CA (Revision) APN No 29/2018 | B. A. Ranjan Somasinghe v Director General, Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery or Corruption CPA 2/21 | Ajahn Gardiye Punchihewa v Officer in Charge Financial Investigation Unit III CA (Writ) Application No. WRT 311/2019 | Director General, Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery or Corruption v W. A. Lalith Kumara CA Application No. LTA/06/16
standard_of_review_applied: Revisionary jurisdiction (Exceptional Circumstances)

judges: Prasantha De Silva, J. | K.K.A.V. Swarnadhipathi, J.
offence_sections: Section 90(4) of the Agrarian Development Act No. 46 of 2000 as amended by Act No. 46 of 2011
brief_facts_summary: The Appellants obstructed an agricultural road that had been in use since the 1950s for transporting farming equipment to paddy fields. Although the Appellants had previously agreed to a settlement to clear the road, they subsequently obstructed it again at another point. The Additional Commissioner of Agrarian Services instituted action against them for this obstruction.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 90(4) of the Agrarian Development Act No. 46 of 2000 as amended by Act No. 46 of 2011
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court upheld the Magistrate's finding of guilt regarding the obstruction charges. However, the High Court partly allowed the revision application by suspending the six-month rigorous imprisonment sentence for a period of five years, while maintaining the fine.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Appellants appealed against the High Court's order in revision, seeking to set aside the conviction and sentence. They argued that the lower courts erred in their findings, but the Court of Appeal focused on whether the High Court correctly exercised its revisionary jurisdiction.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Challenge to the Order of the Magistrate's Court dated 15.09.2009 | Challenge to the Order of the High Court dated 23.02.2016
gnd_other_description: Challenge to the correctness of the High Court's exercise of revisionary jurisdiction
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Magistrate accepted the evidence that the road had been used since the 1950s and was obstructed by the Appellants. The High Court affirmed this evaluation, noting the Appellants had breached a previous settlement by re-obstructing the road.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court found the charges were proved beyond reasonable doubt based on the evidence led.
defence_version_summary: The Appellants admitted to a settlement in 2008 but were found to have obstructed the road again. They attempted to challenge the conviction through appeal and revision, arguing against the findings of the lower courts.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal limited its review to determining whether the High Court had properly exercised its revisionary jurisdiction regarding legality, propriety, and regularity. The Court found that the High Court had correctly evaluated the evidence and the Magistrate's order. The Court observed that the Appellants had re-obstructed the road despite a prior settlement and failed to demonstrate any miscarriage of justice or exceptional circumstances warranting intervention.
coa_sentence_type: Six months rigorous imprisonment suspended for 5 years and a fine of Rs. 5000/-
brief_judgment_file_summary: The Appellants were convicted by the Magistrate for obstructing an agricultural road under the Agrarian Development Act. The High Court, acting in revision, affirmed the conviction but suspended the sentence of imprisonment. The Court of Appeal dismissed the subsequent appeal, holding that the High Court correctly exercised its revisionary powers and that the conviction was supported by evidence of continued obstruction.
prosecution_counsel: S. Samaratunga JASA with S. Ahamed S.C
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court found the Magistrate's decision on facts and law to be correct but suspended the sentence. The Court of Appeal agreed with this analysis.
precedents_cited_list: Nandawathi and another Vs. Mahindasena [(2009) 2 SLR 218] | Attorney General Vs. Ranasinghe & Others (1993) 2 SLR 81
standard_of_review_applied: Revisionary jurisdiction (Legality, Propriety, Regularity)

judges: K. Priyantha Fernando, J. | Wickum A. Kaluarachchi, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The deceased left home to collect money from the accused and did not return. The accused was seen running and sweating from the area where the deceased's body was later found. The accused gave the deceased's mobile phone to a witness on the same day and asked him to dispose of it later. The cause of death was determined to be strangulation.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court Judge convicted the appellant based on circumstantial evidence. The Judge sentenced the appellant to death after finding the proved circumstances were consistent only with the guilt of the accused.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the prosecution failed to establish the time of death and that the trial judge failed to consider evidence in totality, specifically evidence favoring the accused. It was also argued that the judge compartmentalized evidence, ignored contradictions, misdirected himself regarding the defence, and failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The prosecution has failed to establish the time of death. | The learned High Court Judge has failed to consider the evidence in its totality and hence failed to consider evidence which is in favour of the accused-appellant. | The learned High Court Judge has compartmentalized evidence and therefore failed to properly evaluate the evidence. | The learned High Court Judge has failed to consider the inter-se contradictions of the evidence led by the prosecution. | The learned High Court Judge has misdirected himself regarding the defence taken by the accused-appellant. | The prosecution has failed to prove their case beyond reasonable doubt.
gnd_other_description: Failure to establish time of death
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The prosecution relied on neighbors and the deceased's family members to establish movements and possession of the deceased's phone. The medical officer (PW12) provided expert opinion on the time and cause of death. The Court found the circumstantial evidence regarding the phone and the accused's behavior to be strong and consistent.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court applied the principles regarding circumstantial evidence, finding the facts inconsistent with innocence and consistent only with guilt.
defence_version_summary: The accused made a statement from the dock denying possession of the mobile phone. He claimed he was travelling in a bus at the time the death was estimated to have occurred.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal upheld the High Court's findings, noting that the medical evidence regarding the time of death was consistent with the prosecution's timeline. The Court emphasized the strong circumstantial evidence, particularly the accused's possession of the deceased's phone and his subsequent instruction to destroy it. The Court concluded that the circumstances were consistent only with the guilt of the accused and rejected the defence's alibi.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused appealed a conviction for murder and a death sentence imposed by the High Court of Anuradhapura. The prosecution's case relied on circumstantial evidence, including the accused being seen near the crime scene and possessing the deceased's phone. The accused claimed an alibi and challenged the time of death, but the Court of Appeal found the evidence robust and dismissed the appeal.
prosecution_counsel: Dilan Ratnayake, Senior Deputy Solicitor General
cause_of_death_exact_text: strangulation
hc_analysis_summary: The COA found that the trial Judge rightly rejected the defence version and clearly discussed and analyzed the evidence. The proved circumstances were found to be consistent with guilt.
precedents_cited_list: The King v. Appuhamy [1945] 46 NLR 128 | Junaiden Mohmed Haaris V. Hon. Attorney General SC Appeal 118/17 | R. V. Blom 1939 AD 188
standard_of_review_applied: Review of whether circumstantial evidence excludes every reasonable inference save the one to be drawn.

judges: Sampath B Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 364(1) of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The accused, a 'Kapu Mahaththaya', was hired by the prosecutrix to perform a ritual to bring back her boyfriend. On a subsequent visit, he sent family members away, applied oil to the prosecutrix's genitals, and raped her while she was dizzy. A complaint was made two days later after an alms-giving ceremony.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 364(1) of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the accused of rape and sentenced him to nine years rigorous imprisonment, a fine, and compensation. The trial judge accepted the prosecutrix's evidence as credible and found the delayed complaint justifiable under the circumstances.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued the prosecutrix was not credible, penetration was not proved, and prejudice was caused by leading medical evidence before the prosecutrix testified. He also alleged infirmities in the judgment regarding the burden of proof and failure to consider certain evidence.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The prosecutrix was not a credible witness hence, her evidence should not have been relied on by the learned trial judge. | The prosecution has failed to prove the penile penetration. | Medial evidence was not conclusive and leading of medical evidence before the prosecutrix gave evidence has caused prejudice to the appellant. | Infirmities in the judgment.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court found the prosecutrix credible, noting her explanation for the delayed complaint (fear and family event) was plausible. Her testimony was consistent with the medical evidence of a hymeneal tear.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court found the prosecution proved the case beyond reasonable doubt, rejecting the defence theory of false implication due to a fee dispute.
defence_version_summary: The accused admitted visiting the house for rituals but denied rape, claiming the charge was fabricated because he was only paid Rs. 500 instead of the agreed Rs. 7500.
procedural_defects_description: The JMO's evidence was led before the prosecutrix's evidence, which is irregular but was found not to have caused prejudice.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal, affirming the High Court's findings on credibility and the justification for the delayed complaint.
