The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal, ruling that no direct appeal lies against such interlocutory orders in extradition proceedings. The court clarified that the proper remedy is to seek a Writ of Habeas Corpus after the conclusion of the High Court inquiry. The High Court was directed to expedite the pending inquiry.
defence_counsel: Shanaka Ranasinghe PC with Niroshan Mihindu Kulasuriya and Ms. Tharakee Manchanayak
hc_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found no error in the High Court's decision to proceed, affirming that the High Court's jurisdiction is limited to determining if the offence is extraditable and if evidence is sufficient, rather than reviewing the Minister's discretion.
precedents_cited_list: The Attorney General v Ruberoe and Others (1988) 1 SLR 323 | Philip Gordon James Benwell v The Attorney General (1986) 1 SLR 30 | Re Naresh Parsaram Butani (1991) 1 SLR 350 | Benwell v The Attorney General 1988 1 SLR 1 | R v. Governor of Brixton Prison ex parte Schtraks | In re Galway | SC Appeal No 41/2-15 | SC/CHC Appeal 37/2008
standard_of_review_applied: Review of procedural maintainability of appeal against interlocutory order

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Section 15 (1) of the Tobacco Tax Act
offence_category: Possession of imported cigarettes without paying taxes
brief_facts_summary: The petitioner, who ran a registered rent-a-car business, rented a vehicle to the accused, Sunil. Sunil was arrested for possessing imported cigarettes without paying taxes, and the vehicle was seized. Sunil pleaded guilty, and the Magistrate ordered the confiscation of the vehicle, which the High Court affirmed, despite the petitioner's claim of innocence.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 15 (1) of the Tobacco Tax Act
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court affirmed the Magistrate's order to confiscate the vehicle used in the commission of the offence. The court did not adequately consider the petitioner's lack of knowledge regarding the illegal activity conducted by the hirer.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner argued that he was the registered owner of the vehicle used in a rent-a-car business and had no knowledge of the offence. He contended that the lower courts failed to consider the rental agreement which prohibited illegal use and his lack of physical control over the vehicle.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Magistrate and High Court failed to adequately consider the petitioner's lack of knowledge of the incident. | Petitioner had a valid rental agreement prohibiting illegal use. | Physical control of the vehicle was not with the petitioner. | Petitioner should not be penalized for the hirer's actions.
gnd_other_description: Failure to consider innocent owner's rights and lack of knowledge in forfeiture proceedings
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal focused on the documentary evidence provided by the petitioner (V1 and V2), specifically the rental agreement. The court found that the agreement demonstrated the petitioner's bona fides and the clause prohibiting illegal activities supported his lack of knowledge.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court found it very unlikely that the petitioner had knowledge of the incident because physical control was not with him and the agreement prohibited illegal acts.
defence_version_summary: The petitioner (claimant) maintained that he rented the vehicle to the accused under a lawful agreement and was unaware of the illegal transportation of cigarettes. He argued he should not lose his property due to the renter's crime.
legal_errors_description: The lower courts failed to apply the provisions allowing a claimant to prove lack of knowledge before confiscation of tools used in an offence.
procedural_defects_description: Failure to adequately hear the claimant regarding his lack of knowledge.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal held that under the relevant Act, a claimant must be heard and given the opportunity to prove they had no knowledge of the offence before their property is confiscated. The Court found the rental agreement (V1/V2) sufficient to establish the petitioner's lack of knowledge and lack of control over the vehicle at the time of the offence. Consequently, the confiscation orders were set aside.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The petitioner challenged the confiscation of his vehicle, which had been used by a renter to transport illegal cigarettes. The accused renter had pleaded guilty. The Court of Appeal set aside the confiscation orders of the Magistrate and High Court, ruling that the petitioner successfully proved his lack of knowledge through a valid rental agreement prohibiting illegal use.
defence_counsel: Anura Meddegoda PC with Asela Muthumudalige
hc_analysis_summary: The COA criticized the High Court for not adequately considering the petitioner's knowledge of the incident and for failing to give weight to the rental agreement which prohibited illegal activities.
standard_of_review_applied: Revision jurisdiction to correct errors of law and fact regarding forfeiture.
sentencing_principles_applied: Protection of property rights of innocent third parties who lack knowledge of the offence.

judges: K Priyantha Fernando, J. | Sampath B Abayakoon, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code and Section 300 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The appellant was indicted for the murder of his wife and attempted murder of his daughter by setting them on fire at their rented house. The prosecution's case relied on the evidence of the child witness (daughter) and dying declarations made by the deceased. The appellant contended that the deceased committed suicide due to marital disputes.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code and Section 300 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court found the appellant guilty as charged after a trial without a jury. The judge accepted the evidence of the child witness and the dying declarations, despite noting certain infirmities and rejecting the evidence of the deceased's mother.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the High Court judge failed to properly consider the evidence and erred in finding the charges proved beyond reasonable doubt. Specifically, the defence challenged the reliability of the child witness due to age and delay, and noted the prosecution's failure to call material witnesses.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The learned High Court judge has failed to properly consider the evidence led at the trial. | On the basis of the evidence placed before the Court, the learned High Court judge could not have come to a finding that the charges were proved beyond reasonable doubt.
gnd_other_description: Failure to call material witness (landlady) and shifting burden of proof to accused
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found the child witness unreliable due to her tender age (3 years at incident), the long delay before giving evidence, and potential influence by relatives. The court also found the written dying declaration unsafe as there was no medical evidence of the victim's capacity to write despite severe burns, and the recording officer was partial.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court held that the prosecution failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt, and the appellant's explanation of suicide created a reasonable doubt.
defence_version_summary: The appellant stated in a dock statement that his wife committed suicide due to marital disputes and that he was innocent. He explained that he kept petrol at home for his motorcycle.
legal_errors_description: The High Court judge misdirected himself on the facts regarding the reliability of the child witness and misdirected on the law by shifting the burden of proof to the accused to call the landlady, instead of drawing an adverse inference against the prosecution for not calling her.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal determined that the trial judge erred in relying on the child witness's evidence given the possibilities of coaching and the child's inability to recall details during earlier examinations. The Court also found the written dying declaration doubtful due to the lack of medical certification of the deceased's capacity to write. Crucially, the Court held that the failure to call the landlady, a material witness, should have resulted in an adverse inference against the prosecution under Section 114(f) of the Evidence Ordinance, rather than shifting the burden to the accused.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The appellant was convicted of murder and attempted murder for allegedly setting his wife and daughter on fire. The High Court relied on the child's testimony and dying declarations. The Court of Appeal set aside the conviction, finding the child witness unreliable and the dying declaration unsafe due to medical uncertainties. The Court also ruled that the trial judge improperly shifted the burden of proof to the accused regarding a missing material witness.
defence_counsel: ASM Perera, PC, with Angela Joseph
hc_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal criticized the High Court for accepting the child's evidence despite contradictions and for failing to draw an adverse inference from the prosecution's failure to call the landlady.
precedents_cited_list: The King Vs. Asirvadan Nadar 51NLR 322 | The Queen Vs. Sumanasena 66 NLR 350 | Pantis Vs. The Attorney General (1998) 2 SLR 148
standard_of_review_applied: Re-evaluation of evidence reliability and application of burden of proof

judges: K Priyantha Fernando, J. | Sampath B Abayakoon, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 and Section 316 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The accused appellant drove a motor lorry into a crowd of people on the Colombo-Galle main road, killing two individuals (including a police officer) and injuring four others. This incident occurred shortly after a dispute involving the appellant's relatives. The appellant surrendered to the police shortly after the incident.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 and Section 316 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court judge found the accused guilty as charged after a trial without a jury. The judge rejected the defense that the incident was an unavoidable accident and sentenced the accused accordingly.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the main prosecution witness (PW-01) was unreliable and that the incident was an unavoidable accident, not an intentional act. It was further argued that the trial court erroneously shifted the burden of proof to the accused.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The trial Court was in error when it failed to consider that PW-01 Nirmal Ajantha Gunawardena was not a reliable witness. | The learned trial judge has failed to consider that the entire incident was an accident and the accused appellant cannot be found guilty for the charges in the indictment. | The trial Court was in error when it shifted the burden of proof of certain matters to the accused appellant, whereas the burden rests on the prosecution. | The learned trial judge has failed to analyze the charges against the appellant separately.
gnd_other_description: Argument that the act was an unavoidable accident and lacked intention
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found that supposed contradictions in PW-01's evidence regarding road locations were irrelevant because the appellant admitted to driving the lorry that hit the victims. The medical evidence confirmed deaths and injuries were due to a road traffic accident. The injured witnesses were consistent in their testimony.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court assessed that the appellant must have known his act was so imminently dangerous that it would in all probability cause death, satisfying the fourth limb of Section 294.
defence_version_summary: The appellant made a dock statement claiming he was driving to pick up his mother-in-law when people fell onto the road, making the collision unavoidable. He claimed he fled due to fear of assault.
legal_errors_description: The High Court judge misdirected by commenting that the appellant's failure to call persons he claimed were in the vehicle was relevant under section 114 of the Evidence Ordinance.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court held that even if there was no specific intention to kill, the appellant's actions fell under the fourth limb of Section 294 of the Penal Code (knowledge of imminent danger). The Court noted the short distance from which the appellant entered the road made his 'accident' defense implausible. The misdirection regarding Section 114 of the Evidence Ordinance was deemed not to have prejudiced the appellant as the conviction was based on prosecution evidence.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The appellant appealed against his conviction for murder and causing hurt resulting from a 2005 incident where he drove a lorry into a crowd. The High Court rejected his defense of accidental collision. The Court of Appeal affirmed the conviction, ruling that the appellant's conduct demonstrated knowledge that his act was imminently dangerous, satisfying the definition of murder under the fourth limb of Section 294 of the Penal Code.
defence_counsel: Darshana Kuruppu with Sajini Elvitigala and Dinara Bandara
cause_of_death_exact_text: road traffic accident
hc_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal noted the High Court judge correctly analyzed the evidence despite a minor misdirection on Section 114 of the Evidence Ordinance. The COA agreed with the HC's rejection of the accident defense.
precedents_cited_list: A.W.Haprul Asad and another Vs. The Attorney General C.A. Case No-91-2013 | The King Vs. Rengasamy 25 NLR 438
standard_of_review_applied: Analysis of evidence and application of the fourth limb of Section 294 of the Penal Code

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna, J. | R. Gurusinghe, J.
offence_sections: Section 383 of the Penal Code and Section 296 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The accused was indicted for murdering Sivakumar Indumathi and stealing two gold rings and a chain. The prosecution's case rested on circumstantial evidence, alleging a pawn receipt for the deceased's rings was found in the accused's wallet. The High Court convicted him based on the presumption of possession of stolen goods.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 383 of the Penal Code and Section 296 of the Penal Code
hc_sentence_type: Death Sentence and 10 years Rigorous Imprisonment
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the accused on both counts, relying on Section 114 of the Evidence Ordinance. The judge inferred guilt because the accused allegedly possessed a pawn receipt for rings identified as the deceased's and offered no explanation.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appeal argued that the circumstantial evidence was insufficient to sustain a conviction. It challenged the identification of the jewelry, the validity of the recovery of the pawn receipt, and the lack of a proven link between the accused and the crime.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Identification of the rings as belonging to the deceased was not proved beyond reasonable doubt. | The recovery of the pawn receipt from the appellant's wallet was not a valid discovery under Section 27 of the Evidence Ordinance. | The pawn receipt did not bear the name of the accused and was not produced in the original record. | The chain of custody and connection between the accused and the pawned items were not established.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found the prosecution witnesses unreliable on key points. The husband (PW2) could not uniquely identify the rings and had poor recollection of the deceased's attire. The pawn broker (PW3) could not identify the accused or link the receipt to him.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court found the evidence insufficient to exclude reasonable hypotheses of innocence, as the connection between the accused and the stolen items was not firmly established.
defence_version_summary: The accused maintained his innocence in a dock statement, asserting that no pawning receipt or jewelry was obtained from him by the police and denying involvement in the murder or plunder.
legal_errors_description: The High Court erred in applying the presumption under Section 114 of the Evidence Ordinance without cogent proof of possession. The court also improperly relied on a 'discovery' from the accused's wallet which did not meet Section 27 requirements.
procedural_defects_description: The pawn receipt allegedly recovered was not found in the original case record and was not listed in the production list. The recovered part of the chain was not produced in court.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court held that the circumstantial evidence was not consistent only with guilt. The recovery of the pawn receipt was flawed, the receipt lacked the accused's name, and the identification of the rings was weak. Consequently, the possession of stolen property was not proved, breaking the chain of circumstances required for conviction.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused was convicted of murder and robbery by the High Court of Vavuniya based on circumstantial evidence involving a pawn receipt found in his wallet. The Court of Appeal set aside the conviction, ruling that the prosecution failed to prove the items belonged to the deceased or that the accused pawned them. The appeal was allowed and the accused was acquitted.
defence_counsel: Gayan Perera, with Ms. P. H. Perera and Panchali Ekanayake
cause_of_death_exact_text: severe injuries caused to the esophagus and pharynx (throat)
hc_analysis_summary: The COA criticized the HC for basing the conviction on a factual error regarding the recovery of the rings and for drawing a presumption of guilt without sufficient established facts. The HC statement that rings were recovered based on the accused's statement was deemed not completely correct.
precedents_cited_list: The King vs. Abeywickrema 44NLR 254 | Abeysekera v Attorney General [1981] 1SriLR 376 | Chiraveddi Munayya v. Emperor (21 MLJ) (1071)(3) | Chenga Reddy and Ors. v. State of A.P. (1996) 10 SCC 193
standard_of_review_applied: Proof beyond reasonable doubt in circumstantial evidence cases.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Section 40(1)(a) of the Industrial Disputes Act
brief_facts_summary: The Assistant Commissioner of Labour instituted criminal proceedings against Ceylon Oxygen Limited and its directors for non-compliance with an arbitral award dated 29.03.2005. The prosecution was filed on 21.05.2010, after the Respondent had repudiated the award, causing it to cease effect on 20.05.2006. The Magistrate's Court upheld a preliminary objection that it lacked jurisdiction as the award was no longer in force when proceedings commenced.
hc_judgment_summary: The Provincial High Court dismissed the revision application, affirming the Magistrate's order. The High Court held that the repudiation of the award prevented the enforcement of obligations via criminal sanctions under the Industrial Disputes Act, as the award had ceased to have effect prior to the institution of proceedings.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Petitioner-Appellant argued that the Magistrate's Court had jurisdiction despite the repudiation. They contended that repudiation only has prospective application and does not extinguish criminal liability for non-compliance during the period the award was in force. They also relied on Section 456 of the Code of Criminal Procedure regarding prescription.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Set aside the judgment of the learned Provincial High Court Judge dated 20.07.2017 | Set aside the order of the Additional Magistrate in MC Case No. 55549/5 dated 10.07.2014 | Order/direct the learned Additional Magistrate to take up for trial the Complaint and the Charge Sheet
gnd_other_description: Effect of repudiation of arbitral award on criminal liability
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The court did not analyze witness evidence as the case was determined on a preliminary objection of law regarding the jurisdiction of the court and the validity of the charge sheet in light of the award's repudiation. The analysis focused on the statutory interpretation of the Industrial Disputes Act.
defence_version_summary: The Respondent argued that the Magistrate Court lacked jurisdiction to hear the matter because the arbitral award had been validly repudiated and ceased to have effect on 20.05.2006, years before the criminal prosecution was instituted in 2010.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal held that criminal prosecution under Section 40(1)(a) of the Industrial Disputes Act cannot be maintained if the arbitral award is no longer in operation. The court noted that while Section 20 of the Act allows for prosecution during the period an award is in force, in this case, the prosecution was instituted after the award had validly ceased to have effect due to repudiation. The court distinguished previous case law, clarifying that while civil rights might accrue, the criminal offence requires a subsisting award. Therefore, no charge sheet could be served.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The Petitioner-Union appealed against the High Court's affirmation of a Magistrate's Court order discharging Ceylon Oxygen Limited from charges of non-compliance with an arbitral award. The Respondent had repudiated the award, which ceased to exist in 2006, while prosecution was filed in 2010. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal, ruling that criminal liability for non-compliance under the Industrial Disputes Act cannot be enforced after the award has ceased to have effect via valid repudiation. The court distinguished between accrued civil rights and criminal enforceability.
defence_counsel: Kamran Aziz with Ershan Anaratnam, Chandana Sri Nissanka
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court correctly held that the repudiation of the award prevents the enforcement of obligations under the award by imposing criminal sanctions. It noted that while accrued rights are not wiped out, the penal provisions cannot be invoked after the award ceases to effect.
precedents_cited_list: Elmo Rex Lord and Another, Partners, Mercantile Printers and Stationers v Eksath Kamkaru Samithiya 2001 1 SLR 161 | K. Ram Banda v, The River Valleys Development Board 71 NLR 25 | Walker Sons & Co., Ltd v F. C. W. Fry 68 NLR 73 | Somasunderam Vanniasingham v Forbes and Another 1993 2 SLR 369 | Thirunawakarasu v Siriwardena and others 1981 1 SLR 185 | Kulatunga, Labour Officer, Colombo South v Dodangoda and Others 1987 (1) SLR 1
standard_of_review_applied: Legal interpretation of statutory provisions

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna J. | R. Gurusinghe J.
offence_sections: Section 364(2)(e) of the Penal Code as amended by Act No 22 of 1995
brief_facts_summary: The accused-appellant, the paramour of the victim's mother, was indicted for raping the victim (under 16) on two occasions between 2007 and 2008. The victim, 12 years old at the time, lived with her mother and the accused in a house provided by a tile factory. The victim revealed the abuse to her sister later, leading to a police complaint and medical examination which confirmed repeated sexual intercourse.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 364(2)(e) of the Penal Code as amended by Act No 22 of 1995
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the accused on two counts of rape based on the victim's testimony and medical evidence. The Judge sentenced him to 15 years rigorous imprisonment for each count to run concurrently and ordered compensation.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the trial judge failed to consider weaknesses and omissions in the victim's evidence and the possibility of false implication due to animosity. They also raised issues regarding the consistency of medical evidence and the delay in lodging the first complaint.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The learned Trial Judge had not considered the weaknesses of PW2's evidence. | The learned Trial Judge had not considered properly the possibility of a false allegation despite there being evidence of previous animosity. | The learned Trial Judge had failed to consider the subsequent conduct of the accused-appellant, which is a relevant fact. | The learned Trial Judge had not considered the inconsistencies in medical evidence.
gnd_other_description: Failure to consider subsequent conduct (surrendering to police)
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court found the victim (PW 02) to be a credible witness despite minor omissions and delay, which were explained by the accused's influence. The medical evidence (PW 06) corroborated the history of repeated abuse, contradicting the defence's denial.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court held the prosecution proved the case beyond reasonable doubt, rejecting the defence of false implication due to animosity.
defence_version_summary: The accused pleaded not guilty and claimed false implication due to animosity from the victim's family regarding his relationship with the victim's mother. He also pointed out that another person (Indunil) had sex with the victim, attempting to shift blame.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal affirmed the High Court's findings, noting that the trial judge had the benefit of observing witness demeanour. The Court accepted the explanation for the delay in the complaint (accused's influence). It held that minor omissions did not affect the root of the case. The defence of false implication was rejected, and the fact that another person may have also abused the victim did not absolve the appellant of his statutory liability.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused-appellant appealed a conviction for two counts of statutory rape committed against a 12-year-old girl. The High Court had sentenced him to 15 years rigorous imprisonment and ordered compensation. The appellant argued that the conviction was based on contradictory evidence and motivated by family animosity. The Court of Appeal dismissed these grounds, finding the victim's testimony credible and corroborated by medical evidence, and ruled that minor omissions did not undermine the prosecution's case. The conviction and sentence were affirmed.
hc_analysis_summary: The COA found the HC Judge meticulously analyzed evidence, correctly handled omissions, and properly evaluated the defence of animosity.
precedents_cited_list: Bandara vs. The State 2001 (2) SLR 63 | Bharwada Bhoginbhai Hirjibhai vs State Of Gujar 1983 AIR 753, 1983 SCR (3) 280 | Talpe Liyanage Manatunga vs Attorney General CA/47/98
standard_of_review_applied: Review of evidence evaluation and witness credibility findings by the trial judge.

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna, J. | R. Gurusinghe, J.
offence_sections: Section 436 of the Penal Code for trespass and Section 364 of the Penal Code for rape
brief_facts_summary: The appellant was accused of trespassing and raping the prosecutrix (PW1) twice in her home during the night. PW1 claimed the rapist switched off the lights, lit a lighter to show his face, and identified himself as Sugath. PW1 stated she had seen the appellant before when he came with Jagath to pluck coconuts. The prosecutrix suffered from chronic schizophrenia with delusions and hallucinations and lacked capacity to consent to sex according to medical reports.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 436 of the Penal Code for trespass and Section 364 of the Penal Code for rape
hc_sentence_type: Ten years rigorous imprisonment for Count 1 (trespass) and twenty years rigorous imprisonment for Count 2 (rape), with fines and default imprisonment terms for both counts
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the appellant on both counts of trespass and rape. For the first count, the appellant was sentenced to ten years rigorous imprisonment and a fine of Rs. 25,000 with a default term of one year rigorous imprisonment. For the second count, he received twenty years rigorous imprisonment and a fine of Rs. 25,000 with a default term of one year rigorous imprisonment, plus Rs. 500,000 compensation to the prosecutrix with a default term of two years rigorous imprisonment.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant challenged the conviction on identification grounds, arguing there were serious issues with the prosecutrix's identification of him. He contended the trial judge failed to apply probability tests to prosecution evidence, failed to consider the motive for false implication, and failed to properly evaluate defence evidence. The appellant also challenged the sentence as excessive.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The Learned Trial Judge has failed to take into consideration the fact that there is a serious issue with regard to the identification of the appellant | The Learned Trial Judge has failed to apply the test of probability and improbability to the prosecution witnesses | The Learned Trial Judge has failed to consider the motive behind falsely implicating the appellant | The Learned Trial Judge has failed to apply the law and legal principles relating to defence evidence when convicting the appellant | The Learned Trial Judge has imposed an excessive sentence on the appellant
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found serious problems with the prosecutrix's identification evidence. PW1 failed to mention in her police statement that she had seen the appellant before the incident. The evidence about the rapist lighting a lighter to show his face was deemed improbable. The investigating officer found no fingerprints or scientific evidence to implicate the appellant, and evidence showed the appellant was improperly shown to PW1 at the police station before the identification parade.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court found the prosecutrix's evidence improbable, particularly regarding the rapist switching off lights to conceal identity but then lighting a lighter to reveal his face. The Court also found the evidence about preparing tea for the rapist unreliable given her statement that she never cooks. The omission of key facts from the police statement raised doubts about identification.
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty and gave evidence under oath. He denied committing the rape and claimed he was shown to PW1 at the police station before the identification parade. He stated he was assaulted by police and forced to admit guilt, which he told the examining doctor. He had been in custody since arrest in December 2014 and was unable to furnish Rs. 5,000 bail. The Court found his version credible regarding being shown to the prosecutrix at the police station.
legal_errors_description: The High Court Judge asked leading questions during re-examination, assuming facts not in evidence (that lights were switched on). The Judge failed to properly evaluate the improbability of the prosecutrix's evidence. The Judge did not adequately consider the tainted identification parade where the appellant was shown to the prosecutrix at the police station beforehand.
procedural_defects_description: The appellant was shown to the prosecutrix at the police station before the identification parade, rendering the parade almost worthless. The appellant was not represented by a lawyer at the time of the identification parade. Leading questions were improperly asked during examination of PW1.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found critical flaws in the identification evidence. The prosecutrix failed to mention in her police statement that she had seen the appellant before, which the Court deemed a vital omission. The Court found it improbable that someone who switched off lights to conceal identity would use a lighter to reveal his face. Evidence established the appellant was improperly shown to PW1 at the police station, which tainted the subsequent identification parade. The investigating officer found no fingerprints or scientific evidence linking the appellant to the crime. The Court noted contradictions in the prosecutrix's evidence, including regarding preparing tea. The medical report revealed the prosecutrix had chronic schizophrenia with delusions and hallucinations. With the identification parade evidence excluded due to the improper showing at the police station, only dock identification remained, which the Court found worthless and insufficient. The Court concluded the identity of the perpetrator had not been established beyond reasonable doubt.
coa_sentence_type: Conviction and sentence set aside; appellant acquitted
brief_judgment_file_summary: Walimuni Arachilage Mendis was convicted by the High Court of Chilaw for trespass and rape, sentenced to 10 and 20 years rigorous imprisonment respectively, with Rs. 50,000 in total fines and Rs. 500,000 compensation. The appellant appealed primarily on grounds of mistaken identity. The Court of Appeal found serious defects in the identification evidence, including vital omissions in the prosecutrix's police statement, improbable testimony about the rapist revealing his identity, and most critically, evidence that the appellant was improperly shown to the prosecutrix at the police station before the identification parade. With no forensic evidence and the identification parade tainted, the Court found only dock identification remained, which was deemed insufficient. The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal, set aside the conviction and sentence, and acquitted the appellant.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court convicted the appellant based primarily on the prosecutrix's identification and testimony. However, the Court of Appeal found the High Court Judge improperly asked leading questions assuming facts not in evidence and failed to properly evaluate the improbability of key prosecution evidence. The High Court also failed to adequately consider the tainted identification procedure where the appellant was shown to the prosecutrix at the police station before the formal identification parade.
precedents_cited_list: Munirathne and others v. The State [2001] 2 Sri L.R.382 | R vs. Howick | Regina vs. Turnbull & Another | Gunaratne Banda vs. The Republic S.C. 132-136/76 H.C. Kegalle 79/75 SCM 2.3.1978
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the identity of the perpetrator was established beyond reasonable doubt, examining the reliability and credibility of identification evidence and whether the conviction was safe

judges: Sampath B Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 364(1) of the Penal Code as amended by Penal Code (Amendment) Act number 22 of 1995 and Section 314 of the Penal Code
location_of_offence: Between Thoppuwa junction and Chilaw area
brief_facts_summary: The appellant forcibly abducted the prosecutrix from a bus at Thoppuwa junction on 15th July 2013, dragged her into a three-wheeler, and took her to a guest house near Chilaw where he raped her three times and assaulted her. The prosecutrix reported the incident to her mother and police the same day and was admitted to hospital. Medical examination revealed injuries consistent with assault and forcible sexual intercourse.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 364(1) of the Penal Code as amended by Penal Code (Amendment) Act number 22 of 1995 and Section 314 of the Penal Code
hc_sentence_type: 20 years rigorous imprisonment on count one and 01 year rigorous imprisonment on count two, with fines and compensation
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court found the appellant guilty on both counts after trial. The HC imposed 20 years rigorous imprisonment for rape and 1 year for causing simple hurt, along with fines totaling Rs. 51,000 and compensation of Rs. 700,000 to the victim, finding the prosecution proved the case beyond reasonable doubt.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant challenged the conviction arguing that the prosecution failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt, the HC Judge unreasonably rejected defence evidence, and failed to consider matters favorable to the appellant including the defence of consensual sexual relationship. The appellant also argued that the HC Judge shifted the burden of proof to him.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The prosecution failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt against the appellant and hence, the conviction is bad in law | The learned High Court Judge rejected the defence evidence unreasonably | The learned High Court Judge failed to consider matters favourable to the appellant in the judgment
gnd_other_description: Defence of consent not properly considered; HC Judge failed to consider matters favorable to appellant
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The prosecutrix gave detailed evidence about the forcible abduction and rape. The JMO's evidence strongly corroborated her account with findings of assault injuries and vaginal injuries consistent with forcible intercourse. The guest house owner's evidence contradicted the prosecutrix but was rejected by the trial judge.
probability_assessment_by_court: Court found prosecution proved case beyond reasonable doubt based on direct testimony of victim corroborated by medical evidence and prompt complaint
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty and gave evidence under oath. He admitted having an illicit affair with the prosecutrix and claimed the sexual intercourse was consensual. He called two witnesses to support his version. The court rejected this defence finding the victim's evidence credible and corroborated by medical evidence.
legal_errors_description: The HC Judge misdirected by concluding that the appellant failed to substantiate his defence by not calling additional evidence, thereby improperly shifting the burden of proof to the accused
procedural_defects_description: The HC Judge's procedure of remanding prosecution witness PW-02 for allegedly giving false evidence was highly unwarranted and irregular, as no application was made to treat him as adverse witness during evidence
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found that despite misdirection on burden of proof, the conviction was safe. The COA held that the prosecutrix's evidence was credible and corroborated by medical evidence showing injuries consistent with forcible intercourse. The appellant's admitted conduct of forcibly removing her from the bus and assaulting her publicly contradicted the consent defence. The COA applied the proviso to Article 138 finding no substantial prejudice or failure of justice despite the legal error. However, the COA found the 20-year sentence excessive given mitigating circumstances and reduced it to 15 years.
coa_sentence_type: 15 years rigorous imprisonment on count one and 01 year rigorous imprisonment on count two (concurrent), with fines and compensation
brief_judgment_file_summary: Samarappulige Neville Titus Fernando was convicted by the High Court of Chilaw for raping Nirosha Subashini Fernando on 15th July 2013 and causing simple hurt, sentenced to 20 years imprisonment, fines totaling Rs. 51,000 and Rs. 700,000 compensation. He appealed arguing the prosecution failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt and that the sexual relationship was consensual. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal against conviction finding the victim's evidence credible and corroborated by medical evidence, but reduced the sentence to 15 years imprisonment considering mitigating factors.
defence_counsel: Anil Silva, P.C. with Nandana Perera and Mark Anton
hc_analysis_summary: The HC Judge properly considered the prosecutrix's evidence and medical corroboration, correctly finding consent was not established. However, the HC misdirected on burden of proof and irregularly remanded PW-02. The COA found these errors did not cause substantial prejudice but the HC failed to consider mitigating circumstances in sentencing.
precedents_cited_list: Inoka Gallage Vs. Kamal Addararachchi and another (2002) 1 SLR 307 | Rao Harnarian Vs. The State of Punjab 1958 AIR 123 | State of Punjab Vs. Gurmeet Singh and others (1996) 2 SCC 384 | Pantis Vs. The Attorney General (1998) 2 SLR 148 | Lafeer Vs. Queen 74 NLR 246 | De Silva and Others Vs. The Attorney General (2010) 2 SLR 169 | Chaminda Vs. The Republic (2009) 1 SLR 144
standard_of_review_applied: Appellate court will not lightly disturb findings of trial judge who observed witnesses' demeanor unless evidence is totally inconsistent or perverse; applied proviso to Article 138 of Constitution regarding errors that do not prejudice substantial rights
sentencing_principles_applied: The COA considered mitigating circumstances including the fact that appellant had no previous convictions and found the maximum 20-year sentence was not warranted, reducing it to 15 years rigorous imprisonment

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Penal Code and Offences Against Public Property Act No 12 of 1982 (as amended)
brief_facts_summary: The petitioner along with 5 other accused were indicted for conspiracy to commit criminal misappropriation of Rs. 1,090,000 out of funds belonging to the Fisheries Harbour Corporation. The petitioner was Chairman of the Corporation and allegations involved ordering and purchasing 2000 Diaries violating tender procedures. The indictment followed an investigation by the Criminal Investigation Department pursuant to a Commission of Inquiry appointed by the President in 2015.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Penal Code and Offences Against Public Property Act No 12 of 1982 (as amended)
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court delivered an order dated 23.07.2018 overruling the petitioner's objection to computer evidence. The HC directed parties to come into agreement to facilitate access to proposed computer evidence and utilized discretion under Section 7(1)(d) of the Evidence (Special) Provisions Act No. 14 of 1995 to facilitate inspection.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner raised an objection to prevent the leading of computer evidence proposed by the prosecution. The petitioner contended that the prosecution's non-compliance with the 15-day time period stipulated in Section 7(1)(c) of the Evidence (Special) Provisions Act violated mandatory requirements and frustrated the legislative intention, thus the prosecution should be estopped from relying on such evidence at trial.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Non-compliance with 15-day time period under Section 7(1)(c) of Evidence (Special) Provisions Act No. 14 of 1995 | Violation of mandatory requirement for providing access to computer evidence | Frustration of legislative intention by failing to grant timely access to proposed evidence
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The proposed computer evidence consisted of printouts of documents including quotations involved in the alleged conspiracy for criminal misappropriation with signatures. The prosecution noted these documents were available to the petitioner during the Commission of Inquiry, indicating no prejudice was caused by the access issue.
defence_version_summary: The petitioner refused the prosecution's offer on 29.09.2018 to inspect the proposed computer evidence, relying on the previous objection that non-compliance with Section 7(1)(c) rendered any attempt to permit access nugatory. The petitioner was unwilling to entertain inspection of the evidence prior to trial commencement.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal held that Section 7(1)(c) provisions are directory rather than mandatory despite the use of 'shall'. The COA examined the combined effect of Sections 7(1)(a), (b), (c) and (d) of the Evidence (Special) Provisions Act and found the High Court properly exercised discretion under Section 7(1)(d) to facilitate access. The Court emphasized that facts cannot be divorced from law and the administration of justice is not a game for clever tactics but a serious proceeding to discover actual facts for public safety. The COA rejected invocation of Section 7(2) to shut out evidence, noting crimes of this nature have grave impact on public interest, and directed the HC to stipulate a clear timeframe for facilitating inspection before trial commencement.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This is a revision application by the 5th accused Upali Liyanage challenging a High Court order dated 23.07.2018 that overruled his objection to computer evidence in a criminal misappropriation case involving Rs. 1,090,000 from Fisheries Harbour Corporation funds. The petitioner argued the prosecution violated mandatory 15-day time limits under the Evidence (Special) Provisions Act for providing access to proposed computer evidence. The Court of Appeal dismissed the application, holding that Section 7(1)(c) time limits are directory not mandatory, the High Court properly exercised discretion under Section 7(1)(d), and shutting out evidence on technical grounds would frustrate justice in matters of grave public interest. The COA directed the HC to stipulate a clear timeframe for inspection before trial.
defence_counsel: Sanjeewa Jayawardana PC with R. C. Meepage
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court rejected the strict interpretation of the 15-day time bar in Section 7(1)(c) and recognized practical difficulties in implementation. The HC refused to shut out evidence based on technical non-compliance and directed parties to reach agreement on modalities of access, utilizing discretion under Section 7(1)(d) to facilitate inspection in the interests of justice.

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna J. | R. Gurusinghe J.
offence_sections: Sections 140, 296, 300 and 426 of the Penal Code read with section 146 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: On 05.01.2015 during Presidential Election campaign, an unlawful assembly led by political figures vandalized UNP decorations at Kahawaththa. A shooting incident occurred during the altercation between opposing political groups, resulting in the death of Dodamgodage Susil Perera alias Shantha Dodamgoda and injuries to two others. Three accused-appellants were convicted for being members of an unlawful assembly that committed murder and attempted murder.
evidence_type_secondary: Medical evidence, CCTV footage, Physical evidence (empty cartridges), Telephone records
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Sections 140, 296, 300 and 426 of the Penal Code read with section 146 of the Penal Code
hc_sentence_type: Death sentence for Count 3 (murder), 20 years rigorous imprisonment for Counts 4 and 5 (attempted murder), 3 years rigorous imprisonment for Count 2 (mischief), 6 months rigorous imprisonment for Count 1 (unlawful assembly), with fines and compensation orders
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted all three accused-appellants for Counts 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 based on vicarious liability under section 146 of the Penal Code. The court found them guilty of being members of an unlawful assembly that committed murder and attempted murders. Death sentences were imposed for murder, along with rigorous imprisonment for other charges, fines totaling Rs.40,000 per accused, and compensation totaling Rs.400,000 per accused to victims' families.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The accused-appellants challenged the conviction on multiple grounds including: failure to consider discrepancies in prosecution witnesses' evidence, failure to treat eyewitnesses as interested witnesses, inadequate evaluation of CCTV evidence, failure to prove elements of unlawful assembly, improper rejection of defence evidence, misdirection on common object, shifting burden of proof to defence, procedural irregularities, and failure to apply appropriate legal tests for credibility and probability.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The learned trial Judge failed to consider inbuilt discrepancies in evidence of PW 1 and PW 3 | The learned trial Judge failed to consider that PW 1 was an interested witness | The learned trial Judge failed to consider discrepancies regarding the 1st accused's vehicle | The learned trial Judge failed to consider ingredients required to prove charge 2 | The learned trial Judge failed to consider evidence establishing accused were not together at time of shooting | The learned trial Judge failed to consider that PW 3 was an investigator and interested witness | The learned trial Judge failed to consider ingredients of common object charge | The learned trial Judge failed to evaluate vicarious liability properly | The learned trial Judge wrongly rejected defence evidence | The learned trial Judge misdirected on common object of unlawful assembly | The learned trial Judge erred in imputing vicarious liability under section 146 | The prosecution failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt | The learned High Court Judge failed to evaluate prosecution evidence applying relevant tests | The learned High Court Judge failed to consider absence of conclusive identity of productions | The learned High Court Judge failed to consider contradictions inter se and per se | The learned High Court Judge failed to consider main elements of unlawful assembly charge | The learned High Court Judge misdirected in fact and law | The learned High Court Judge failed to address authenticity of CCTV productions | The learned High Court Judge used impermissible evidence against co-accused | The learned High Court Judge relied on suspicious circumstances | The learned High Court Judge failed to consider procedural irregularities | Judgment not in compliance with Section 283 of Code of Criminal Procedure | The learned High Court Judge shifted burden of proof to defence | The learned High Court Judge misdirected in analysing dock statement | The learned High Court Judge acted on surmise and conjecture | Section 114(f) presumption should be applied against prosecution | The appellant was not afforded a fair trial
gnd_other_description: Shifting burden of proof to defence, use of impermissible evidence against co-accused, reliance on surmise and conjecture, improper evaluation of dock statements
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The prosecution called 23 witnesses including four eyewitnesses (PW 1, PW 3, PW 5, PW 6). The Court of Appeal found significant discrepancies between PW 1 and PW 3 who claimed to be at the same location but did not see each other. The court noted that evidence was not evaluated properly considering tests of probability, consistency, and interestedness. Defence called two witnesses who were rejected by trial judge as interested witnesses.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal held that the trial judge failed to apply tests of probability and improbability. The appellate court found it improbable that PW 1 and PW 3 were at same narrow location taking cover but did not see each other. The court found reasonable doubt regarding whether accused-appellants knew shooting would occur and whether they were members of unlawful assembly with requisite knowledge.
defence_version_summary: All three accused-appellants pleaded not guilty. The 1st accused denied presence at the scene. The 2nd and 3rd accused admitted presence but denied the shooting occurred at the time claimed by prosecution. They made dock statements. The 3rd accused called two defence witnesses including his driver Sirisena and security officer Kelum Janaka. The defence contended they went to inquire about removal of their propaganda materials and left before shooting occurred. The trial court rejected defence evidence as coming from interested witnesses.
legal_errors_description: The Court of Appeal found that the trial judge failed to properly evaluate evidence using established tests of credibility, probability, consistency, and interestedness. The trial judge failed to properly analyze material contradictions and omissions. The trial judge improperly rejected dock statements by comparing them with prosecution evidence rather than evaluating if they raised reasonable doubt. The trial judge failed to properly establish elements of vicarious liability under section 146 including knowledge and common object. The trial judge shifted burden of proof to defence.
procedural_defects_description: The judgment was found not in compliance with Section 283 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. Key witness SI Sampath who was present at scene was not called by prosecution. The trial judge acted on surmise and conjecture regarding leadership of unlawful assembly without proper evidence. The trial judge used impermissible evidence against co-accused.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal conducted extensive analysis and found multiple fatal flaws in the High Court's judgment. The appellate court held that material contradictions between PW 1 and PW 3 (who allegedly took cover at same narrow location but never saw each other) were wrongly dismissed. The court found PW 1 was an interested witness due to political rivalry and subsequent political appointment but this was not properly considered. The prosecution failed to prove beyond reasonable doubt that accused-appellants were members of an unlawful assembly with knowledge that murder would likely occur. No evidence established that accused-appellants held weapons or fired shots. The shooter was never identified. The trial judge improperly rejected dock statements and shifted burden of proof to defence. The court applied principles from Woolmington vs DPP, holding prosecution must stand on its own legs and cannot derive strength from weakness in defence. The court found prosecution failed to prove vicarious liability under section 146 as there was no evidence of overt acts by accused-appellants toward prosecution of common object. The court cited Martin Fernando vs Inspector of Police holding that appellate court must test evidence both intrinsically and extrinsically, and where close examination raises strong doubt, accused should get benefit of doubt. The court concluded prosecution failed to prove case beyond reasonable doubt.
coa_sentence_type: Acquittal and discharge from all charges
brief_judgment_file_summary: Three accused-appellants were indicted in Ratnapura High Court for murder, attempted murder, mischief, and unlawful assembly arising from a political violence incident on 05.01.2015 during Presidential Election campaign at Kahawaththa. The High Court convicted all three for Counts 1-5 based on vicarious liability under section 146, imposing death sentences for murder, 20 years imprisonment for attempted murders, fines totaling Rs.40,000 each, and compensation of Rs.400,000 each. The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal after finding the prosecution failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt, noting material contradictions in eyewitness testimony, failure to identify the shooter, improper evaluation of interested witnesses, failure to establish elements of vicarious liability, improper rejection of dock statements, and shifting of burden of proof. The convictions were quashed and all three accused-appellants were acquitted and discharged from all charges.
prosecution_counsel: Dilan Rathnayaka SDSG with Shaminda Wickrema SC
defence_counsel: Anuja Premaratna PC with Iromie Jayarathna AAL, Naushalya Rajapaksha AAL, Imasha Senadheera AAL and Nayana Dissanayaka AAL for 1st accused; U.R. de Silva PC with Harshana Wijenayaka AAL, Kasun Liyanage AAL and Savithri Fernando AAL for 2nd accused; Nalin Ladduwahetty PC with Niran Ankitel AAL, Hafeel Faris AAL, Amila Palliyage AAL, Vajira Ranasinghe AAL, Thusitha Ranasinghe AAL, Buddhika Chandrasekara AAL, Shalika Gunawardana AAL, Kavithri Ubeysekara AAL and Yasas Hewapathirana AAL for 3rd accused; Saliya Pieris PC with Amila Egodamahawatta AAL for aggrieved party
weapon_type: Firearm (not recovered - illegal T-56 rifle based on cartridge analysis)
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court analyzed the evidence and concluded that the accused-appellants were members of an unlawful assembly led by the 3rd accused, who as a Deputy Minister and political leader knew or ought to have known that violence including shooting was likely to occur. The trial judge found the testimony of PW 1 and PW 3 credible and corroborated by PW 5, PW 6, medical evidence, CCTV footage, and recovery of cartridges. However, the Court of Appeal found the High Court failed to properly analyze material contradictions, failed to consider interestedness of witnesses, improperly evaluated defence evidence, acted on surmise regarding leadership and common object, failed to establish elements of vicarious liability, and shifted burden of proof to defence.

standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal applied the standard that it is not absolved from testing evidence extrinsically and intrinsically despite weight given to trial judge's observations of demeanour. Where close examination raises strong doubt as to guilt, accused should get benefit of doubt. Appellate court will not lightly disturb findings on credibility unless manifestly wrong, but must intervene where there is miscarriage of justice.

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 364(2) of the Penal Code as amended
brief_facts_summary: The victim, a 14-year-old girl living with her father, was raped by the Appellant in March 2010. The Appellant arrived at her house requesting an air pump, and when she went inside to get it, he surreptitiously entered, caught her by her hand, pinned her against a trestle, and had sexual intercourse with her. The victim did not disclose the incident due to threats from the Appellant, but it came to light when the Appellant's wife mentioned it to the victim's father. A formal complaint was lodged at Tanamalwila Police Station on 20 August 2010.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 364(2) of the Penal Code as amended
hc_sentence_type: 15 years of rigorous imprisonment with a fine of Rs.10,000/- in default of which simple imprisonment for 01 year, plus Rs.200,000/- compensation to the victim with a default sentence of 02 years simple imprisonment
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the Appellant as charged under Section 364(2) of the Penal Code. The court was satisfied that there was a case to be answered and called for the defence. The Appellant made a dock statement admitting the victim was a frequent visitor but claimed his wife had assaulted the victim after finding a letter, and he was falsely implicated.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Appellant raised three main grounds of appeal. First, the date of the offence had not been proved by the prosecution. Second, the prosecution witnesses failed the test of probability and credibility. Third, the rejection of the case for the defence was on unreasonable grounds.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Date of the offence had not been proved by the prosecution | Prosecution witnesses fail the test of probability and credibility | Rejection of the case for the defence on unreasonable grounds
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The victim gave evidence without any contradiction, and her testimony passed the test of probability and credibility. PW02, the father, corroborated the disclosure and explained the delay in reporting. PW6, the JMO, confirmed vaginal penetration corroborating the victim's history. The Court of Appeal found the victim's evidence convincing despite minor discrepancies in the medico-legal report regarding the exact date and location details.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal held that the victim's evidence passed the test of probability and credibility. The court found no contradiction or omission in the victim's trial testimony and accepted her explanation for the delay in reporting due to the threat from the Appellant.
defence_version_summary: The Appellant pleaded not guilty and made a dock statement. He did not call any witnesses. In his dock statement, he admitted the victim was a frequent visitor to his house but claimed his wife had assaulted the victim after finding a letter from another person (Panikiriya), and he was falsely implicated. The court rejected the defence version and found the prosecution case credible.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal carefully examined all three grounds of appeal. On the first ground regarding the date of the offence, the court held that the victim correctly disclosed the month and year within the indictment period, and there was no prejudice to the Appellant. The court cited precedents stating that exact dates are not material unless essential to the offence. On the second ground regarding credibility, the court found the victim's evidence convincing and cogent despite minor discrepancies in the medico-legal report. The court applied the principle that hearsay history in medico-legal reports is not admissible and does not undervalue the victim's direct testimony. The court also found the delay in reporting was adequately explained. On the third ground, the court held that the High Court Judge had properly considered and rejected the defence version with valid reasons. The Court of Appeal emphasized that rape is a grave offence and sexual assault on children must be dealt with severely. Finding no merit in any ground of appeal, the court affirmed the conviction and sentence.
coa_sentence_type: 15 years of rigorous imprisonment with a fine of Rs.10,000/- in default of which simple imprisonment for 01 year, plus Rs.200,000/- compensation to the victim with a default sentence of 02 years simple imprisonment
brief_judgment_file_summary: The Appellant was convicted by the High Court of Monaragala for rape under Section 364(2) of the Penal Code and sentenced to 15 years rigorous imprisonment, a fine of Rs.10,000/-, and Rs.200,000/- compensation to the victim. The victim, who was 14 years old at the time of the incident in March 2010, testified that the Appellant raped her in her house. The appeal raised three grounds: the date of offence was not proved, prosecution witnesses lacked credibility, and the defence was unreasonably rejected. The Court of Appeal dismissed all grounds, finding the victim's evidence convincing and cogent, the delay in reporting adequately explained, and no prejudice from the date variance. The conviction and sentence were affirmed.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court Judge found that there was a case to be answered after evaluating the prosecution evidence. The judge called for the defence and explained the Appellant's rights. After hearing the dock statement, the judge accepted the victim's evidence as true and cogent, properly analyzing the delay in making the complaint. The Court of Appeal found the High Court Judge had correctly applied the test of probability and credibility and had given valid reasons for rejecting the defence version.
precedents_cited_list: Sunil and Another v. The Attorney General [1986] 1 Sri. L. R. 230 | Bhoginbhai Hirjibhai v. State of Gujarat [1983] AIR 753 | R. v. Dossai 13 Cr.App.R. 158 | Gamini Dolawatte V. Attorney General [1988] 1 Sri. L. R 221 | Wickremasuriya v. Dedoleena and others 1996 [2] SLR 95 | Iswari Prasad v. Mohamed Isa 1963 AIR (SC) 1728
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the appeal grounds had merit by examining the evidence, applying tests of probability and credibility, and assessing whether the High Court Judge properly analyzed the prosecution and defence cases
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court of Appeal stated that rape is one of the gravest forms of sexual offences that violates and degrades a fellow human being. The court emphasized that society cannot condone sexual assault on children and that courts have a positive obligation to protect children from sexual abuse. Sexual offenders must be dealt with severely.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Poisons Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance as amended by Act No 13 of 1984 under section 54 A or B
brief_facts_summary: On 8.4.2016 the petitioner was arrested by the Police Narcotics Bureau for being in possession of 198.84 grams of heroin. The petitioner was indicted and indictment was served on 10.11.2017. Trial commenced on 13.11.2018 but proceeded with significant delays due to prosecution lack of preparedness and unavoidable circumstances including COVID-19 lockdown.
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court refused bail on 18.3.2021 which is the impugned order being challenged in this revision application. The trial judge had previously refused bail on 4.9.2020 and again on 17.12.2020 on the ground that the prosecution had informed court of changes to their witness list.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner sought revision to set aside the High Court order dated 18.3.2021 refusing bail. The main argument was that the petitioner had been in remand for nearly 7 years due to delays in the trial proceedings caused by lack of prosecution preparedness, violating the fundamental right to liberty.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Delay in trial proceedings | Petitioner detained in remand for nearly 7 years without trial conclusion | Lack of prosecution preparedness causing numerous adjournments | Violation of fundamental right to liberty under the Constitution
gnd_other_description: Application for bail based on excessive delay and constitutional right to liberty
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The trial had commenced with PW1 whose evidence concluded on 25.2.2019. Thereafter multiple adjournments occurred due to prosecution not leading PW2 despite his presence, PW3 not being present or being detained by CID for questioning, and missing documents. The prosecution eventually informed court they would not be leading PW2, 3, and 6.
defence_version_summary: The petitioner filed a revision application seeking bail based on exceptional circumstances arising from the excessive delay of nearly 7 years in remand without trial conclusion. No details of plea or defence on merits are mentioned as this was a bail application during ongoing trial.
legal_errors_description: The High Court erred in refusing bail despite the excessive and oppressive delay of 7 years in remand without trial conclusion, which violated the petitioner's fundamental right to liberty under Article 13 of the Constitution.
procedural_defects_description: The High Court proceedings showed lack of preparation by prosecution on numerous occasions, causing multiple adjournments on dates 7.6.2019, 19.9.2019, and 10.12.2019. Witnesses were not led despite being present, documents were not in court, and witnesses were involved in other duties.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal considered the law on bail under section 83 of the Poisons Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance which requires exceptional grounds for bail in cases under section 54A or B. The Court acknowledged the serious nature of drug offences citing Carder vs. OIC Narcotics Bureau 2006 3 SLR 74, but held that this does not justify keeping persons in remand for uncertain lengthy periods. The Court found that the High Court proceedings clearly indicated lack of prosecution preparation on numerous occasions, some unavoidable and some due to sheer lack of preparedness. The Court held that the petitioner's detention for nearly 7 years without trial conclusion constituted 'excessive and oppressive' delay, citing precedent. The Court emphasized that Article 13(4) of the Constitution enshrines that persons under arrest should not be detained pending trial without justifiable reason, and cited Sumanadasa Vs. Attorney General (Sri Lanka Law reports 2006 pg 202) regarding infringement of fundamental rights through prolonged detention. The Court concluded that the delay constituted exceptional circumstances warranting bail.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This is a revision application by Noberst Padmanadam alias Robert challenging the High Court Negombo order dated 18.3.2021 refusing bail. The petitioner was arrested on 8.4.2016 for possession of 198.84 grams of heroin and charged under the Poisons Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance. The trial commenced in 2018 but suffered significant delays due to prosecution lack of preparedness and unavoidable circumstances. The Court of Appeal held that the petitioner's detention for nearly 7 years without trial conclusion constituted excessive and oppressive delay amounting to exceptional circumstances and violation of constitutional rights to liberty. The Court allowed the revision application, set aside the High Court order, and directed that the petitioner be enlarged on suitable bail conditions.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court refused bail on multiple occasions based on the serious nature of the charge and that trial had already commenced. The Court of Appeal criticized the High Court for failing to consider that the delay of nearly 7 years constituted exceptional circumstances warranting bail despite the serious nature of the offence.
precedents_cited_list: Carder vs. OIC Narcotics Bureau 2006 3 SLR 74 | Abdul Hamidkari Path and etc vs. the State of Gujarat and other 15 476 | Sumanadasa Vs. Attorney General (Sri Lanka Law reports 2006 pg 202) | CA/PHC/APN68/2021
standard_of_review_applied: Revision under Article 138 of the Constitution read with section 404 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act No 15 of 1979

judges: Sampath B Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code and Section 4(2) of the Offensive Weapons Act No 18 of 1966
brief_facts_summary: On 13th February 1999, the appellant allegedly threw a bomb into a house where five people were sleeping. The bomb exploded causing the death of Ranpatidevage Podihamy alias Somawathi and injuries to four others. The only eyewitness, Punchadevage Sunethra (PW-02), testified that after hearing a window being opened and something rolling on the floor, she switched on the light and saw two persons running away from the window, identifying one as the appellant based on body features (dark, tall, and fat). The appellant was arrested on 14th February before the eyewitness gave her statement to police on the 17th.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code and Section 4(2) of the Offensive Weapons Act No 18 of 1966
hc_sentence_type: Death on count 1; 10 years rigorous imprisonment on each of counts 2-5 (concurrent); Fine of Rs. 10,000 on each of counts 2-5 with 3 months imprisonment in default on each count
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court found the appellant guilty as charged on all five counts. The appellant was sentenced to death for murder and to 10 years rigorous imprisonment on each of the four counts under the Offensive Weapons Act, to run concurrently. The High Court concluded that the eyewitness PW-02 clearly identified the appellant before, during, and after the bomb was thrown.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant challenged the conviction on two main grounds: (1) The High Court Judge failed to give adequate consideration to whether there was evidence to prove beyond reasonable doubt the identity of the appellant; (2) The High Court Judge considered several inaccurate facts as evidence when there was no such evidence, resulting in a miscarriage of justice.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The learned High Court Judge failed to give adequate consideration to the fact that whether there was evidence to prove beyond reasonable doubt, the identity of the appellant | The learned High Court Judge has considered several inaccurate facts as evidence, when there was no such evidence, which has resulted in a miscarriage of justice towards the appellant
gnd_other_description: High Court Judge misapprehended the evidence and reached factually wrong conclusions about when and how the identification occurred
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The only eyewitness PW-02 testified that she saw two persons running away from the window after the bomb was thrown, identifying one based on body features (dark, tall, fat) from behind. The Court of Appeal found that the High Court misapprehended her evidence by concluding she clearly identified the appellant before and during the bomb throwing, when she only saw persons running away after the incident.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal found that there was insufficient evidence to establish identity beyond reasonable doubt, noting the witness only saw the appellant from behind while running away and was not well known to the witness
defence_version_summary: The defence contended that the appellant's name was given to police only on suspicion because the sister of the eyewitness had an affair with a person who works in the Army. The eyewitness denied this and stated that the appellant had no association with her family and she only knew him from seeing him in the area. The Court found the identification highly unreliable.
legal_errors_description: The High Court Judge committed a total misdirection as to the evidence by concluding that the eyewitness clearly identified the appellant before, during, and after the bomb was thrown, when the evidence showed she only saw persons running away from behind after the explosion. The judge failed to consider whether identity was sufficiently established under the circumstances and did not address factors such as distance, light availability, and time of observation required for proper identification.
procedural_defects_description: The prosecution failed to call PW-01 (the husband of the eyewitness) who was the first person to whom the identification was allegedly communicated and who provided first information to police. This failure was fatal to the credibility of the eyewitness, especially since the appellant was arrested before the eyewitness gave her statement to police.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found that the High Court Judge fundamentally misapprehended the eyewitness evidence by concluding that PW-02 clearly identified the appellant before, during, and after the bomb was thrown. In fact, the witness only saw two persons running away from behind after the explosion, identifying one based solely on body features. The COA held that the High Court failed to consider critical factors for identification evidence such as distance, light availability, duration of observation, and the fact that the appellant was not well known to the witness. The COA applied principles from Regina v. Turnbul regarding the special need for caution in identification cases and noted that even honest witnesses can be mistaken. The prosecution's failure to call PW-01 (the husband who received first information) was found to be a fatal error that undermined the credibility of the eyewitness, especially since the appellant was arrested before the eyewitness gave her statement. The COA emphasized that the prosecution must prove its case beyond reasonable doubt regardless of the strength of the defence case. Finding the conviction unsafe, the COA set aside the conviction and sentence and acquitted the appellant.
coa_sentence_type: Conviction and sentence set aside; appellant acquitted
brief_judgment_file_summary: The appellant Mapa Mudiyanselage Sisira Kumara Bogamuwa was convicted by the High Court of Kurunegala for murder and four counts under the Offensive Weapons Act for throwing a bomb into a house on 13th February 1999, killing one person and injuring four others. He was sentenced to death for murder and 10 years rigorous imprisonment with fines totaling Rs. 40,000 on the other counts. The appellant appealed on grounds that the High Court failed to properly assess identification evidence and misapprehended facts. The Court of Appeal found that the High Court Judge fundamentally misdirected herself by concluding the eyewitness clearly identified the appellant when she only saw persons running away from behind after the explosion. The COA held that the identification was unreliable, the prosecution failed to call a crucial witness (PW-01), and the conviction was unsafe. The appeal was allowed, and the appellant was acquitted.
defence_counsel: U. R. De Silva, P.C. with Savithri Fernando
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court concluded that the eyewitness PW-02 clearly identified the appellant before, during, and after the bomb was thrown. The Court of Appeal found this to be a fundamental misdirection, as the evidence showed the witness only saw persons running away from behind after the explosion, identifying one based solely on body features without seeing the face.
precedents_cited_list: Regina Vs. Turnbul and Another (1997) QB 224 | R. Vs. Wood and Brown [Ann-Reg. 1784]
standard_of_review_applied: Appellate review of identification evidence and whether conviction is safe; assessment of whether High Court properly directed itself on evidence

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna, J. | R. Gurusinghe, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 and 300 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: On March 19, 2006, the appellant broke into his son's house at night where his wife (PW1), daughter-in-law D.M. Damayanthi Senehelatha, and three grandchildren were sleeping. The appellant attacked his wife and daughter-in-law with a knife with a long handle. The daughter-in-law was killed and his wife sustained grievous head injuries. A knife marked as P1 was recovered from an irrigation canal following information provided by the appellant.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 and 300 of the Penal Code
hc_sentence_type: Death for first count (murder), five years rigorous imprisonment for second count (attempted murder)
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Polonnaruwa found the appellant guilty of murder of D.M. Damayanthi Senehelatha and attempted murder of M.G. Anulawathie. The court sentenced the appellant to death for the murder charge and five years rigorous imprisonment for the attempted murder charge.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the conviction based on voice identification alone was unsafe. He contended that PW1 was improperly held to be a hostile witness without a formal application under section 154. He claimed PW4 was threatened by the prosecutor during testimony. He also argued that the trial judge erred in holding that PW1's evidence was corroborated by the short history given to the doctor.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The Learned Trial Judge erred in law by failing to consider, that it is unsafe to convict the appellant by relying on the voice identification | The Learned High Court Judge has erred in holding that PW1 was a hostile witness to the prosecution, where the prosecution had not made any such application in terms of section 154 of the Evidence Ordinance | PW4 was threatened by the prosecutor when she was giving evidence | The Learned Trial Judge erred in holding that the evidence of PW1 was corroborated by the short history given to the doctor
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: PW2 (seven-year-old grandson) identified the appellant by voice while sleeping in a dimly lit room. PW1 (wife) did not identify the attacker due to poor vision. PW4 (daughter) testified that the appellant admitted to attacking her mother. Medical evidence from PW9 corroborated the injuries sustained by PW1 and linked them to the weapon P1.
