probability_assessment_by_court: The court held that voice identification by a close relative (grandson) who was intimately familiar with the appellant's voice, combined with the recovery of the weapon from a unique location shown by the appellant and the extra-judicial confession to PW4, established guilt beyond reasonable doubt.
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty. He gave a very short dock statement denying the allegations against him. He did not call any witnesses and did not present an alibi. The court rejected the dock statement as a mere denial that did not create reasonable doubt in the prosecution case.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal held that voice identification cannot be excluded as unsafe when the accused is intimately known to the witness. The court noted that PW2 had frequent opportunity to be familiar with his grandfather's voice. The court emphasized that voice identification was not the only evidence, citing PW4's testimony about the extra-judicial confession and the recovery of the weapon from a unique location shown by the appellant. The court held that the injuries on the deceased could have been caused by weapon P1 and that the evidence was legally admissible. The court rejected arguments about PW1 being improperly treated as hostile, finding no procedural error. The court found no evidence that PW4 was threatened and noted her testimony was consistent. The court held that the High Court Judge properly rejected the dock statement as a mere denial.
coa_sentence_type: Death for first count (murder), five years rigorous imprisonment for second count (attempted murder)
brief_judgment_file_summary: Lagamuwathanna Viyannalage Premadasa was convicted by the High Court of Polonnaruwa for the murder of his daughter-in-law D.M. Damayanthi Senehelatha and attempted murder of his wife M.G. Anulawathie on March 19, 2006, and sentenced to death and five years rigorous imprisonment respectively. On appeal, the appellant challenged the conviction primarily on the basis that voice identification by his seven-year-old grandson was unsafe, and raised procedural objections about witness treatment. The Court of Appeal held that voice identification by an intimate family member familiar with the appellant's voice was reliable, especially when corroborated by an extra-judicial confession to his daughter and recovery of the murder weapon from a location shown by the appellant. The appeal was dismissed.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court properly used only legally admissible evidence to convict the appellant. The court correctly excluded PW1's evidence against the appellant on the murder charge under section 120 of the Evidence Ordinance but used her evidence for the attempted murder count. The Court of Appeal found the High Court's treatment of the dock statement and evaluation of witness credibility to be appropriate.
precedents_cited_list: Dalbir Singh vs State Of Haryana decided on 15 May 2008 | State of Rajasthan v. Raja Ram [(2003) 8 SCC 180] | E.R.S.R. Coomaraswamy's book 'The Law of Evidence' Volume 1 page 257
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the High Court Judge used only legally admissible evidence to convict the appellant and whether the conviction was safe based on the evidence presented.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Tobacco Tax Act No 8 of 1999 (as amended)
brief_facts_summary: The petitioner was accused in Magistrate's Court Case No 20370/10 for dealing with 3000 packets of counterfeit John Player Gold Leaf cigarettes under the Tobacco Tax Act. The petitioner pleaded guilty at the first instance. The Magistrate imposed a fine of Rs. 25,000 with a default sentence of 3 months Rigorous Imprisonment.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Tobacco Tax Act No 8 of 1999 (as amended)
hc_sentence_type: 2 years Rigorous Imprisonment suspended for ten years and fine of Rs. 1 million with default sentence of 1 year Rigorous Imprisonment
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court enhanced the sentence on appeal filed by Ceylon Tobacco Company PLC. The High Court sentenced the petitioner to 2 years Rigorous Imprisonment suspended for ten years and imposed the maximum fine of Rs. 1 million with a default sentence of 1 year Rigorous Imprisonment. The petitioner was not represented during oral arguments of the appeal before the High Court.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner challenged the High Court order dated 31.01.2014 which enhanced the sentence imposed by the Magistrate. The primary contention was whether the impugned order was plagued with any irregularity amounting to an exceptionality that warrants the invocation of the revisionary jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal. The petitioner argued that the High Court erred in imposing both a fine and a term of imprisonment.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The High Court order dated 31.01.2014 is irregular and warrants invocation of revisionary jurisdiction | The High Court erred in imposing both a fine and a term of imprisonment when Section 15(1) of the Tobacco Tax Act only allows for either a fine or imprisonment
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: This was a revision application concerning sentencing rather than a trial on merits. The petitioner pleaded guilty at the Magistrate's Court. No witness evidence was discussed in the judgment.
defence_version_summary: The petitioner pleaded guilty at the Magistrate's Court. The petitioner was not represented during oral arguments of the appeal before the High Court. The petitioner filed a revision application challenging the High Court's enhancement of the sentence.
legal_errors_description: The High Court erred in imposing both a fine and a term of imprisonment when Section 15(1) of the Tobacco Tax Act clearly stipulates either a fine or imprisonment, not both. This was a grave misconstruction of the plain and unambiguous stipulations of law.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal analyzed Section 15(1) of the Tobacco Tax Act No 8 of 1999 as amended, which stipulates that a person guilty of an offence shall be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years. The Court held that the plain intention of the legislature was clear - the statute envisions either a fine or imprisonment, not both. The Court emphasized that when legislative language is plain and definitive, courts are bound to enforce it. The High Court's imposition of both a fine and imprisonment was held to be a grave misconstruction of the law. The Court found no irregularity in the Magistrate's original sentence of Rs. 25,000 fine, noting that the Magistrate exercised discretion appropriately after the petitioner pleaded guilty at the first instance. The Court cited Attorney General and Others v Sumathipala (2006) 2 SLR 126 and Maxwell on the Interpretation of Statutes to support the principle of plain meaning interpretation.
coa_sentence_type: Fine of Rs. 25,000 with default sentence of 3 months Rigorous Imprisonment
brief_judgment_file_summary: This was a revision application by Nizar Mohammed Nizarthir against the High Court order dated 31.01.2014 which enhanced his sentence. The petitioner was originally convicted in the Magistrate's Court for dealing with 3000 packets of counterfeit cigarettes under the Tobacco Tax Act, pleaded guilty, and was fined Rs. 25,000. Ceylon Tobacco Company PLC appealed, and the High Court enhanced the sentence to 2 years Rigorous Imprisonment suspended for ten years plus a fine of Rs. 1 million. The Court of Appeal held that Section 15(1) of the Tobacco Tax Act allows for either a fine or imprisonment, not both, and that the High Court gravely misconstrued the law. The Court set aside the High Court judgment and affirmed the Magistrate's original sentence of Rs. 25,000 fine.
defence_counsel: L. M. K. Arulanandane, PC with L. Kulatunge and Meth Perera
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court enhanced the sentence on appeal by Ceylon Tobacco Company PLC, imposing both 2 years Rigorous Imprisonment suspended for ten years and a fine of Rs. 1 million. The Court of Appeal found that the High Court gravely misconstrued Section 15(1) of the Tobacco Tax Act by imposing both a fine and imprisonment when the statute clearly provides for either one or the other. The High Court's interpretation was held to be absurd and contrary to common sense.
precedents_cited_list: Attorney General and Others v Sumathipala (2006) 2 SLR 126 | Maxwell on the Interpretation of Statutes (12th Edition at page 28)
standard_of_review_applied: Revisionary jurisdiction to determine whether the impugned order is plagued with any irregularity amounting to an exceptionality that warrants the invocation of the revisionary jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court held that Section 15 of the Tobacco Tax Act entrusts courts with broad discretion to impose either imprisonment or a fine, and such discretion must not be arbitrary or fanciful but sound and guided in law. A party cannot insist on the imposition of the maximum penalty unless compelling reasons are provided. The Court emphasized plain meaning interpretation of statutory provisions.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
brief_facts_summary: The deceased with two witnesses consumed arrack at a boutique in the evening. After an initial irritation over cigarettes with some of the accused, the deceased restarted an argument when encountering accused again. An exchange of fisticuffs developed into a brawl where the 1st accused stabbed the deceased and the 7th and 9th accused came with clubs, resulting in the deceased sustaining 7 injuries including a fatal stab wound to the chest.
hc_sentence_type: Count 1: 6 months RI and Rs 5000 fine, in default 3 months imprisonment. Count 2: 10 years RI and Rs 10000 fine, in default 6 months imprisonment with compensation.
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court found all petitioners guilty for both the 1st and 2nd charges in the indictment relating to murder and unlawful assembly to commit murder. The High Court sentenced the petitioners to 6 months RI and Rs 5000 fine for the first count, and 10 years RI and Rs 10000 fine for the second count with compensation.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioners challenged only the sentence, not the conviction. Defence counsel argued that the incident took place without premeditation, without motive, and occurred after consumption of liquor. The defence contended that the sentence was excessive given these circumstances.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The incident had taken place without premeditation | The incident is without motive | The incident is after an act of consuming liquor
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The evidence showed the 1st accused stabbed the deceased and the 7th and 9th accused came to the scene with clubs. The deceased sustained 7 injuries with the fatal blow to the chest classified as necessarily fatal by the doctor. The Court observed that both parties agreed the incident commenced on the instigation of the deceased.
defence_version_summary: The petitioners pleaded not guilty to the indictment and proceeded to trial. Defence argued the incident occurred without premeditation or motive, and after liquor consumption. No specific alibi was mentioned. The Court found the deceased was the main contributory factor to the incident.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal considered the submissions from both parties regarding sentence reduction. The Court noted the petitioners challenged only the sentence through revision rather than appeal, but accepted their reasons given no obvious delay. The COA observed that both parties agreed the incident commenced on the instigation of the deceased, who had consumed liquor and restarted the argument. The prosecution countered that the deceased was isolated, assaulted, and stabbed while fallen. The COA found that given the deceased was the main contributory factor to the incident, it was fair to vary the sentence for the second charge from 10 years to 5 years RI, while maintaining the rest of the sentence including compensation. The COA cited the precedent of Kumara vs. Attorney General 2003 1 SLR 139 on sentence reduction.
coa_sentence_type: Count 1: 6 months RI and Rs 5000 fine, in default 3 months imprisonment. Count 2: 5 years RI and Rs 10000 fine, in default 6 months imprisonment with compensation.
brief_judgment_file_summary: Four accused-petitioners (1st, 7th, 9th and 10th accused) out of 12 accused applied for revision of their sentence for murder and unlawful assembly to commit murder. The High Court Gampaha had convicted them and sentenced them to 6 months RI with Rs 5000 fine for count 1, and 10 years RI with Rs 10000 fine plus compensation for count 2. The petitioners challenged only the sentence, arguing the incident was without premeditation or motive and occurred after liquor consumption. The Court of Appeal found the deceased was the main contributory factor having instigated the incident, and reduced the sentence for count 2 from 10 years to 5 years RI while maintaining other aspects of the sentence. The revision application was dismissed subject to this variation.
defence_counsel: Shehan De Silva with Hemal Senevirathna
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court had sentenced the petitioners to the maximum sentence of 10 years RI on the basis of knowledge. The COA did not examine the legality of the High Court's conclusion or reasons as only the sentence was being challenged. The COA accepted the conviction but found the sentence excessive given the deceased's contributory role.
precedents_cited_list: Kumara vs. Attorney General 2003 1 SLR 139
standard_of_review_applied: Revision application under Article 138 of the Constitution
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court considered the contributory conduct of the deceased as a mitigating factor in sentencing. The Court noted the incident commenced on the instigation of the deceased and this warranted a reduction from the maximum statutory sentence.

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna J. | R. Gurusinghe J.
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The accused-appellant Kankanam Tantrilage Antony alias Baba, brother-in-law of the deceased Ushettige Aladin Peter Appuhami, stabbed the deceased with a knife on 13.04.2000. The incident occurred after an altercation in the afternoon when the deceased's son accidentally knocked down a kerosene oil cooker being repaired by the appellant. The eyewitness Niluka Damayanthi saw the appellant stab the deceased, and the knife was recovered based on the appellant's statement. The JMO found two stab injuries on the deceased's back, one of which pierced the lung and was necessarily fatal.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Chilaw found the accused-appellant guilty of murder under Section 296 of the Penal Code and sentenced him to death. The trial judge found the prosecution proved its case beyond reasonable doubt based on eyewitness testimony, dying declaration, recovery of the knife, and medical evidence. The trial judge rejected the appellant's belated claim that his statement was obtained through police assault.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant challenged his conviction on the basis that the trial judge convicted him based on uncorroborated and inconsistent evidence, failed to apply the test of probability and improbability, did not apply law and legal principles correctly, and improperly relied on his failure to disclose that police obtained his statement by compulsion after assault. The appellant argued the trial judge erred in viewing his belated complaint of police torture as unreliable.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The learned trial Judge has convicted the appellant based on uncorroborated and inconsistent evidence of the prosecution witnesses | The learned trial Judge has failed to apply the test of probability and improbability to the prosecution witnesses and defence evidence in convicting the appellant | The learned trial Judge has not applied the law and legal principles in convicting the Appellant | As per the learned trial Judge, one of the grounds for the conviction of the appellant is his failure to disclose that the police officers had obtained the statement from him by compulsion after being assaulted by the police | Failure on the part of the appellant to explain the failure to disclose that the statement given to the police was obtained from him through compulsion
gnd_other_description: Challenge to voluntariness of statement to police claiming it was obtained through torture and assault
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found the prosecution evidence strong and consistent. The eyewitness Niluka Damayanthi's testimony was credible and not contradicted in cross-examination. The dying declaration made by the deceased to his son corroborated the eyewitness account, and the knife recovered on the appellant's statement was compatible with the injuries observed by the JMO.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal upheld the trial judge's finding that prosecution witnesses met the test of probability and promptness, their evidence was convincing and consistent, and the prosecution proved its case beyond reasonable doubt
defence_version_summary: The accused-appellant pleaded not guilty and gave evidence on oath denying any involvement with the murder of the deceased. He claimed for the first time at trial that his statement to police was not voluntary and that he was assaulted by police after surrendering on 14.04.2000. The court found his evidence improbable and rejected his belated complaint of police torture made 16 years after the alleged incident.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal thoroughly examined the appellant's grounds of appeal and found them without merit. The COA noted that the trial judge properly considered the eyewitness testimony of Niluka Damayanthi, the dying declaration made to Pradeep Nandana, the fact that defense failed to mark any contradictions or omissions in the eyewitness evidence, the consistency and corroboration of prosecution evidence, the recovery of the knife compatible with the injuries, and the improbability of the appellant's evidence. The COA rejected the appellant's reliance on SC FR Application No. 244/2010 regarding belated complaints of police torture, distinguishing the facts and noting the appellant's complaint was made 16 years after the alleged incident with no prior complaint to the Magistrate or during non-summary inquiry. The COA found the trial judge correctly applied all legal principles and properly concluded the prosecution proved its case beyond reasonable doubt.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused-appellant Kankanam Tantrilage Antony alias Baba was convicted by the High Court of Chilaw for murdering his brother-in-law Ushettige Aladin Peter Appuhami on 13.04.2000 and sentenced to death under Section 296 of the Penal Code. The appellant stabbed the deceased with a knife following an afternoon altercation. The appellant appealed claiming conviction was based on uncorroborated inconsistent evidence, improper application of legal principles, and that the trial judge erred in not accepting his claim that police obtained his statement through torture. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal, finding strong eyewitness testimony, a reliable dying declaration, recovery of the murder weapon, and consistent prosecution evidence proved the case beyond reasonable doubt. The COA affirmed both the conviction and death sentence.
cause_of_death_exact_text: Two stab injuries on the back of the deceased. One on the left has pierced the lung and that has been identified as a necessarily fatal injury
hc_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found the High Court trial judge properly analyzed all evidence, correctly applied legal principles, and properly found the prosecution proved its case beyond reasonable doubt. The COA rejected all grounds of appeal and found no errors in the High Court's reasoning or conclusions.
precedents_cited_list: SC FR Application No. 244/2010 | Sudath Silva Vs Kodithuwakku 1987 (2) SLR 119
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the trial judge properly applied legal principles, correctly evaluated evidence for consistency and probability, and whether the prosecution proved its case beyond reasonable doubt

judges: Neil Iddawala, J. | Menaka Wijesundera, J.
offence_sections: Section 443 and Section 369 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The petitioner, a 62-year-old man, was sentenced for a string of house breaking at night and theft offenses. He pleaded guilty to charges under Section 443 and Section 369 of the Penal Code in multiple cases before the Magistrate Courts of Gangodawila and Kaduwela. He has been incarcerated since 2006 and has close to 29 convictions. The combined effect of consecutive sentences means he will remain imprisoned until 2041.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 443 and Section 369 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Colombo dismissed the revision applications filed by the petitioner without issuing notices to the respondent. Three dismissals were due to undue delay of 9 years and one dismissal was due to delay of 5 years. The High Court stated that sentences in different cases cannot be combined and must run separately.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner sought revision of sentencing orders arguing that considering the totality of circumstances, the operation of the four impugned sentences should be suspended. The main ground was based on the petitioner's age (62 years) and the prospect of continued incarceration for the next two decades until 2041.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The totality of circumstances warrants suspension of the operation of sentences | The petitioner's age of 62 years and continued incarceration until 2041 constitute exceptional circumstances
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: No witness evidence analysis was present in this case as the petitioner had pleaded guilty to all charges in the Magistrate Court proceedings.
probability_assessment_by_court: Not applicable as petitioner pleaded guilty
defence_version_summary: The petitioner pleaded guilty to all charges. No alibi was raised. The defence counsel submitted only mitigatory facts concerning the petitioner's age of 62 years and the prospect of continued incarceration for the next two decades. No witnesses were called by the defence.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal examined whether it had the power to grant relief based on the combined effect of multiple consecutive sentences under Section 300 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act. The Court noted that Section 300 mandates that when a person undergoing imprisonment is sentenced to imprisonment in another case, the latter sentence shall commence at the expiration of the previous sentence, with no discretion for concurrent sentences. The Court distinguished the case from Weerawarnakula v The Republic of Sri Lanka where relief was granted because all offences were part of a single transaction. In the instant case, the petitioner is a habitual offender with close to 29 convictions for offences committed on different occasions by different courts. The Court held there is no discretion available to provide relief based on the combined effect of a multiplicity of convictions, especially when offences were not committed in a single transaction and sentences were given by different courts on different occasions. The appeals were dismissed.
coa_sentence_type: Rigorous Imprisonment (original Magistrate Court sentences upheld)
brief_judgment_file_summary: W.D. Marks Wanigasekera, a 62-year-old habitual offender with close to 29 convictions, appealed four High Court orders dismissing his revision applications challenging Magistrate Court sentences for house breaking and theft under Sections 443 and 369 of the Penal Code. The High Court had dismissed his applications due to delays of 5 to 9 years. The petitioner pleaded guilty in all cases and has been incarcerated since 2006, with consecutive sentences extending his imprisonment until 2041. He sought relief based on his age and the totality of circumstances. The Court of Appeal held that Section 300 of the Criminal Procedure Code mandates consecutive sentences with no discretion for concurrent running, and distinguished the case from precedents involving single transactions. The Court dismissed all appeals, finding no discretion to provide relief for a habitual offender whose convictions arose from separate offences tried by different courts.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court dismissed the revision applications without issuing notices to the respondent due to undue delays. The High Court stated that sentences in different cases cannot be combined and that law requires sentences in different cases to run separately. The Court of Appeal found no error in the High Court's approach.
precedents_cited_list: Weerawarnakula v The Republic of Sri Lanka (2002) 3 SLR 213 | Godagama v Mathea (1908) 4 ACR VII
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal examined whether it could exercise its powers under Section 336 of the Criminal Procedure Code to substitute lesser sentences in revision jurisdiction
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court applied Section 300 of the Criminal Procedure Code which mandates consecutive sentences when a person undergoing imprisonment is sentenced in another case. The Court considered the principle that relief may be granted in exceptional circumstances where offences form part of a single transaction, but held this principle inapplicable to habitual offenders convicted of separate offences by different courts.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Section 443 and Section 369 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The petitioner pleaded guilty to charges under Section 443 and Section 369 of the Penal Code for a string of house breaking at night and theft. He is a 62-year-old habitual offender with close to 29 convictions. He has been incarcerated since 2006 and sentenced in four different Magistrate Court cases on different dates between 2007 and 2010.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 443 and Section 369 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Colombo dismissed four revision applications filed by the petitioner on 18.11.2015 without issuing notices to the respondent. Three dismissals were occasioned by undue delay of 9 years and the fourth dismissal was due to a delay of 5 years. The High Court stated that sentences in different cases cannot be combined and that law requires sentences in different cases should run separately.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner filed four applications in appeal impugning four different orders delivered by the High Court of Colombo that dismissed his revision applications. The counsel for the petitioner submitted that considering the totality of the circumstances and the combined effect of all convictions resulting in incarceration till 2041, the operation of the four impugned sentences should be suspended. The main ground was the excessive combined sentence considering the petitioner's age of 62 years.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The operation of the four impugned sentences should be suspended considering the totality of the circumstances | The petitioner's age (62 years old) and the prospect of continued incarceration for the next two decades warrant relief
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: This was a guilty plea case with no witness evidence analysis. The petitioner pleaded guilty to all charges under Section 443 and Section 369 of the Penal Code in four separate Magistrate Court proceedings.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal noted there is no discretion available to provide relief based on the combined effect of a multiplicity of convictions, especially given that the offences were not committed in the course of a single transaction.
defence_version_summary: The petitioner pleaded guilty to all charges. The counsel for the petitioner did not present any mitigatory facts other than the age of the petitioner (62 years) and the prospect of continued incarceration for the next two decades till 2041.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal examined whether it had the power to grant relief based on the combined effect of multiple convictions under Section 300 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act. The Court distinguished the case from Weerawarnakula v The Republic of Sri Lanka where relief was granted because all offences were part of a single transaction. In contrast, the petitioner was a habitual offender with close to 29 convictions for offences committed separately and sentenced by different Courts on different occasions. The Court found that Section 300 of the CPC operates imperatively requiring sentences in different cases to run consecutively. The Court held there is no discretion available to provide relief based on the combined effect of a multiplicity of convictions where offences were not committed in the course of a single transaction. The respondent's contention that the Court of Appeal is not a court of mercy and that there was no valid legal reasoning for relief was accepted.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The petitioner, W.D. Marks Wanigasekera, a 62-year-old habitual offender with 29 convictions, filed four appeals challenging the High Court of Colombo's dismissal of his revision applications against sentences imposed by Magistrate Courts for house breaking and theft under Sections 443 and 369 of the Penal Code. The petitioner had pleaded guilty and been incarcerated since 2006 with sentences running consecutively until 2041 under Section 300 of the Criminal Procedure Code. The Court of Appeal examined whether it could grant relief based on the combined effect of multiple convictions and distinguished the case from precedent where offences were part of a single transaction. Finding the petitioner to be a habitual offender whose offences were committed separately and sentenced by different courts on different occasions, the Court held there was no discretion to provide relief and dismissed all appeals.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court of Colombo dismissed the revision applications without issuing notices to the respondent due to undue delays of 5 to 9 years. The High Court stated that sentences in different cases cannot be combined and that law requires sentences in different cases should run separately. The Court of Appeal found no error in this approach and upheld the High Court's position.
precedents_cited_list: Weerawarnakula v The Republic of Sri Lanka (2002) 3 SLR 213 | Godagama v Mathea (1908) 4 ACR VII
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal sitting in revision examined whether it is empowered to grant relief under Section 336 of the CPC to substitute lesser sentences based on the circumstances.
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court applied Section 300 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act which mandates that when a person undergoing imprisonment is sentenced to imprisonment, such imprisonment shall commence at the expiration of the imprisonment to which he has been previously sentenced. The Court noted this provision is couched in imperative terms with no discretion for concurrent sentences across different cases. The principle that relief may be granted where offences are part of a single transaction was distinguished from cases involving habitual offenders with separate offences.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Animal's Act 1957 of No.29
brief_facts_summary: A lorry bearing number SPCL-3081 transported 12 heads of cattle without a valid permit. The lorry was taken into custody by police and charged in the Magistrate's Court. The two accused pleaded guilty and were convicted. The claimant petitioner sought to claim the cattle but failed to prove ownership.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Animal's Act 1957 of No.29
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court affirmed the Magistrate's Court order dated 07.07.2015. The High Court upheld the confiscation of the cattle on the basis that the petitioner had not successfully proved ownership of the animals.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The application for revision was filed to set aside the order of the High Court dated 15.03.2017 and the order of the Magistrate's Court dated 07.07.2015. The petitioner challenged the confiscation of cattle and sought their release.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Set aside High Court order dated 15.03.2017 | Set aside Magistrate's Court order dated 07.07.2015 | Release of confiscated cattle to the claimant
gnd_other_description: Failure to prove ownership of animals and knowledge of offence
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Magistrate considered the evidence and concluded that the evidence describing the cattle does not tally with the description of the cattle produced in Court. There were discrepancies in the evidence led in favour of the claimant which failed to prove ownership.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Magistrate assessed that the petitioner failed to establish ownership and that the offence was committed without his knowledge as required by Section 3AA(3) of the Animal's Act
defence_version_summary: The two accused pleaded guilty and were convicted. The claimant petitioner attempted to establish ownership of the cattle through evidence but the court found discrepancies in the evidence and descriptions that did not tally with the cattle produced in court.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal reviewed the application for revision under Article 154 of the Constitution. The court examined the provisions of Section 3AA(3) of the Animal's Act which requires the claimant to establish both ownership of the animal and that the offence was committed without his knowledge or connivance. The court noted that the Magistrate properly considered the evidence and found that the description of the cattle by the claimant did not tally with the cattle produced in court, and there were discrepancies in the evidence. The High Court had affirmed the Magistrate's order. The Court of Appeal found no exceptional circumstance which shocks the conscience of the court and therefore dismissed the application for revision.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This is a revision application challenging the confiscation of 12 heads of cattle transported without a valid permit. The two accused who transported the cattle pleaded guilty in the Magistrate's Court and were convicted under the Animal's Act 1957. The claimant petitioner sought release of the cattle but failed to prove ownership. The Magistrate found discrepancies in the claimant's evidence and confiscated the cattle. The High Court affirmed this order. The Court of Appeal dismissed the revision application finding no exceptional circumstances.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court affirmed the Magistrate's Court order confiscating the cattle. The Court of Appeal found that the High Court correctly upheld the Magistrate's findings that the claimant failed to establish ownership and that there was no evidence the offence was committed without his knowledge.
standard_of_review_applied: Revision jurisdiction under Article 154 of the Constitution and Act No 19 of 1990 - whether there are exceptional circumstances which shock the conscience of the court

judges: Sampath B Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 read with Section 32 of the Penal Code (Count 1); Section 300 read with Section 32 of the Penal Code (Count 2)
brief_facts_summary: On 25-01-2002, Chandralika Samaraweera was shot and killed at the Anuradhapura Court premises where a divorce case involving her son was being heard. Sumith Bodhinayake (her son) was also shot and injured with a firearm. A police officer (PW-08) on duty heard gunshots, saw a person with a pistol get into a cream-colored three-wheeler, and fired at the fleeing vehicle, breaking its windscreen. The first appellant was arrested 4 km away driving a three-wheeler with a broken windscreen and glass in his hair.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 read with Section 32 of the Penal Code (Count 1); Section 300 read with Section 32 of the Penal Code (Count 2)
hc_sentence_type: Death on count one and ten years rigorous imprisonment on count two in addition to fines
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted both appellants on murder charges under Section 296 read with Section 32 of the Penal Code and attempted murder under Section 300 read with Section 32. The HC sentenced them to death on the first count and ten years rigorous imprisonment on the second count plus fines. The HC Judge relied on identification parade evidence to establish identity of both accused despite witnesses' inability to identify them in court.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The main grounds of appeal were: (1) prosecution failed to establish identity of appellants beyond reasonable doubt; (2) identification parade notes were wrongly admitted as witnesses failed to correctly identify the accused; (3) common intention under Section 32 of the Penal Code was not proven. The defence argued that the substantive evidence showed witnesses could not identify the accused at the time of the incident.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The prosecution failed to establish the identity of the appellants beyond reasonable doubt and hence, the judgement is bad in law | Identification parade notes relied on by the prosecution were wrongly admitted as evidence, despite the witnesses failed to correctly identify the accused appellants | The common intention under in terms of Section 32 of the Penal Code was also not proven before the trial Court
gnd_other_description: Common intention under Section 32 not proven
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: PW-08 clearly testified he could not identify either the gunman or the driver of the three-wheeler. PW-01's deposition was admitted but he stated in his police statement that he was not close enough to identify the shooter. The identification parade notes showed PW-01 failed to identify the second appellant, instead pointing to the 8th person from left when the second accused stood 3rd from right.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal found that while there were serious suspicions against the first appellant (arrested driving three-wheeler with broken windscreen), suspicious circumstances do not establish guilt. The prosecution failed to connect the circumstantial evidence to prove involvement beyond reasonable doubt.
defence_version_summary: Both accused pleaded not guilty. The defence challenged the identification evidence, arguing that key witnesses could not identify the accused at the time of the incident. The defence successfully established contradictions showing PW-01 told police he could not identify the shooter. The Court found defence arguments credible and the Senior State Counsel conceded inability to support conviction.
legal_errors_description: The HC Judge was misdirected when he concluded identity was proved beyond reasonable doubt despite PW-08 testifying he did not see the first appellant under any circumstances. The HC Judge wrongly relied on identification parade evidence when the substantive trial evidence was that witnesses could not identify accused at time of incident. The HC also failed to properly consider infirmities in admitting deposition evidence without the witness being available for cross-examination.
procedural_defects_description: The identification parade conducted by the Acting Magistrate had procedural defects - he failed to mention the identified suspects by their names in the notes. The HC Judge also failed to properly consider the infirmities and limitations of admitting deposition evidence when the witness was not available for cross-examination at trial.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found fundamental errors in the HC's reliance on identification evidence. The COA emphasized that PW-08's substantive evidence was clear that he did not see the first appellant, making the identification parade evidence irrelevant. Similarly, PW-01 failed to identify the second appellant at the identification parade (identifying the 8th person from left when second accused stood 3rd from right). The COA cited The Queen Vs. Sumanasena, holding that suspicious circumstances do not establish guilt and the prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt. The COA found the HC Judge was misdirected on facts when assuming PW-01 identified the second appellant at the parade. With no proper identification of either accused, common intention under Section 32 could not be established. The Senior State Counsel conceded inability to support the conviction, which the COA appreciated.
coa_sentence_type: Acquittal - conviction and sentences set aside
brief_judgment_file_summary: Two accused appellants were convicted by the High Court of Anuradhapura for the murder of Chandralika Samaraweera and attempted murder of Sumith Bodhinayake on 25-01-2002 at court premises, and sentenced to death and ten years imprisonment respectively. The main appeal grounds challenged identification of the accused. The Court of Appeal found that key witness PW-08 clearly testified he could not identify either accused, and identification parade notes showed PW-01 failed to identify the second appellant. The COA held the HC Judge was misdirected in relying on identification parade evidence when substantive trial evidence showed no identification occurred. Citing precedent that suspicious circumstances do not establish guilt, and with the Senior State Counsel conceding the case, the COA allowed the appeals, set aside convictions and sentences, and acquitted both appellants on 29-03-2022.
defence_counsel: Neville Abeyratne, P.C. with Shanika Perera for the 1st Accused-Appellant; Ermaza Tegal with Thiagi Piyadasa and Mark Schobert for the 2nd Accused-Appellant
cause_of_death_exact_text: Death was due to several gunshot injuries
hc_analysis_summary: The HC Judge accepted identification parade evidence as establishing identity of both accused despite witnesses' inability to identify them in court. The HC reasoned that trial evidence is substantive and identification parades establish consistency. The COA found this reasoning fundamentally flawed because the substantive evidence was that witnesses never saw the accused during the incident, making identification parade evidence irrelevant and the HC's reliance on it a misdirection on facts.
precedents_cited_list: The Queen Vs. Sumanasena 66 NLR 350 | Athukoralage Nihal Perera and another Vs. Attorney General, CA case No. 43/44/2002 decided on 05-08-2005 | Ram Nath Mahto Vs. State of Bihar (1996) A.I.R Supreme Court (11) 2511 | King Vs. Assappu 50 NLR 324
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the High Court properly evaluated identification evidence and whether the prosecution proved guilt beyond reasonable doubt. The COA examined the totality of evidence and found the HC was misdirected on facts.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Section 443 and Section 369 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The petitioner pleaded guilty to charges under Section 443 and Section 369 of the Penal Code for house breaking at night and theft. He is a 62-year-old habitual offender who has been incarcerated since 2006 and has close to 29 convictions. The petitioner was sentenced in four different cases by the Magistrate Courts of Gangodawila and Kaduwela on different dates, with sentences running consecutively until 2041.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 443 and Section 369 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Colombo dismissed the revision applications filed by the petitioner on 18.11.2015 without issuing notices to the respondent. Three of the four dismissals were due to undue delay of 9 years and the fourth dismissal was due to a delay of 5 years. The learned High Court Judge stated that sentences in different cases cannot be combined and that law requires sentences in different cases should run separately.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner filed four applications in appeal impugning four different orders delivered by the High Court of Colombo. The counsel for the petitioner submitted that considering the totality of the circumstances, the operation of the four impugned sentences should be suspended. The counsel cited the petitioner's age and the prospect of continued incarceration for the next two decades as mitigating factors.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Sentences should be suspended considering the totality of circumstances | Age of the petitioner (62 years old) should be considered | Prospect of continued incarceration until 2041 is excessive
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: This was a guilty plea case and no evidence analysis was required. The petitioner pleaded guilty to charges in multiple cases before the Magistrate Courts. No witnesses were discussed in the judgment.
defence_version_summary: The petitioner pleaded guilty in all four cases. The defence counsel did not present any mitigatory facts other than the age of the petitioner (62 years old) and the prospect of continued incarceration for the next two decades. No witnesses were called by the defence.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal examined whether it had the power to grant relief to the petitioner based on the combined effect of multiple convictions and sentences. The Court discussed Section 300 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act, which mandates that sentences in different cases must run consecutively when a person already undergoing imprisonment is sentenced again. The Court distinguished the precedent of Weerawarnakula v The Republic of Sri Lanka, noting that case involved offences committed in a single transaction over two years, whereas the petitioner in this case is a habitual offender with close to 29 convictions committed on different occasions in different courts. The Court held that there is no discretion available to provide relief based on the combined effect of a multiplicity of convictions, especially when offences were not committed in the course of a single transaction and convictions were given by different courts on different occasions. The Court dismissed all four appeals.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The petitioner, W. D. Marks Wanigasekera, a 62-year-old habitual offender with close to 29 convictions, filed four applications in appeal challenging orders of the High Court of Colombo that dismissed his revision applications. The petitioner had pleaded guilty to charges under Section 443 and Section 369 of the Penal Code for house breaking at night and theft in multiple cases before Magistrate Courts of Gangodawila and Kaduwela. The High Court dismissed the revision applications due to delays of 5-9 years and held that sentences in different cases must run separately. The petitioner appealed on grounds that the combined sentences were excessive and sought suspension of sentences considering his age and prospect of incarceration until 2041. The Court of Appeal, per Neil Iddawala J. with Menaka Wijesundera J. concurring, dismissed all four appeals, holding that Section 300 of the Criminal Procedure Code mandates consecutive sentences and that there is no discretion to provide relief based on the combined effect of multiple convictions when offences were committed on different occasions and sentences were given by different courts, distinguishing the case from Weerawarnakula where offences were part of a single transaction.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court dismissed the revision applications without issuing notices to the respondent due to undue delays of 5-9 years. The High Court stated that sentences in different cases cannot be combined and that law requires sentences in different cases should run separately. The Court of Appeal found no error in the High Court's dismissal and upheld the principle that Section 300 of the CPC mandates consecutive sentences.
precedents_cited_list: Weerawarnakula v The Republic of Sri Lanka (2002) 3 SLR 213 | Godagama v Mathea (1908) 4 ACR VII
standard_of_review_applied: Revision jurisdiction under Section 336 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act to determine whether the Court of Appeal is empowered to grant relief based on the combined effect of multiple convictions
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court applied Section 300 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act, which mandates that when a person undergoing imprisonment is sentenced to imprisonment, such imprisonment shall commence at the expiration of the imprisonment to which he has been previously sentenced. The Court held that there is no discretion to order concurrent sentences when offences were committed on different occasions and sentences were given by different courts, distinguishing cases involving a single transaction.

judges: K. Priyantha Fernando, J. (P/CA) | Wickum A. Kaluarachchi, J.
offence_sections: Sections 54A(b) and (d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance
brief_facts_summary: PW2 PC30204 Bandara and Inspector Tennakoon (PW1) were on surveillance duty when they received information about heroin trafficking at 0805 hours. They met an informant near Nawaloka Hotel Peliyagoda who informed them that a person named Upali would be at Kandana Church Road Junction at 1000 hours. At about 1020 hours, the informant pointed out the appellant walking along Church Road, and when Inspector Tennakoon searched him, a parcel containing 3.5 grams of heroin was found in the front-right side pocket of his shorts.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Sections 54A(b) and (d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the appellant on both counts of trafficking and possessing 3.5 grams of heroin after trial. The appellant was sentenced to life imprisonment. The High Court found PW2 Bandara to be a credible witness and concluded that the appellant could be convicted based on his testimony despite lack of corroboration.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant challenged whether the learned trial Judge correctly evaluated the discrepancies in the prosecution case. The defence argued that the High Court Judge erred in concluding that PW2 Bandara was a credible witness and that conviction could be based on his uncorroborated testimony. The defence also contended that the trial Judge rejected the evidence of defence witnesses for no good reason.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Has the learned trial Judge correctly evaluated the discrepancies in the case of the prosecution? | Did the learned High Court Judge err in concluding that PW2 Bandara was a credible witness and that the accused appellant could be convicted based on his uncorroborated testimony?
gnd_other_description: Failure to corroborate single witness testimony and unfair rejection of defence evidence
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found critical inconsistencies in PW2's evidence regarding timing and location that were not explained. PW2 stated they were at the petrol station near Samantha Cinema Hall when receiving information at 0805 hours, but later testified they were waiting near Nawaloka Hotel from 0650 to 0800 hours. The prosecution failed to call other police officers who participated in the raid to corroborate PW2's testimony.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court found that the prosecution failed to prove the charges beyond reasonable doubt due to unexplained inconsistencies in sole witness testimony and failure to call corroborating witnesses
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty and made a dock statement. The defence version was that the appellant was arrested at his house, not on the road as claimed by the prosecution. Two witnesses gave sworn evidence: the appellant's wife (DW1) and a neighbour (DW2), both of whom testified to support the defence version. The Court of Appeal found that the trial Judge unfairly rejected the defence evidence on flimsy grounds.
legal_errors_description: The trial Judge failed to properly consider inconsistencies in PW2's evidence, unfairly rejected defence evidence on trivial grounds, and failed to apply the principle that when prosecution fails to call listed witnesses who could corroborate or explain inconsistencies, an adverse inference may be drawn
procedural_defects_description: The prosecution failed to call key witness PW1 who led the raid party and other police officers who participated in the raid and made notes, despite being listed in the indictment
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found that PW2's evidence contained unexplained inconsistencies about location and timing. The prosecution failed to corroborate PW2's testimony by calling other police officers who participated in the raid, and under Section 114(f) of the Evidence Ordinance, the Court drew an adverse inference that such evidence would have been unfavourable to the prosecution. The Court found the trial Judge unfairly rejected defence evidence: DW1's evidence was rejected because she gave a false age when marrying years ago and did not complain to authorities, and DW2's evidence was rejected on flimsy grounds about house numbering despite clearly stating she was a neighbour. The Court held that defence witnesses are entitled to equal treatment with prosecution witnesses, and the defence evidence needed to be considered in totality with prosecution evidence. The Court concluded that the trial Judge failed to give due consideration to defence evidence and that the prosecution failed to prove charges beyond reasonable doubt.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The appellant was convicted by the High Court of Negombo for trafficking and possessing 3.5 grams of heroin under Sections 54A(b) and (d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance and sentenced to life imprisonment. On appeal, the defence challenged the evaluation of discrepancies in prosecution evidence and argued conviction based on uncorroborated testimony of a single witness was unsafe. The Court of Appeal found unexplained inconsistencies in the sole prosecution witness PW2's testimony, noted that the prosecution failed to call other listed witnesses who participated in the raid, and held that the trial Judge unfairly rejected defence evidence on trivial grounds. The Court concluded the prosecution failed to prove charges beyond reasonable doubt and acquitted the appellant of all charges.
defence_counsel: Nalin Ladduwahetty, PC with Kavithri Ubeysekera
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court found PW2 Bandara to be a credible witness and convicted the appellant based on his testimony alone despite lack of corroboration, and rejected defence evidence. The Court of Appeal held that the High Court Judge failed to properly consider inconsistencies in PW2's evidence, unfairly rejected defence evidence on flimsy grounds, and failed to give due consideration to defence witnesses who were entitled to equal treatment with prosecution witnesses.
precedents_cited_list: Sumanasena v Attorney General [1999] 3 SLR 137 | Wijepala v Attorney General S.C. appeal no. 104 of 1999 | State of U.P. v M.K. Anthony 1985 Cri.L.J. 493 | Dudh Nath Panday v. State of Uttar Pradesh (1981) AIR 911 | Don Samantha Jude Anthony Jayamaha v The Attorney General, C.A. 303/2006
standard_of_review_applied: Appellate review of credibility findings and evaluation of whether prosecution proved guilt beyond reasonable doubt

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Sections 54(A)(b) and 54(A)(d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance as amended by Act No. 13 of 1984
brief_facts_summary: On 06/09/2011, SI Manoj Gayantha received information about Appellant trafficking heroin near Ruby Cinema Hall, Maradana in three-wheeler QB 3133. Police team stopped the three-wheeler and found a grocery bag containing white substance in Appellant's right trouser pocket which tested positive for heroin. The passenger Mohamed Murshid Anwer was also arrested but nothing was recovered from him. The substance weighed 31.2 grams at Police Narcotic Bureau and Government Analyst confirmed 8.93 grams of heroin.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 54(A)(d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance as amended by Act No. 13 of 1984
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Colombo found the Appellant guilty on the 2nd count of possession of 8.93 grams of heroin and imposed life imprisonment on 05/04/2018. The Appellant was acquitted from the 1st count of trafficking.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Appellant challenged the conviction on grounds that the trial judge failed to consider the defence that heroin belonged to Mohamed Murshid Anwer who was later released. The Appellant argued that PW9 improperly based testimony on PW4's notes without complying with Section 159 of Evidence Ordinance. The Appellant also contended that the trial judge misdirected himself by adopting evidence from predecessor judge and using PW9's testimony to corroborate PW1.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The Learned Trial Judge failed to consider the defence taken up by the Accused Appellant which is that heroin was in the possession of Mohamed Murshid Anwer and that he was released after his wife and others saw him at the PNB for alleged investigations | The Learned High Court Judge misdirected himself on the law by allowing PW9 to base his testimony before Court on the notes made by PW4 without satisfying himself that the provisions of Section 159 of the Evidence Ordinance have been complied with | The Learned Trial Judge who delivered the judgement misdirected himself on the law by adopting the evidence led before his predecessor under Section 48 of the Judicature Act No.2 of 1978, when the main witnesses for the prosecution had already testified and therefore knowing very well that he will be bereft of the opportunity of applying the legally recognised yardsticks of the law such as demeanour and deportment to assess the evidence of the main witnesses who testified on behalf of the prosecution | The Learned Trial Judge misdirected himself on the law by using the testimony of PW9 to corroborate the testimony of PW1
gnd_other_description: Failure to consider defence version that heroin belonged to co-arrested person Mohamed Murshid Anwer who was subsequently released
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found serious issues with prosecution evidence. PW1's evidence stood uncorroborated on material points after PW4 deserted and PW9 testified based on PW4's incomplete notes. The prosecution failed to explain why Mohamed Murshid Anwer, arrested with Appellant, was released and not charged, creating significant doubt in the case.
probability_assessment_by_court: Court held that PW1's evidence did not pass the probability test and was tainted with ambiguity and uncertainty. The suspicious conduct regarding Mohamed Murshid Anwer's arrest and discharge created reasonable doubt.
defence_version_summary: Appellant pleaded not guilty and made a lengthy dock statement. He claimed the heroin was recovered from Mohamed Murshid Anwer who hired his three-wheeler that day, not from him. He stated Mohamed Murshid Anwer's family visited him at Police Narcotic Bureau and he was later discharged while Appellant was charged instead. He did not call witnesses and relied only on dock statement. Court found the dock statement created reasonable doubt on prosecution case.
legal_errors_description: Court found prosecution failed to explain the arrest and discharge of Mohamed Murshid Anwer who was arrested alongside Appellant on aiding and abetting charge. PW1's evidence regarding further investigations involving only Mohamed Murshid Anwer was suspicious and unexplained. The prosecution's reliance on PW9 testifying from PW4's incomplete notes left PW1 uncorroborated on material points.
procedural_defects_description: The prosecution called the Officer-in-Charge of Raiding Unit (PW9) to testify based on notes of PW4 who had deserted, which was described as unusual practice. Defence highlighted that important materials essential to corroborate PW1 were not found in PW4's notes.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal held that while conviction can be based on single witness testimony if wholly reliable, PW1's evidence in this case did not pass the probability test and was tainted with ambiguity and uncertainty. The Court found it highly suspicious that Mohamed Murshid Anwer was arrested alongside Appellant on aiding and abetting charge but prosecution led no evidence regarding his discharge. The fact that only Mohamed Murshid Anwer was taken for further investigations by a different team, not PW1, while Appellant was kept detained, raised serious doubts. The Court emphasized that police evidence in drug cases should be received with caution and closely scrutinized, and judges must not rely on presumption of truthfulness regarding trained officers. The Court also considered the Appellant's dock statement which, though having less evidential value, created doubt on prosecution case. The complete silence regarding arrest and discharge of Mohamed Murshid Anwer, combined with deviation from routine practice by prosecution witnesses, created reasonable doubt warranting acquittal.
coa_sentence_type: Acquitted from second charge, conviction and sentence set aside
brief_judgment_file_summary: Sandar Thadeeswaran was convicted by High Court of Colombo for possession of 8.93 grams of heroin and sentenced to life imprisonment on 05/04/2018, though acquitted of trafficking charge. He appealed arguing the trial judge failed to consider his defence that heroin belonged to co-arrested Mohamed Murshid Anwer who was subsequently released. The Court of Appeal on 29/03/2022 found serious defects in prosecution case including failure to explain why Mohamed Murshid Anwer arrested alongside Appellant was discharged, uncorroborated evidence of key witness PW1, and suspicious conduct of police in taking only Mohamed Murshid Anwer for further investigations. The Court held that Appellant's dock statement combined with prosecution's unexplained conduct created reasonable doubt. The appeal was allowed, conviction and sentence set aside, and Appellant acquitted.
defence_counsel: Rienzie Arseculeratne, P.C. with Eranga Yahandawala
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court relied on PW1's evidence to convict Appellant on possession charge. However, Court of Appeal found the High Court should not have relied on PW1's evidence which was uncorroborated and tainted with ambiguity. The High Court failed to properly scrutinize police evidence and did not adequately consider the suspicious circumstances surrounding Mohamed Murshid Anwer's arrest and discharge.
precedents_cited_list: Mohamed Nimnaz v. Attorney General CA/95/94 | Miller v. Minister of Pensions (1947) 2 All E.R. 372 | Devundarage Nihal v. AG SC. Appeal No.15 of 2010 | Chacko Alias Aniyan Kunju & others v. State of Kerala [2004] INSC 87 | Iswari Prasad v. Mohamed Isa 1963 AIR (SC) 1728 | Sinniah Kalidasa v. The Hon. Attorney General CA/128/2005 | Queen v. Buddharakkita Thero and others 63 NLR 433 | Kathubdeen v. Republic of Sri Lanka [1998] 3 SLR 107
standard_of_review_applied: Proof beyond reasonable doubt standard. Court applied principle that evidence must reach high degree of probability and examined whether prosecution case left only remote possibility in Appellant's favour that could be dismissed, or whether reasonable doubt existed.

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna, J. | R. Gurusinghe, J.
offence_sections: Section 365b (2)b of The Penal Code as Amended by Act No 22 of 1995
brief_facts_summary: The accused-appellant was charged with committing indecent sexual activity on Sachini Kalpana Wijesuriya, a child under sixteen years of age, on 13.10.2000. After being served with the indictment, the accused absconded and the trial proceeded in absentia under section 241 of the Criminal Procedure Code. The prosecution led evidence from the victim (PW1), her mother (PW2), the Judicial Medical Officer (PW3), and two police officers, none of whom were cross-examined by the defence.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 365b (2)b of The Penal Code as Amended by Act No 22 of 1995
hc_sentence_type: 10 years rigorous imprisonment and fine of Rs. 10,000/-, in default one-year simple imprisonment
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Gampaha convicted the accused-appellant in absentia on 08.02.2013 to the charge of grave sexual abuse under Section 365b (2)b of the Penal Code. The accused was sentenced to 10 years rigorous imprisonment with a fine of Rs. 10,000/- and a default term of 1-year simple imprisonment. The trial proceeded under section 241 of the Criminal Procedure Code as the accused absconded after being served with the indictment.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The accused-appellant appealed against the conviction and sentence primarily on the ground that the indictment was not read over to him before the trial commenced on 03.03.2006, 29.01.2007, or 18.07.2007. The appellant challenged the procedural validity of the trial conducted in absentia and argued that the High Court Judge misdirected herself by stating in the judgment that the indictment was read and the accused pleaded not guilty. The appellant also contended that the prosecution evidence was unreliable and inconsistent.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The indictment was not read over to the accused-appellant before the trial commenced | The learned trial Judge misdirected herself by writing in the judgment that the indictment was read over to the accused-appellant and then he pleaded not guilty | The prosecutrix is not a reliable witness and her evidence seriously lacks consistency | Evidence of the prosecutrix is highly inconsistent with her statement to the police and her case history | The elder sister who was an eye-witness was never called to give evidence | Evidence of the prosecutrix's mother consists of inconsistencies and does not corroborate the evidence of the prosecutrix | Medical evidence does not support the evidence of the prosecutrix | The learned trial Judge has not considered the manifest inconsistencies of the prosecution witnesses
gnd_other_description: Failure to properly evaluate evidence and consider manifest inconsistencies; prosecution failed to establish prima facie case
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found that the prosecutrix's evidence was full of inconsistencies and she could not remember key details about the alleged incident. Her evidence contradicted her statement to the police and the case history recorded by the doctor. The mother's evidence also contained inconsistencies and did not corroborate the prosecutrix's testimony. The elder sister, who was allegedly an eye-witness, was never called to testify.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court held that consistent evidence does not create a reasonable doubt or pass the test of probability. The manifestly inconsistent evidence of all prosecution witnesses did not establish a prima facie case against the accused-appellant. The prosecution failed to prove its case beyond reasonable doubt.
defence_version_summary: The accused-appellant absconded after being served with the indictment and the trial proceeded in absentia under section 241 of the Criminal Procedure Code. An Attorney at Law appeared on behalf of the accused during trial and requested re-summoning of witnesses for cross-examination, but subsequently informed the court there would be no cross-examination. No defence was put forward and the accused remained absent throughout, including when sentence was passed.
legal_errors_description: The High Court Judge misdirected herself by writing in the judgment that the indictment was read over to the accused-appellant and then he pleaded not guilty, when in fact the accused was absconding and the indictment was never read. The trial Judge failed to properly evaluate the evidence and did not consider the manifest inconsistencies per se and inter se of the prosecution witnesses.
procedural_defects_description: While the Court of Appeal held that the failure to read the indictment was the fault of the accused-appellant due to his absconding, the High Court Judge erroneously stated in the judgment that the indictment was read and the accused pleaded not guilty, which was factually incorrect.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal conducted a comprehensive review of the evidence and found serious inconsistencies throughout the prosecution's case. The prosecutrix testified 12 years after the alleged incident but could not remember whether the accused came to her home or what he did to her. Her evidence contradicted her police statement and the case history recorded by the JMO regarding the place of occurrence and details of the alleged act. The mother's evidence was also inconsistent and did not corroborate the prosecutrix's testimony. Critically, the elder sister who was allegedly an eye-witness was never called to testify. The Court noted that the trial Judge failed to properly evaluate the evidence and did not consider the manifest inconsistencies. Citing Martin Fernando vs Inspector of Police, Minuwannda 46 NLR 210, the Court held that an Appellate Court must test evidence both extrinsically and intrinsically, and where close examination raises strong doubt, the accused should receive the benefit of the doubt. The Court concluded that the prosecution failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt.
coa_sentence_type: Accused-appellant acquitted and discharged from the charge
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused-appellant Wijesuriya Arachchilage Damith Nayanapriya Wijesuriya was convicted by the High Court of Gampaha on 08.02.2013 for grave sexual abuse of a child under Section 365b (2)b of the Penal Code and sentenced to 10 years rigorous imprisonment with a fine of Rs. 10,000/-. The trial proceeded in absentia as the accused absconded after being served with the indictment. On appeal, the Court of Appeal found that the prosecutrix's evidence was full of inconsistencies and contradicted her police statement and medical case history. The mother's evidence also contained inconsistencies, and the alleged eye-witness was never called to testify. The Court held that the trial Judge failed to properly evaluate the evidence and consider the manifest inconsistencies. Finding that the prosecution failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt, the Court of Appeal allowed the appeal and acquitted the accused-appellant on 28.03.2022.
defence_counsel: N. Jayasinghe AAL with Jaliya Samarasinghe AAL
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court convicted the accused in absentia based on the prosecution evidence. However, the Court of Appeal found that the High Court Judge misdirected herself by incorrectly stating that the indictment was read and the accused pleaded not guilty. More significantly, the Court of Appeal held that the trial Judge failed to properly evaluate the evidence and did not consider the manifest inconsistencies per se and inter se of the prosecution witnesses.
precedents_cited_list: Sudarman De Silva vs. the Attorney General 1986 (1) SLR 09 | Dadimuni Wimalasena vs. AG - CA 135/03 decided on 10.06.2018 | Martin Fernando vs Inspector of Police, Minuwannda 46 NLR 210
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal applied the standard from Martin Fernando vs Inspector of Police, Minuwannda 46 NLR 210, holding that an Appellate Court must test evidence extrinsically and intrinsically, and where close examination raises strong doubt as to guilt, the accused should be given the benefit of the doubt.

judges: K. PRIYANTHA FERNANDO, J (P/CA) | WICKUM A. KALUARACHCHI, J
offence_sections: Section 298 of the Penal Code
offence_category: Causing Death by Rash or Negligent Act
brief_facts_summary: The accused-appellant was the driver of a bus that collided with the deceased Kapuruge Sunil Jayantha causing his death in a motor traffic accident. The deceased was crossing the road from the middle island when he saw two buses coming at excessive speed. He turned back and sat on the road. The bus driven by the appellant struck the deceased. An eyewitness who was a passenger on the bus testified that the appellant was driving at excessive speed and did not apply brakes before the accident.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 298 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The learned High Court Judge convicted the appellant on 26.04.2019 for causing the death of Kapuruge Sunil Jayantha by a rash or negligent act under Section 298 of the Penal Code. The High Court Judge accepted the evidence of the independent eyewitness PW 1 and rejected the testimony of PW 5 as vague and uncertain. The High Court found the appellant guilty of negligent driving.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant raised four main grounds of appeal. First, the High Court Judge failed to appreciate the infirmities in the evidence of the eyewitness and failed to consider contradictions between the eyewitness and PW 5. Second, the High Court Judge failed to duly analyze the testimony of PW 5. Third, there was failure to analyze the defence evidence. Fourth, the judgment is contrary to the evidence led at the trial.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The learned High Court Judge has failed to appreciate the infirmities in the evidence of the eye witness and failed to consider contradictions between the eye witness and PW 5 | The learned High Court Judge has failed to duly analyze the testimony of PW 5 | Failure to analyze the defence evidence | The judgment is contrary to the evidence led at the trial
gnd_other_description: Failure to properly analyze defence evidence and judgment contrary to evidence
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found PW 1 to be a credible independent eyewitness who had no reason to give false evidence. The Court agreed with the High Court that PW 5's evidence was vague and uncertain. The sketch prepared by PW 5 and the eyewitness testimony both confirmed that brake marks appeared only after the place of the accident, supporting that the appellant applied brakes only after the collision.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court assessed that the eyewitness testimony combined with physical evidence of brake marks established beyond reasonable doubt that the appellant drove at excessive speed and applied brakes only after the accident, proving the very high degree of negligence required under Section 298 of the Penal Code.
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty and gave evidence claiming contributory negligence on the part of the deceased. He testified that he saw the deceased crossing the road, applied brakes, and turned the bus towards the middle island thinking the deceased would go to the left side. He claimed he did not drive at excessive speed. The Court rejected the defence version finding it unacceptable based on the evidence of brake marks and eyewitness testimony.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal upheld the High Court's conviction finding that PW 1 was a credible independent eyewitness whose testimony was reliable. The Court found that both the eyewitness testimony and the sketch prepared by PW 5 confirmed that brake marks appeared only after the accident location, contradicting the appellant's claim. The Court rejected the defence argument of contributory negligence, holding that the deceased did everything possible for his own safety when faced with two buses speeding past each other. The Court found that the appellant drove at inordinately excessive speed where he could not stop the bus even at a distance of 40 to 50 meters, and could not stop for more than 80 feet even after applying brakes. Applying precedents including Palansuriya v. Johoran, Lewis v. Denye, and Froom v. Butcher, the Court held that the very high degree of negligence required under Section 298 of the Penal Code was proved through reckless driving at excessive speed.
coa_sentence_type: The sentence passed by the High Court was affirmed as lawful and correct in principle
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused-appellant Devarahandi Asiri Kankanamge Sudath Rohana Jayasiri was convicted by the High Court of Panadura on 26.04.2019 for causing the death of Kapuruge Sunil Jayantha by rash or negligent act under Section 298 of the Penal Code. The appellant was driving a bus that collided with the deceased who had turned back and sat on the road after seeing two buses coming at excessive speed. The Court of Appeal heard the appeal on 28.02.2022 with the appellant unrepresented. The main grounds of appeal challenged the High Court's treatment of eyewitness evidence, analysis of PW 5's testimony, and evaluation of defence evidence. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal on 28.03.2022, holding that the independent eyewitness PW 1 was credible, the sketch and brake marks proved the appellant applied brakes only after the accident, there was no contributory negligence by the deceased, and the very high degree of negligence required under Section 298 was established through reckless driving at excessive speed.
prosecution_counsel: Dilan Rathnayake, Senior Deputy Solicitor General
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court Judge correctly accepted the evidence of the independent eyewitness PW 1 and correctly rejected the vague and uncertain evidence of PW 5. The Court of Appeal found that the High Court Judge properly analyzed PW 5's evidence and correctly found him not to be a credible witness to rely upon. The High Court correctly convicted the appellant.
precedents_cited_list: Lewis v. Denye (1939) 1 K.B. 540 | Froom v. Butcher (1975) 3 all. E.R. 520 - C.A. | Karunadasa v. OIC Motor Traffic, Nittambuwa Police (1987) 1 Sri L.R. 155 | Premasiri v. Officer-in-Charge, Police Station, Matara (1993) 2 Sri L.R. 23 | Palansuriya v. Johoran 48 NLR 400
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the High Court Judge correctly accepted the eyewitness evidence and whether the very high degree of negligence required to prove a charge under Section 298 of the Penal Code was established
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court of Appeal found the sentence passed by the learned High Court Judge to be lawful and correct in principle, affirming both conviction and sentence

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 read with Section 32 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: On September 15, 2006, the 1st Appellant confessed to PW4 that he, along with the 2nd and 3rd Appellants, had killed the deceased and dumped his body in a well near the deceased's house. Police recovered the body from the well with a sarong wrapped around the neck and injuries on the head and neck. An iron bar and sword with blood-like stains were recovered from a nearby toilet pit upon the 1st Appellant's statement. Blood-like stains were found in the deceased's house where the 3rd Appellant was residing.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
