hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Kurunegala convicted all three Appellants under Section 296 of the Penal Code for the murder of Agangangoda Acharige Dharmaratna and sentenced them to death on July 25, 2017. The conviction was based on the confession made by the 1st Appellant and circumstantial evidence presented by the prosecution.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The 1st Appellant challenged the legality of recovery under Section 27(1) of the Evidence Ordinance, argued that the prosecution failed to establish the nexus between recovered weapons and injuries without calling the JMO, contended that the date and time of death were not established, and claimed the High Court Judge failed to properly apply principles of circumstantial evidence. The 2nd and 3rd Appellants argued that using the 1st Appellant's confession against them was inadmissible and their convictions were illegal and bad in law.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Learned High Court Judge misdirected herself causing a failure to assess the legality of the recovery made under Section 27(1) of the Evidence Ordinance | The prosecution failed to establish beyond reasonable doubt the nexus between the alleged recovery of the weapon and the injuries on the deceased by failing to call the JMO | The prosecution failed to establish the date and the time of death beyond reasonable grounds | The Learned High Court Judge misdirected herself causing a failure to apply the principle related to circumstantial evidence | Using the confession of 1st Appellant against the 2nd and 3rd Appellants is inadmissible and their convictions are illegal and bad in law
gnd_other_description: Inadmissibility of confession against co-accused under Section 30 of the Evidence Ordinance
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court analyzed the confession made by the 1st Appellant to PW4 and the subsequent recovery of the body and weapons upon the 1st Appellant's statement under Section 27(1) of the Evidence Ordinance. The Post Mortem Report was admitted under Section 420 without calling the JMO. The Court found insufficient admissible evidence against the 2nd and 3rd Appellants apart from the 1st Appellant's confession which could not be used against them.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court held that the circumstantial evidence against the 1st Appellant was consistent with guilt and inconsistent with innocence. For the 2nd and 3rd Appellants, the Court found that the confessionary evidence was inadmissible against them and no other sufficient incriminating evidence was led.
defence_version_summary: All three Appellants pleaded not guilty and made dock statements denying the allegations. No defence witnesses were called. The 1st Appellant failed to offer any explanation despite strong incriminating evidence. The Court found the defences not credible for the 1st Appellant but found insufficient evidence against the 2nd and 3rd Appellants.
legal_errors_description: The High Court erred by using the confession of the 1st Appellant as evidence against the 2nd and 3rd Appellants in violation of Section 30 of the Evidence Ordinance. The High Court also failed to properly assess that there was insufficient independent evidence against the 2nd and 3rd Appellants beyond the inadmissible confession.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal meticulously examined each ground of appeal. For the 1st Appellant, the Court upheld the conviction finding that the recovery under Section 27(1) was legal, the admission of the Post Mortem Report under Section 420 dispensed with calling the JMO, the date of death was established through the confession and autopsy findings, and the circumstantial evidence was properly applied. The Court noted that the 1st Appellant's failure to explain strong incriminating circumstances strengthened the prosecution's case. However, for the 2nd and 3rd Appellants, the Court applied Section 30 of the Evidence Ordinance and held that the confession of the 1st Appellant could not be used against co-accused. The Court found no independent admissible evidence linking the 2nd and 3rd Appellants to the murder. The prosecution conceded the appeal for the 2nd Appellant, and the Court found that the 3rd Appellant's mere residence in the deceased's house, without other incriminating evidence, was insufficient for conviction. The Court cited precedents including King v. Abeywickrama, Tamil Nadu v Rajendran, Vivekanandan v. Selvaratnam, and Ranjith v. State to support its findings.
coa_sentence_type: 1st Appellant: Death sentence upheld; 2nd and 3rd Appellants: Acquitted
brief_judgment_file_summary: Three Appellants were charged with murder under Section 296 read with Section 32 of the Penal Code for killing Agangangoda Acharige Dharmaratna on September 22, 2006. The High Court of Kurunegala convicted all three and sentenced them to death on July 25, 2017. The 1st Appellant challenged the legality of evidence recovery, lack of JMO testimony, failure to establish time of death, and improper application of circumstantial evidence principles. The 2nd and 3rd Appellants argued that the 1st Appellant's confession was inadmissible against them. The Court of Appeal dismissed the 1st Appellant's appeal, finding strong circumstantial evidence and proper application of Section 27(1) of the Evidence Ordinance. However, the Court allowed the appeals of the 2nd and 3rd Appellants, holding that the 1st Appellant's confession could not be used against co-accused under Section 30 of the Evidence Ordinance and that no independent admissible evidence existed against them. The 2nd and 3rd Appellants were acquitted.
defence_counsel: Tenny Fernando for the 1st Appellant, Nihara Randeniya for the 2nd Appellant, Anil Silva, P.C. with Nandana Perera for the 3rd Appellant
cause_of_death_exact_text: death was caused due to head injury caused by blunt and sharp weapons
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court convicted all three Appellants based on the confession of the 1st Appellant and circumstantial evidence. The Court of Appeal found that the High Court erred in using the confession of the 1st Appellant against the 2nd and 3rd Appellants in violation of Section 30 of the Evidence Ordinance, and that the High Court failed to recognize the lack of independent admissible evidence against the 2nd and 3rd Appellants.
precedents_cited_list: King v. Abeywickrama Et Al 44 NLR 254 | Tamil Nadu v Rajendran Appeal (1996) 917(Cr.L) | Somaratne Rajapakse Others v Hon. Attorney General (S.C. Appeal) 2/2002 TAB | Vivekanandan v. Selvaratnam 79I NLR 337 | Joseph v. Peris et al 24 NLR 485 | Ranjith v. State [2000] 3 SLR page 346 | Perera v. Attorney General (1998) 1 SLR 378
standard_of_review_applied: The Court applied appellate review of conviction and sentence examining whether the prosecution proved guilt beyond reasonable doubt and whether evidence was properly admitted and evaluated

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna J. | R. Gurusinghe J.
offence_sections: Section 357 of the Penal Code and Section 364(1) of the Penal Code as amended by Act No.22 of 1995
brief_facts_summary: On 16.09.2006, the 19-year-old victim was returning home after collecting 'seettu money' when the accused-appellant, whom she had known since childhood, forcibly abducted her by covering her mouth and strangling her. He dragged her to a forest and tea estate, assaulted her, tore off her clothes despite her resistance, and forcibly raped her on a rock for approximately 30 minutes. The victim testified that the accused grabbed her neck during the assault and she saw white liquid and sperm on his penis and her clothes afterward.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 357 of the Penal Code and Section 364(1) of the Penal Code as amended by Act No.22 of 1995
hc_sentence_type: Rigorous Imprisonment for Count 1: 6 years, Count 2: 14 years; Fines and Compensation with default terms; All sentences to run consecutively
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Ratnapura convicted the accused-appellant on both counts of kidnapping and rape. For Count 1, the accused was sentenced to 6 years rigorous imprisonment with a fine of Rs.20,000 and default term of 6 months simple imprisonment. For Count 2, he was sentenced to 14 years rigorous imprisonment, a fine of Rs.50,000 with default term of 6 months simple imprisonment, and compensation of Rs.200,000 with default term of 2 years simple imprisonment, with all sentences running consecutively.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The accused-appellant challenged only the sentence imposed by the High Court, not the conviction itself. The defence counsel requested the Court of Appeal to consider mitigating factors including that the accused had no previous convictions and had saved the Court's time by not challenging the conviction. The counsel argued for a reduction in sentence based on these factors and that the accused was a father of two children.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The accused-appellant has no previous conviction | He has saved the time in the Court of Appeal | The accused-appellant is a father of 2 children
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The prosecution led evidence from 6 witnesses including the victim (PW 1) who provided detailed testimony of the kidnapping and rape. The Judicial Medical Officer (PW 8) testified that while there was no evidence of vaginal penetration, findings were consistent with inter labia penetration with probable attempt to penetrate the vagina. Medical evidence showed physical injuries of intentional violence including numerous abrasions on lower limbs, injuries on buttock and back of elbows, all consistent with the victim's account of sexual assault.
defence_version_summary: The accused pleaded not guilty at trial. In his dock statement, he denied the allegations and claimed he had a relationship with the victim and they had known each other for a long time. He stated that on the day in question, they were chatting near a stream when her brothers came, causing them to run away together to a tea estate. He claimed the victim refused to go home saying her brothers would beat her. The court found the victim's testimony credible and rejected the accused's version.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal noted that the accused-appellant did not challenge the conviction but only the sentence. The court acknowledged the mitigating factors presented by the defence counsel, including that the accused had no previous convictions, was a father of two children, and had saved the court's time by not challenging the conviction. The learned President's Counsel for the respondent agreed that these reasons were reasonable. The Court of Appeal recognized that the accused's wife and children would suffer due to his bad behaviour and considered it important that he saved the court's time by challenging only the sentence. Given these circumstances and the absence of previous convictions, the court decided the accused-appellant deserved some relief and reduced the sentence for Count 2 from 14 years to 10 years rigorous imprisonment while keeping other sentences the same, and ordered all sentences to run concurrently instead of consecutively.
coa_sentence_type: Count 1: 6 years rigorous imprisonment with fine of Rs.20,000 and default term of 6 months simple imprisonment; Count 2: 10 years rigorous imprisonment with fine of Rs.50,000 and default term of 6 months simple imprisonment and compensation of Rs.200,000 with default term of 2 years simple imprisonment; All sentences to run concurrently from 23.04.2020
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused-appellant Sudu Hakurugee Chaminda Kumara was convicted by the High Court of Ratnapura on 23.04.2020 for kidnapping and raping a 19-year-old woman on 16.09.2006, and sentenced to 22 years rigorous imprisonment with fines totaling Rs.70,000 and compensation of Rs.200,000, with all sentences running consecutively. The accused appealed only the sentence, presenting mitigating factors including no previous convictions, being a father of two children, and saving court time by not challenging the conviction. The Court of Appeal, agreeing with both defence and prosecution that these factors were reasonable, reduced the sentence for Count 2 from 14 years to 10 years rigorous imprisonment and ordered all sentences to run concurrently instead of consecutively, effective from 23.04.2020. The appeal was dismissed subject to these alterations.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court convicted the accused on both counts based on the victim's testimony and supporting medical evidence from the Judicial Medical Officer. The High Court imposed substantial sentences including consecutive imprisonment terms totaling 22 years (6 years for Count 1 and 14 years for Count 2 running consecutively). The Court of Appeal did not identify any errors in the High Court's conviction but found that some relief on sentencing was warranted given the mitigating circumstances.
standard_of_review_applied: Sentencing review considering mitigating factors
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court of Appeal applied principles of mitigation in sentencing, considering factors such as absence of previous convictions, family circumstances (father of two children), and saving court time by not challenging conviction. The court balanced the seriousness of the offences with mitigating circumstances to provide some relief to the accused.

judges: Sampath B Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 140 of the Penal Code (Count 1); Section 296 read with Section 146 of the Penal Code (Count 2); Section 296 read with Section 32 of the Penal Code (Count 3)
brief_facts_summary: Five accused were charged with murdering Alfred Sirimal Bandara on 29 September 1995. The victim was at his sister Swarnalatha's home helping with roof repairs when the accused entered the house armed with weapons (swords, knives), dragged him outside, and inflicted 48 cut and stab wounds resulting in his death. The only eyewitness was the victim's sister PW-02, who identified all accused as neighbours and saw the attack despite limited lighting. The 1st accused absconded during trial and was tried in absentia.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 140 of the Penal Code (Count 1); Section 296 read with Section 146 of the Penal Code (Count 2); Section 296 read with Section 32 of the Penal Code (Count 3)
hc_sentence_type: Six months rigorous imprisonment and fine of Rs. 5000 on Count 1; Death sentence on Counts 2 and 3
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Kegalle found all five appellants guilty as charged on all three counts after trial without jury. The trial proceeded in absentia for the 1st accused who absconded. The court accepted the eyewitness testimony of PW-02 and medical evidence showing 48 cut and stab wounds on the deceased, sentencing all accused to six months rigorous imprisonment and Rs. 5000 fine on Count 1, and death sentence on Counts 2 and 3.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The main ground raised by all appellants was that conviction was bad in law due to doubt regarding identification of the accused. Additional grounds included: the 3rd count should have been alternative to Count 2; Section 27 statement was improperly admitted causing prejudice; the trial judge failed to evaluate individual participation; credibility of main witness PW-02 was not properly considered; no evidence of common object for murder or participatory presence; and PW-02 did not actually witness the murder making her unreliable.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Conviction bad in law due to doubt as to identification | 3rd count should have been considered as alternative count | Section 27 statement should not have been admitted and caused prejudice | Trial judge failed to evaluate part played by 2nd appellant in judgment | Credibility of main witness PW-02 not properly considered | No evidence of common object for murder or participatory presence of 4th appellant | PW-02 did not witness the murder and is unreliable witness
gnd_other_description: 3rd count should have been considered as alternative count to 2nd count
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal thoroughly examined the eyewitness testimony of PW-02, concluding it was credible and trustworthy. The court found she could identify the accused when they entered with three lamps lit, and could observe the outside attack through moonlight and a remaining lamp despite two lamps being extinguished. Medical evidence from DMO (PW-06) confirmed 48 cut and stab wounds on the deceased, corroborating the violent group attack described by PW-02.
probability_assessment_by_court: Court found proof beyond reasonable doubt based on credible eyewitness testimony corroborated by medical evidence showing 48 wounds inflicted by multiple persons, consistent with witness account of group attack
defence_version_summary: All five accused pleaded not guilty and made only dock statements. Each denied any involvement in the crime and claimed they were falsely implicated due to animosity. They did not call any defence witnesses. The court rejected their denials as the eyewitness testimony and medical evidence established their guilt beyond reasonable doubt.
legal_errors_description: The Court of Appeal found that the 3rd count (murder with common intention under Section 296 read with Section 32) should have been considered as an alternative count to the 2nd count (murder by unlawful assembly under Section 296 read with Section 146). The High Court Judge should not have convicted on both counts as they relate to the same offence of murder, which would amount to punishing the appellants twice for the same offence.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal systematically addressed each ground of appeal. On identification, the court held that PW-02 credibly identified the accused who were her neighbors, with sufficient lighting from three lamps initially and moonlight through the removed roof. The court cited Regina v. Turnbull principles and found the High Court Judge properly assessed identification circumstances. On the Section 27 recovery, the court found no prejudice as the torch was not material to conviction. On common object and common intention, the court found sufficient evidence that all accused were members of the unlawful assembly who attacked the deceased with weapons. However, the court agreed that the 3rd count should have been alternative to the 2nd count, and set aside the conviction on Count 3 to avoid double punishment for the same murder offence. The appeal was otherwise dismissed as the convictions on Counts 1 and 2 were properly sustained.
coa_sentence_type: Six months rigorous imprisonment and fine of Rs. 5000 on Count 1; Death sentence on Count 2 only (conviction on Count 3 set aside)
brief_judgment_file_summary: Five accused-appellants were convicted by the High Court of Kegalle for the 1995 murder of Alfred Sirimal Bandara, sentenced to six months rigorous imprisonment and Rs. 5000 fine on Count 1 (unlawful assembly), and death on Counts 2 and 3 (murder by unlawful assembly and common intention). They appealed challenging identification, credibility of the sole eyewitness, and procedural issues. The Court of Appeal upheld the convictions on Counts 1 and 2, finding the eyewitness testimony credible and corroborated by medical evidence of 48 wounds. However, the court agreed Count 3 should have been alternative to Count 2, and set aside the conviction on Count 3 to avoid double punishment. The appeal was partly allowed with modified conviction and sentence.
defence_counsel: Duminda De Alwis (1st Accused); Anil Silva, P.C. (2nd Accused); Sheron Wanigasooriya (3rd and 5th Accused); Nayantha Wijesundera (4th Accused)
weapon_type: Sword, sharp pointed knife, ketti knife, curved knife
cause_of_death_exact_text: Death due to excessive bleeding from the injuries within a short span of time
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court Judge properly assessed the eyewitness testimony and medical evidence, correctly determining guilt on all counts. However, the COA found the High Court erred in not treating Count 3 as an alternative count to Count 2, as both related to the same murder offence. The High Court Judge was mindful of this by imposing a single death sentence for Counts 2 and 3 combined, but should have only convicted on one of these counts.
precedents_cited_list: Regina V. Turnbull and Another (1997) QB 224 | Bhoginbhai Hitijibhai Vs. State of Gujarat (AIR 1983-SC 753) | Kulanthunga V. Mudalyhami 42 NLR 331 | The Queen V N.K.A. Appuhamy 62 NLR 484 | King Vs. Assappu 50 NLR 324
standard_of_review_applied: Appeal from conviction - Court of Appeal reviewed sufficiency of evidence, credibility of witnesses, and legal errors in conviction and sentencing
sentencing_principles_applied: Death sentence imposed for murder under Section 296 of the Penal Code; court recognized that imposing punishment on both Count 2 and Count 3 would amount to double punishment for the same offence of murder

judges: K. Priyantha Fernando, J (P/CA) | Wickum A. Kaluarachchi, J
offence_sections: Section 365 B(2)(b) of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: On 27.12.2007, the 15-year-old victim Mahesh Saman Kumara was returning from his aunt's place when the appellant, an unknown person, caught him by the hand and took him to a room in the Balangoda Municipal ground where he committed grave sexual abuse. After the incident, the victim met Police officer Amarasinghe on night patrol duty around 11.40 p.m., informed him about the incident, and went with the police to the Municipal grounds where he identified the appellant. The appellant was arrested at 12.30 a.m. and brought to the police station.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 365 B(2)(b) of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Rathnapura convicted the appellant of committing grave sexual abuse punishable under Section 365 B(2)(b) of the Penal Code and sentenced him to 14 years rigorous imprisonment with a fine of Rs.25,000/- and ordered Rs.200,000/- as compensation to the victim. The High Court evaluated the evidence and found that the victim properly identified the appellant.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: Although four grounds of appeal were stated in the written submission, the learned counsel for the appellant made submissions only on the ground of false implication of the accused-appellant. The appellant contended that police officers forced the victim to implicate the appellant and that the identification of the appellant by the victim was doubtful. The defence argued there was no evidence of where the victim was from midnight to the next morning and that the prosecution story about the victim going with police to identify the appellant could not be believed.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: False implication of the accused-appellant by police officers | Doubtful identification of the appellant by the victim | No evidence of where the victim was from midnight to the next morning | Prosecution story that victim went with police to the ground and identified the appellant could not be believed
gnd_other_description: False implication by police officers forcing the victim to implicate the appellant
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court found the victim's evidence credible and corroborated by PW 9 (Police officer Amarasinghe) and medical evidence. The victim identified the appellant immediately after the incident without delay, made a complaint to police without delay, and had no reason to falsely implicate an unknown person. The Court noted that the victim properly distinguished between the watcher and the appellant when identifying the perpetrator.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court found the victim's evidence to be of such character as to convince that he was speaking the truth and the charge could be proved beyond reasonable doubt on his evidence alone.
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty and the defence took the position of false implication by police. The defence contended that police officers forced the victim to implicate the appellant for this offence. No witnesses were called by the defence and no cross-examination was conducted to challenge key facts such as the victim going with police to identify the appellant. The Court found no merit in the defence argument of false implication.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal examined the defence argument of false implication and found no merit in it. The Court noted that the victim had no reason to falsely implicate the appellant who was an unknown person at the time of the incident. The Court emphasized that the victim identified the appellant immediately after the incident without delay, which is a strong identification. The Court rejected the argument that where the victim was from midnight to morning was a material fact, noting it had no relevance to the prosecution case. The Court found significance in the fact that the defence failed to cross-examine the victim on key points such as going with police to identify the appellant, making such evidence unchallenged. The Court applied the legal principle from Regina V. W.G. Dharmasena that in sexual offence cases, corroboration is not necessary in law, though here the victim's evidence was corroborated by PW 9 and medical evidence. The Court affirmed that the victim's evidence was of such character to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt. The Court held that the High Court Judge correctly evaluated all relevant evidence, considered all contradictions and omissions, and extensively dealt with the facts in a well-considered 42-page judgment. Finding no reason to interfere, the Court dismissed the appeal and affirmed the conviction and sentence.
coa_sentence_type: 14 years Rigorous Imprisonment with Fine of Rs.25,000/- and Compensation of Rs.200,000/-
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused-appellant Narangoda Arachchilage Sarath Kumara was indicted before the High Court of Rathnapura for committing grave sexual abuse on a 15-year-old boy on 27.12.2007, an offence punishable under Section 365 B(2)(b) of the Penal Code. The High Court convicted him and sentenced him to 14 years rigorous imprisonment, a fine of Rs.25,000/-, and compensation of Rs.200,000/- to the victim. The appellant appealed on grounds of false implication and doubtful identification. The Court of Appeal found that the victim identified the appellant immediately after the incident, the victim had no reason to falsely implicate an unknown person, and the defence failed to cross-examine key witnesses on material points. The Court held that the victim's evidence was credible and corroborated by police and medical evidence, and affirmed the High Court's conviction and sentence, dismissing the appeal.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court Judge evaluated all relevant evidence correctly, considered all contradictions and omissions, and extensively dealt with the relevant facts and circumstances in a well-considered 42-page judgment. The Court of Appeal found that the High Court Judge correctly observed that the victim gave evidence that can be accepted without reasonable doubt and properly found that the victim had properly identified the appellant.
precedents_cited_list: Banda and others V. Attorney General (1999) 3 Sri L.R. 168 | Regina V. W.G. Dharmasena 58 NLR 15 | King V. Themis Singho 45 NLR 378 | Premasiri V. the Queen 77 NLR 85
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether there was any reason to interfere with the judgment and sentence of the learned High Court Judge based on the grounds of appeal raised

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Section 54 A and B of the Poisons Opium and Dangerous Drugs Amendment Act No. 13 of 1984
brief_facts_summary: The petitioner Razik Ramzeen was taken into custody for being in possession of heroin on 9.2.2013. He was indicted for the same on 18.11.2013 under the Poisons Opium and Dangerous Drugs Amendment Act No. 13 of 1984. The petitioner had been in remand for nearly 10 years without trial being concluded, with only prosecution witness no. 1 being partly concluded after 15 trial dates.
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Negombo refused to enlarge the accused petitioner on bail. The trial judge had refused bail on multiple occasions on the basis that the trial had been postponed owing to the huge backlog of cases pending in the Court.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The main grievance of the petitioner is that he has been in remand for nearly 10 years without the trial being concluded. He argued that this constitutes exceptional circumstances warranting bail under the Poisons Opium and Dangerous Drugs Amendment Act. The petitioner challenged the High Court's refusal to grant bail despite the excessive delay in trial proceedings.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Being in remand for 10 years without trial being concluded | Excessive delay constituting exceptional circumstances for bail | Trial postponed on 15 trial dates with only one prosecution witness partly examined
gnd_other_description: Challenge to refusal of bail under exceptional circumstances
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The trial had been postponed on 15 trial dates with only one prosecution witness being partly concluded. No detailed evaluation of witness credibility or evidence quality was provided in this revision application as the trial had not progressed substantially.
defence_version_summary: The petitioner's defence focused on procedural matters rather than substantive defence to the charges. The defence argued for bail based on exceptional circumstances arising from the excessive delay in trial proceedings and prolonged remand detention.
legal_errors_description: The High Court erred in refusing bail despite the excessive and oppressive delay of nearly 10 years without trial conclusion. The trial judge failed to give precedence to the case where the accused was in remand and failed to expedite the matter, which undermines due administration of justice.
procedural_defects_description: The trial had been postponed on 15 trial dates with only one prosecution witness being partly examined, showing failure to expedite trial for accused in remand. The High Court failed to properly consider the exceptional circumstances arising from the oppressive delay.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal held that the nearly 10-year delay in trial proceedings constituted an exceptional circumstance warranting bail. The Court noted that only prosecution witness no. 1 had been partly concluded despite 15 trial dates having passed. The Court emphasized that Article 13(4) of the Constitution states that holding in custody pending trial shall not constitute punishment. Citing CH/PHC/APN/36/2016 and Sumanadasa vs. Attorney General, the Court held that when an accused is on remand, it becomes the duty of the trial judge to expedite and conclude the matter without delay. The Court found the delay to be oppressive and excessive, not merely explicable due to backlog. The Court rejected the respondent's argument about backlog, stating that if the Court is overloaded with work, it is more reason to consider bail because conclusion of trial seems remote. The Court held that the right to a fair trial without delay is of utmost importance and trial judges must give precedence to cases where the accused is in remand.
coa_sentence_type: Bail granted with conditions to be imposed by High Court
brief_judgment_file_summary: Razik Ramzeen was arrested on 9.2.2013 for possession of heroin and indicted on 18.11.2013 under the Poisons Opium and Dangerous Drugs Amendment Act. The High Court of Negombo refused bail multiple times citing backlog of cases. The petitioner filed a revision application challenging the refusal of bail after being in remand for nearly 10 years with trial postponed 15 times and only one prosecution witness partly examined. The Court of Appeal allowed the revision application, holding that the nearly 10-year delay constituted oppressive and exceptional circumstances warranting bail. The Court set aside the High Court's order refusing bail and directed the High Court to impose suitable conditions and enlarge the petitioner on bail.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court had refused bail on multiple occasions based solely on the huge backlog of cases pending in Court. The Court of Appeal criticized this approach, holding that the High Court failed to properly consider the exceptional circumstances arising from the oppressive delay and failed in its duty to expedite trial for an accused in remand.
precedents_cited_list: Carder vs. OIC Narcotics Bureau 2006 3 SLR 74 | CH/PHC/APN/36/2016 | Sumanadasa vs. Attorney General SLR 2006 202 | The AG v Segulebbe Latheef and another S.C. Appeal No. 79A/2007, 24/2008 and 25/2008 | Attorney General vs. Ediriweera 2006 (BLR) 12
standard_of_review_applied: Revision under Article 138(1) of the Constitution to set aside the High Court order refusing bail based on exceptional circumstances arising from excessive delay

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna J. | R. Gurusinghe J.
offence_sections: Section 380 of the Penal Code and Section 383 of the Penal Code read together with Section 32 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: On 04.09.2007, three accused persons committed robbery of cash amounting to Rs. 5,000, a chain valued at Rs. 18,000, and a mobile phone valued at Rs. 4,000 from Nagarasa Kanthimalar. During the robbery, the accused used deadly weapons including a gun and a knife. The accused were arrested and taken into custody on 17.10.2007.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 380 of the Penal Code and Section 383 of the Penal Code read together with Section 32 of the Penal Code
hc_sentence_type: 15 years rigorous imprisonment and a fine of Rs. 10,000 each in default 2 years simple imprisonment
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Vavuniya convicted all three accused-appellants of robbery charges under sections 380 and 383 read with section 32 of the Penal Code. The court sentenced each of them to 15 years rigorous imprisonment and a fine of Rs. 10,000 each, with a default sentence of 2 years simple imprisonment.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellants filed this appeal claiming that the sentence of 15 years rigorous imprisonment was excessive. The learned counsel for the accused-appellants informed the Court that they were not challenging the conviction. The defence requested the Court to consider mitigating factors, primarily that all three accused-appellants had been in custody since 17.10.2007.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The sentence is excessive | All three accused-appellants have been in custody since 17.10.2007 | The incident occurred during LTTE disturbances in September 2007
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The prosecution led evidence from four witnesses including the victim Nagarasa Kanthimalar, a Court Interpreter, and two police officers. The prosecution marked documents P1 to P7. All three accused testified on their own behalf denying the charges levelled against them.
probability_assessment_by_court: Not discussed
defence_version_summary: All three accused pleaded not guilty to the charges. All three accused testified themselves denying the charges levelled against them. The Court of Appeal judgment does not provide details about the credibility assessment of the defence version.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal considered the appeal which was limited to a challenge of the sentence, as the appellants did not challenge the conviction. The Court noted that the accused-appellants had been in custody for more than 13 years since 17.10.2007. The Court also took into consideration that the incident took place in September 2007 during the LTTE disturbances. The learned counsel for the respondent agreed that the reasons submitted to reduce the sentence were reasonable and that the appellants had saved the time of the court. Considering these circumstances and the mitigating factors, the Court of Appeal decided to reduce the sentence from 15 years to 7 years rigorous imprisonment, while maintaining the fine of Rs. 10,000 but reducing the default sentence from 2 years to 6 months simple imprisonment.
coa_sentence_type: 7 years rigorous imprisonment and a fine of Rs. 10,000 each with default sentence of 6 months simple imprisonment, all sentences to run concurrently with effect from 07.10.2016
brief_judgment_file_summary: Three accused-appellants were charged with robbery under sections 380 and 383 read with section 32 of the Penal Code for robbing Nagarasa Kanthimalar of cash, a gold chain, and a mobile phone using deadly weapons on 04.09.2007 in Vavuniya. The High Court of Vavuniya convicted all three and sentenced them to 15 years rigorous imprisonment and a fine of Rs. 10,000 each in default 2 years simple imprisonment. The appellants appealed only against the sentence, arguing it was excessive and citing that they had been in custody since 17.10.2007. The Court of Appeal considered the mitigating factors including the prolonged custody period of over 13 years and the fact that the incident occurred during LTTE disturbances, and reduced the sentence to 7 years rigorous imprisonment with the fine maintained but the default sentence reduced to 6 months simple imprisonment.
defence_counsel: Shanaka Ranasinghe PC with Tharaki Manchanayaka AAL for the 1st Accused-Appellant; N. Mihindukulasuriya AAL with Priyasala Padmasiri AAL for the 2nd and 3rd Accused-Appellants
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court convicted all three accused and imposed a sentence of 15 years rigorous imprisonment and a fine of Rs. 10,000 each. The Court of Appeal did not identify any errors in the High Court's analysis of the conviction but found the sentence to be excessive given the mitigating circumstances.
standard_of_review_applied: Review of sentencing discretion considering mitigating factors
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court of Appeal applied principles of leniency based on prolonged custody period of more than 13 years and the context of LTTE disturbances during which the offence occurred. The Court also considered that the appellants saved court time by not challenging the conviction.

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna, J. | R. Gurusinghe, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The appellant came to the deceased's house on October 15, 2012 and demanded money from her. When she refused and went to the kitchen to prepare lunch, the appellant poured kerosene oil on the deceased and set her on fire. The deceased's 11-year-old son (PW1) witnessed the incident, and the appellant fled after the act. The deceased was taken to Matara General Hospital and later transferred to Colombo, where she died on October 20, 2012.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Tangalle convicted the appellant of murder under section 296 of the Penal Code after trial before judge alone. The court accepted the eyewitness testimony of the deceased's 11-year-old son (PW1) who witnessed the appellant pour kerosene oil on his mother and set her on fire. The court also relied on the dying declaration made by the deceased to multiple witnesses and to the police. The appellant was sentenced to death.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant challenged the conviction primarily on the basis that the solitary eyewitness was an 11-year-old child whose testimony was uncorroborated, contradicted by the dying declaration, possibly tutored, and unnatural in behavior. The appellant also challenged the reliability of the dying declaration and argued that the medical evidence was not properly appreciated. Additional grounds included that the first information did not contain the appellant's name, prosecution failed to call medical reports of the appellant, and the possibility of suicide was not considered.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The Learned High Court Judge failed to consider that evidence of solitary eye witness who is a child cannot be accepted due to: (A) testimony not corroborated by medical evidence, (B) testimony contradicted by dying declaration, (C) failure to record statement from child on day of incident suggesting possibility of tutoring, (D) unnatural behavior of child witness | The Learned High Court Judge failed to consider the inherent weaknesses of the purported dying declaration of the deceased | The Learned High Court Judge seriously erred in appreciating the evidence of other prosecution witnesses including the JMO who performed the post-mortem | The Learned Trial Judge did not consider that the first information does not contain the name of the accused-appellant | The Learned High Court Judge failed to consider that prosecution did not call medical reports of the accused-appellant thereby denying the right to a fair trial | The Learned Trial Judge failed to consider that the probability of suicide cannot be totally ruled out | Prosecution's failure to prove its case beyond reasonable doubt has not been considered in a judicial perspective by the Learned Trial Judge
gnd_other_description: Alternative defense of sudden provocation should have been considered
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found that PW1, an 11-year-old eyewitness, was a competent witness who gave evidence describing how the appellant poured kerosene oil on his mother and set her on fire. The court found that PW1's evidence was corroborated by the dying declaration made by the deceased to PW2, PW3, and the police officer PW6. The medical evidence from the JMO confirmed that 81% of the deceased's body was burnt, consistent with kerosene oil being poured over the body.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court found that the prosecution proved its case beyond reasonable doubt based on the eyewitness testimony of PW1 corroborated by the dying declaration and medical evidence. The court rejected the defense suggestion of suicide as it was never put to any prosecution witnesses and was raised only in the dock statement.
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty and elected trial before judge alone. He made only a dock statement and did not call any witnesses. In his dock statement, he admitted being present at the deceased's house at the time of the incident and that PW1 was also present. He suggested for the first time that the deceased may have committed suicide. The court found this defense unconvincing as it was never put to any prosecution witnesses in cross-examination and the appellant's flight from the scene contradicted the suicide theory.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal conducted a comprehensive review of the High Court's findings and rejected all grounds of appeal. The court held that an 11-year-old child is a competent witness under section 118 of the Evidence Ordinance if capable of understanding questions and giving rational answers. The court found that PW1's statement recorded within 24 hours was not delayed and his behavior was natural for a child of that age who lacked physical strength to act. The court found no material contradictions between PW1's evidence and the medical evidence, noting that 81% of the deceased's body was burnt. The court accepted the dying declaration as reliable, noting it was made to three different persons consistently and was corroborated by PW1's eyewitness account. The court rejected the suicide theory as it was never put to witnesses in cross-examination and was inconsistent with the appellant fleeing the scene. The court cited the principle that evidence not challenged in cross-examination must be accepted, relying on Galagamage Indrawansa Kumarasiri v AG and other precedents. The court also rejected the alternative ground of sudden provocation as there was insufficient evidence and it was never suggested to witnesses.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The appellant Kaluhennadige Rohitha Sarath Jayantha was convicted by the High Court of Tangalle for the murder of Loku Yaddehige Rajika on October 15, 2012 at Kudawella, and sentenced to death. The prosecution case was that the appellant came to the deceased's house demanding money, and when refused, poured kerosene oil on her and set her on fire in the presence of her 11-year-old son PW1. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal, finding that the eyewitness testimony of the child witness was reliable, competent, and corroborated by the dying declaration made by the deceased to multiple persons and by medical evidence showing 81% burns on the body. The court rejected defenses of suicide and sudden provocation as they were not put to prosecution witnesses in cross-examination and were inconsistent with the evidence.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court properly evaluated the evidence of the child witness PW1 and found him to be a competent witness. The High Court considered the dying declaration with caution and found it reliable and consistent with the eyewitness account. The High Court applied the correct legal principles regarding dying declarations citing the Bench Book Law of Evidence, E.R.S.R. Coomaraswamy, and Ranjit vs. The State. The Court of Appeal found no error in the High Court's analysis and reasoning.
precedents_cited_list: Bench Book Law of Evidence | Law of Evidence E.R.S.R. Coomaraswamy | Ranjit vs. The State 2000 3 SLR 346 | Galagamage Indrawansa Kumarasiri and others vs Honorable Attorney General SC TBA appeal No. 02/2012 decided on 2/4/2014 | Dadimuni Indrasena & Dadimuni Wimalasena v AG (2008) | Pilippu Mandige Nalaka Krishantha Kumara Tissera v. AG (2007) | Sarwan Singh v State of Punjab (2002) (AIR SC 111) | Motilal v. State of Madhya Pradesh (1990) (CLJ NOC 125 MP)
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether there was sufficient evidence to support the conviction and whether the High Court Judge properly evaluated the evidence and applied the law. The court examined whether the High Court's findings were reasonable and supported by evidence.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Section 54A and 54B of the Poisons Opium and Dangerous Drugs Act
brief_facts_summary: The petitioner was arrested on 21st May 2015 in Tangalle. No alleged substance was found in his custody at the time of arrest. He was also detained under the Prevention of Terrorism Act due to alleged involvement in terrorist activities. The petitioner has been in remand since arrest and has two other pending cases of similar nature.
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court issued orders dated 12.6.2020 and 23.12.2020 refusing bail to the petitioner. The Court of Appeal disagreed with the legal reasoning of these bail orders but ultimately dismissed the application.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner argued that the High Court orders were illegal and capricious. He contended the High Court declared him guilty before trial, erroneously believed bail applications could only be made once, and incorrectly required 'attractive' rather than 'exceptional' grounds. He also argued five and half years in remand and his health condition after bypass surgery constituted exceptional circumstances.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: High Court declared him guilty of the offence before conviction | High Court erroneously believed a bail application can be made only once | High Court incorrectly believed petitioner has to submit attractive grounds for bail instead of exceptional grounds | Five and half years in remand should be considered exceptional | Health condition requiring post-bypass surgery precautions should be considered exceptional
gnd_other_description: Health condition requiring medical precautions after bypass surgery
defence_version_summary: The petitioner contended he was arrested and no alleged substance was found in his custody. He argued exceptional circumstances exist for bail including prolonged remand and health issues after bypass surgery.
legal_errors_description: The Court of Appeal disagreed with the legal reasoning of the High Court in the two impugned bail orders, particularly regarding the understanding of what constitutes exceptional circumstances and the High Court's belief that bail applications can only be made once.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal analyzed the concept of 'exceptional circumstances' under Section 83 of the Poisons Opium and Dangerous Drugs Act. The Court considered the gravity of the offence, noting the petitioner was accused of possessing a large quantity of heroin for commercial purposes. The Court noted the petitioner had two other pending cases of similar nature and was arrested for the instant matter while on bail for one of them. Regarding the five and half years in remand, the Court held this could not be considered exceptional due to the gravity of offences and the petitioner's criminal history. On health grounds, the Court found the bypass surgery was successfully performed in remand and medical precautions could be adhered to within prison. The Court distinguished between usual delay common to all remand prisoners and inordinate delay, citing Attorney General vs. Letchchami. While disagreeing with the High Court's legal reasoning, the Court found no exceptional grounds to grant bail and dismissed the application.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This is a bail application revision matter before the Court of Appeal. W. Nelson Hubert Caniseus Thamel, the 2nd accused petitioner, was arrested on 21st May 2015 in Tangalle for offences under the Poisons Opium and Dangerous Drugs Act and detained under the Prevention of Terrorism Act. The High Court refused bail orders dated 12.6.2020 and 23.12.2020. The petitioner argued five and half years in remand and health issues after bypass surgery constituted exceptional circumstances warranting bail. The Court of Appeal, while disagreeing with the High Court's legal reasoning, found no exceptional grounds existed given the gravity of the offence involving large quantities of heroin for commercial purposes, the petitioner's two other pending similar cases, and his arrest for the instant offence while on bail. The application was dismissed on 22.03.2022.
prosecution_counsel: Sudharshana De Silva, DSG with Kanishka Rajakaruna, SC
defence_counsel: R Arseculeratne, PC with Thilina Punchihewa and Udara Muhandiramge
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court issued orders refusing bail on 12.6.2020 and 23.12.2020. The Court of Appeal disagreed with the legal reasoning of the High Court, particularly its understanding that bail applications can be made only once and its approach to what constitutes exceptional circumstances for bail under drug offences.
precedents_cited_list: Carder vs. OIC Narcotics Bureau 2006 vol 3 74 | Attorney General vs. Letchchami SC Appeal 13/2006
standard_of_review_applied: Review of bail application in revision under Article 138 of the Constitution, examining whether exceptional circumstances exist under Section 83 of the Poisons Opium and Dangerous Drugs Act

judges: K. Priyantha Fernando, J. (P/CA) | Wickum A. Kaluarachchi, J.
offence_sections: Sections 54A(b) and (d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance
brief_facts_summary: Police received information that heroin was being packed in a 3rd floor flat at Armour Street. CI Nuwan Danthanarayana (PW1) and PC Bandara (PW3) observed three persons packing heroin by candlelight through a window for one and a half hours. After hearing a three-wheeler approach, police raided the flat and arrested the three appellants with 200 packs of heroin weighing 4.11 grams and packing implements.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Sections 54A(b) and (d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Colombo convicted all three appellants on one count of trafficking and one count of possession of 4.11 grams of heroin as charged. The court sentenced each appellant to life imprisonment. The High Court Judge found that PW1 and PW3 were consistent in their evidence and that the photograph P32 showed witnesses could observe inside the flat by standing on their toes.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellants challenged the conviction on three main grounds: that the trial judge failed to properly assess the unreliable and improbable evidence of PW1 and PW3; that the judge failed to apply principles relating to circumstantial evidence; and that the chain of custody of the seized substance was not established beyond reasonable doubt.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The learned trial Judge erred in fact by failing to properly assess the evidence given by PW1 and PW3 ignoring the unreliability and the improbability of the said evidence. | The learned Judge erred in law by failing to appreciate the fact that the instant case was based on circumstantial evidence, and therefore failed to apply the principles relating to circumstantial evidence when considering whether each charge has been proven beyond reasonable doubt against each appellant. | The learned trial Judge erred in fact and law by failing to consider the fact that the chain of custody of the substance claimed to have been seized by PW1 was not established beyond reasonable doubt by the prosecution.
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found the prosecution witnesses' evidence highly improbable. The witnesses claimed to have observed the appellants for one and a half hours by hanging on to a window above eye level with louvred slats angled to show only the upper part of the room, while the appellants were allegedly seated on the floor packing heroin. The court found it impossible for witnesses to continuously observe in this manner.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal found the prosecution version highly improbable due to physical impossibility of sustained observation through the window as claimed, making it unsafe to convict on this evidence.
defence_version_summary: All three appellants pleaded not guilty. After the close of the prosecution case, they made unsworn statements from the dock claiming they were arrested on the road and heroin was introduced (fabricated evidence). The defence did not call any witnesses.
legal_errors_description: The High Court Judge failed to properly appreciate the evidence in cross-examination where PW1 stated they had to hang on to the window to observe, and failed to consider the angle of louvre slats visible in photograph P32 which made observation of persons seated on the floor impossible.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal conducted a thorough analysis of the physical evidence and witness testimony regarding the raid. The court focused on the improbability of the prosecution's version, noting that photograph P32 showed a louvred window above eye level with slats angled to show only the upper part of the room. Given that witnesses claimed to observe appellants seated on the floor packing heroin for one and a half hours by hanging on to this window, the court found this physically impossible and highly improbable. The court noted that PW1 and PW3 claimed to have crouched to avoid being seen, yet allegedly hung on to the window taking turns for extended observation. The court also noted the appellants' consistent position that they were arrested elsewhere and evidence was fabricated. Citing Karuppiah Punkody v. Attorney General, the court held that inbuilt improbabilities warranting acquittal existed even taking prosecution evidence at face value. The court found the High Court Judge erred by accepting witness consistency without properly evaluating the physical impossibility of their claimed observations, making it unsafe to convict.
coa_sentence_type: Acquittal - all three appellants acquitted of both charges
brief_judgment_file_summary: Three appellants were convicted in the High Court of Colombo for trafficking and possession of 4.11 grams of heroin under Sections 54A(b) and (d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance and sentenced to life imprisonment. They appealed challenging the credibility and probability of prosecution evidence. The prosecution case relied on two police officers (PW1 and PW3) who claimed to have observed the appellants packing heroin for one and a half hours through a window. The Court of Appeal examined photograph P32 and witness testimony, finding it physically impossible for witnesses to have observed persons seated on the floor through a louvred window above eye level with slats angled upward for such an extended period. The court held the prosecution version was highly improbable and unsafe to convict upon, noting the High Court Judge failed to properly consider the physical impossibility despite cross-examination evidence. The appeal was allowed and all three appellants were acquitted of both charges.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court Judge found PW1 and PW3 consistent in their evidence and concluded based on photograph P32 that witnesses could observe inside the flat by standing on their toes. However, the Court of Appeal found the High Court Judge failed to appreciate the cross-examination evidence where PW1 stated they had to hang on to the window, and failed to consider the angle of the louvre slats and that appellants were seated on the floor, making observation impossible.
precedents_cited_list: Karuppiah Punkody v. Hon. The Attorney General CA 11 of 2005 decided 26-8-2014
standard_of_review_applied: Appellate review of factual findings and assessment of witness credibility, examining whether it is safe to act upon the evidence considering improbabilities

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 54(d) and 54(b) of the Poisons, Opium, and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance as amended by Act No. 13 of 1984
brief_facts_summary: On 9th September 2010, based on information that a Nepalese national was bringing heroin, Inspector Rangajeewa of the Police Narcotics Bureau arrested the appellant near Queens Hotel in Kandy around 1530 hours. The appellant was holding a black bag containing 60 capsules with off-white powder identified as heroin. The appellant had arrived in Sri Lanka within 24 hours of arrest and was staying at a hotel in Kandy. Upon weighing at PNB Colombo, the substance totaled 386.45 grams, with pure heroin content of 79.84 grams confirmed by Government Analyst.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 54(d) of the Poisons, Opium, and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance as amended by Act No. 13 of 1984
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court found the appellant guilty on the first count of possession of 79.84 grams of heroin and sentenced him to death. The appellant was acquitted of the second count of trafficking heroin. The trial judge concluded that the prosecution proved the possession charge beyond reasonable doubt based on credible evidence of the arrest and discovery of heroin in the appellant's possession.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the High Court Judge failed to properly consider contradictions in prosecution evidence, including discrepancies about arrest time and evidence handling. The dock statement was allegedly not considered in proper perspective. The improbability of the prosecution version was not adequately addressed. The judgment allegedly did not conform to section 283 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act as it failed to properly analyze evidence beyond mere summarization.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The learned High Court Judge failed to consider the contradictions inter se and per se of the witnesses for the prosecution, which are contradictions that goes into the root of the case | The dock statement of the appellant was not considered in its correct perspective | The improbability of the prosecution version of events was not considered correctly | The learned High Court Judge's judgment is not in conformity with section 283 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act, hence, not a valid judgment
gnd_other_description: Judgment not in conformity with section 283 of Code of Criminal Procedure Act - failure to properly analyze evidence
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found that alleged contradictions in prosecution witnesses' evidence (PW-01 and PW-02) regarding arrest time, counting of capsules, and other procedural matters did not go to the root of the matter or affect witness credibility. The evidence of PW-09, the hotel manager, corroborated the prosecution version and was considered particularly important as she was not an interested witness.
probability_assessment_by_court: Court found prosecution proved case beyond reasonable doubt. Alleged contradictions were minor and did not affect credibility. Hotel manager's independent evidence corroborated prosecution version. Appellant's dock statement had no basis and was rejected.
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty and made a dock statement in English. He claimed he came to Kandy as a Buddhist pilgrim and was arrested at Kandy City Centre shopping complex, not near Queens Hotel. He alleged he was detained for days, taken to multiple places, forced to sign blank paper, and denied access to lawyer or phone. He denied possessing any illegal substance. He did not give evidence under oath or call witnesses. The court rejected his dock statement as having no basis and found it created no reasonable doubt.
legal_errors_description: The High Court Judge was misdirected as to facts when he determined that the appellant brought the heroin by swallowing capsules and recovering them through faeces, when there was no evidence to support this contention.
procedural_defects_description: PW-01 made several alterations to his crime notebook without countersigning them, though this was found not to affect witness credibility or suggest evidence tampering.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal comprehensively analyzed each ground of appeal. On contradictions, the Court found that minor discrepancies in arrest time and procedural details did not affect witness credibility or go to the root of the case, citing precedent that only material contradictions affecting the basic version matter. The hotel manager's independent corroboration was considered crucial. On the dock statement, the Court held it carried little weight as it was not under oath or cross-examined, and the appellant's claim of pilgrimage had no basis. On the judgment validity ground, the Court found the trial judge properly analyzed evidence and identified points for determination. While acknowledging a factual misdirection about the method of drug concealment, the Court applied the proviso to Article 138, finding no material prejudice to substantial rights as the evidence proved guilt beyond reasonable doubt even without this finding. The Court emphasized that each judge has their own judgment writing style and detailed evidence summary does not constitute improper analysis.
brief_judgment_file_summary: Chet Bahadur Thapa, a Nepalese national, was convicted by the High Court of Kandy for possession of 79.84 grams of heroin found in his possession near Queens Hotel, Kandy on 9th September 2010, and sentenced to death. He was acquitted of trafficking. On appeal, he challenged contradictions in prosecution evidence, improper consideration of his dock statement, and judgment validity. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal, finding that alleged contradictions were minor and did not affect witness credibility, the dock statement was properly evaluated and created no reasonable doubt, and despite a factual misdirection about drug concealment method, no material prejudice occurred as the evidence proved guilt beyond reasonable doubt. The conviction and death sentence were affirmed.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court properly identified the points for determination and analyzed evidence to conclude the prosecution proved possession beyond reasonable doubt. However, the trial judge was misdirected on facts by concluding the drugs were swallowed and passed through faeces without supporting evidence. The Court of Appeal found this misdirection did not prejudice substantial rights as the conviction was supported by overwhelming evidence.
precedents_cited_list: Pantis v. The Attorney General (1998) 2 SLR 148 | The Attorney General v. Sanadnam Pitchi Theresa (2011) 2 SLR 292 | Bogm Bhai Hirji Bhai v. State of Gujarat AIR (1983) SC 753 | Queen v. Kularatne (1968) 71 NLR 529 | James Silva v. The Republic of Sri Lanka (1980) 2 SLR 167 | Mannar Mannan v. The Republic of Sri Lanka (1990) 1 SLR 280 | Lafeer v. Queen 74 NLR 246
standard_of_review_applied: Whether judgment unreasonable or unsupported by evidence; wrong decision on law; or miscarriage of justice. Applied proviso to Article 138 of Constitution - no reversal where error has not prejudiced substantial rights or occasioned failure of justice.

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Sections 54(A)(d) and 54(A)(b) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance as amended by Act No. 13 of 1984
brief_facts_summary: On 06 February 2003, Excise Officers conducted a raid at a house in Peliyagoda based on informant information. They found three appellants seated around a glass pad packaging heroin into 1380 small packets. Officers recovered 51.450 grams of suspected heroin, two blades, and Rs.196,150 in cash from under a mattress. The Government Analyst later confirmed 10.2 grams of pure heroin.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Sections 54(A)(d) and 54(A)(b) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance as amended by Act No. 13 of 1984
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Colombo found all three appellants guilty on both counts of possession and trafficking of 10.2 grams of heroin. The court imposed a sentence of life imprisonment on all appellants on 27 October 2017.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellants challenged the conviction on multiple grounds including that the case relied on uncorroborated evidence with vital contradictions, the trial judge failed to correctly apply the test of probability, failed to consider the legal principle of joint possession, failed to consider that the prosecution failed to establish the production chain, and did not properly consider the dock statements.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The entire case relies on uncorroborated evidence of witnesses and the trial judge has failed to consider the vital contradictions of the prosecution witnesses | The learned trial judge has not correctly applied the test of probability and improbability in order to determine the creditworthiness of the witnesses | The learned trial judge has failed to consider the legal principle of joint possession | The learned trial judge has failed to consider the fact that the prosecution has failed to establish the production chain of the case | The learned trial judge has not properly considered the dock statement
gnd_other_description: Failure to consider legal principle of joint possession and improper consideration of dock statement
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found fatal contradictions in prosecution witnesses' evidence regarding the height of the wall (PW1 said 3-4 feet, PW2 said 10 feet) and method of entry. The court also noted an ambiguity regarding the timeline of production handover to court. These contradictions went to the root of the case.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal found that PW1's evidence failed the test of probability due to an impossible timeline stating suspects were produced to Magistrate Court at 7:25 p.m. on 07/02/2003. The contradictions regarding wall height also failed the probability test.
defence_version_summary: All three appellants pleaded not guilty. After the close of prosecution case, all appellants made dock statements only and closed their cases without calling witnesses. The court's assessment of credibility is not detailed in the judgment.
legal_errors_description: The High Court Judge identified a break in the chain of custody but proceeded to convict despite this fatal error. The Officer-in-Charge who held the production from 7:00 p.m. on 06/02/2003 to 3:35 p.m. on 07/02/2003 was neither mentioned in the indictment nor called as a witness, creating a break in the production chain.
procedural_defects_description: The prosecution failed to call the Officer-in-Charge of the Excise Narcotic Bureau who had custody of the production, and his name was not mentioned in the indictment. This created a fatal break in the chain of custody.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal held that the prosecution failed to establish the chain of custody beyond reasonable doubt, which was fatal to the conviction. The Officer-in-Charge who held the productions was not called to testify, creating a break in the inward journey. The court found vital contradictions regarding the height of the surrounding wall (3-4 feet vs. 10 feet) and method of entry, which went to the root of the case. An ambiguity existed regarding when productions were presented to court. The court applied precedents requiring proof of unbroken chain of custody in drug cases and found the witnesses failed the test of probability. Following the principle in Mahasarukkalige Chandani v. Attorney General, the court held it was unnecessary to address remaining grounds once the chain of custody failure was established.
coa_sentence_type: Acquittal - conviction and sentence set aside
brief_judgment_file_summary: Three appellants were convicted by the High Court of Colombo for possession and trafficking of 10.2 grams of heroin on 06 February 2003 and sentenced to life imprisonment on 27 October 2017. They appealed challenging contradictions in evidence, failure to establish chain of custody, and improper application of probability test. The Court of Appeal found fatal defects: the Officer-in-Charge who held productions was not called as witness creating a break in chain of custody, vital contradictions existed regarding wall height and entry method, and timeline ambiguities failed probability test. The appeal was allowed, conviction and sentence set aside, and all three appellants were acquitted on 16 March 2022.
defence_counsel: Neranjan Jayasinghe for the 1st and 3rd Appellants; Amila Palliyage with Duminda De Alwis, R. Doralagoda and Savani Udugampola for the 2nd Appellant
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court Judge acknowledged in his judgment that there was a break in the chain of custody because the Officer-in-Charge was neither mentioned in the indictment nor called as a witness. Despite this acknowledgment, the Judge proceeded to convict the appellants, which the Court of Appeal found to be a fatal error.
precedents_cited_list: Mohamed Nimnaz v. Attorney General CA/95/94 | Witharana Doli Nona v. The Republic of Sri Lanka CA/19/99 | Perera v. Attorney General [1998] 1 Sri.L.R 378 | Mahasarukkalige Chandani v. Attorney General CA/213/2009 | Albert Deny Kunja v. The Attorney General CA/92/2007
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal applied the standard that in drug-related cases, the prosecution must prove the chain of custody relating to the inward journey beyond reasonable doubt to establish that productions have not been tampered with

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Sections 354 and 365 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The accused respondent was indicted under sections 354 and 365 of the Penal Code. He pleaded not guilty and the trial commenced. Upon conclusion of the trial, the High Court Judge convicted the respondent and sentenced him on three charges involving a victim of tender age.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Sections 354 and 365 of the Penal Code
hc_sentence_type: Charge one: 2 years Rigorous Imprisonment and fine with default term; Charges two and three: 15 years Rigorous Imprisonment each with fine, default term and compensation
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court Judge convicted the respondent on all three charges. The trial judge considered the gravity of the offence, the tender age of the victim and the matured age of the accused in passing the sentence.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner filed an application for revision challenging only the sentence imposed by the High Court Judge. The application was filed to set aside the sentence pronounced on 31.10.2017.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Challenging only the sentence imposed
defence_version_summary: The accused respondent pleaded not guilty and the trial commenced. No further details are provided about the defence version or credibility assessment.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal dismissed the revision application primarily on two grounds. First, the application was filed in September 2019, exactly two years after the sentence was passed in October 2017, and the petitioner failed to explain this delay. The Court noted that delay if unexplained is fatal in revision applications and the petitioner's counsel adopted a lopsided attitude in explaining the delay. Second, the Court found no illegality in the sentence imposed by the High Court Judge, who had properly considered the gravity of the offence, the tender age of the victim and the matured age of the accused. The Court stated that for revision, it must be shown that there are matters which shock the conscience of the Court or that there has been a failure of justice, neither of which was demonstrated.
coa_sentence_type: Original High Court sentence upheld: Charge one: 2 years Rigorous Imprisonment and fine with default term; Charges two and three: 15 years Rigorous Imprisonment each with fine, default term and compensation
brief_judgment_file_summary: This is a revision application filed by the petitioner Disanayake Mudiyanselage Bisomanika challenging the sentence imposed by the High Court of Polonnaruwa on the accused respondent Wanigasinghe Mudiyanselage Seneviratne Bandara. The accused was convicted under sections 354 and 365 of the Penal Code and sentenced to 2 years RI with fine for charge one, and 15 years RI each with fine and compensation for charges two and three. The Court of Appeal dismissed the revision application on two main grounds: unexplained delay of two years in filing the application, and finding no illegality in the sentence imposed by the High Court Judge who had properly considered the gravity of offence, tender age of victim and matured age of accused.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court Judge properly considered the gravity of the offence, the tender age of the victim and the matured age of the accused in passing the sentence. The Court of Appeal found no illegality in the sentence imposed.
precedents_cited_list: CA/PHC/APN/21/2021 | Rajapakse vs. The State 2001 2 SLR 161 | Camillus Ignatius's OIC of Uhana Police Station CA 907/89 | Herath Mudiyanselage Ratnasirs ails Inflame vs. AG CA(PHC) APN 44/2016 | Seylan Bank vs. Thangavelu (2004) 2 SLR 101 | AG vs. J.S. Ulluwaduge and another BASL News 4/2/95 | SC Appeal No 115/2014
standard_of_review_applied: Revision under Article 138(1) of the Constitution - the Court must be satisfied that there are matters which shock the conscience of the Court in the impugned judgment, and it must be shown that irregularity complained of has prejudiced the substantial rights of the parties or has occasioned a failure of justice
sentencing_principles_applied: The trial judge must consider the offence committed, the gravity of the offence, the manner in which it has been committed, the mechanisms and manipulation resorted to, the effect of the offence, the persons who have been affected by it, and the ingenuity in which it has been committed. The trial judge has sole discretion in imposing sentence.

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 54(A)(d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance as amended by Act No. 13 of 1984
brief_facts_summary: On 02/11/2004, IP Ariyaruwan and a police team from the Police Narcotic Bureau conducted a raid in Modera Kimbula Ela area based on information about heroin trafficking. The Appellant was apprehended on an untarred road near a public bathing well when he tried to flee upon seeing the officers. A parcel wrapped in a pink coloured grocery bag containing powder-like substance was recovered from his trouser pocket. The substance tested positive for heroin, weighing 2.45 grams of pure heroin as confirmed by Government Analyst.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 54(A)(d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance as amended by Act No. 13 of 1984
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Colombo found the Appellant guilty of possession of 2.45 grams of heroin on 02/11/2004 and sentenced him to life imprisonment on 08/09/2017. This was a re-trial ordered after the Court of Appeal set aside the first conviction on 17/12/2009 due to improper rejection of alibi defence evidence. The HC found the prosecution had proven the case beyond reasonable doubt.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Appellant raised six grounds challenging the conviction: irregularity in production handling (heroin kept in PW01's personal locker), irregularity in the raid (PW01 not checked before raid suggesting possible planting of evidence), contradictions between prosecution witnesses regarding the place of arrest, contradictions regarding the colour of the bag containing heroin, and alteration of the Government Analyst Report where the word 'not' was corrected in open court.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Irregularity in the production - alleged Heroin was in PW01's personal locker from 9.30 a.m. to 11.16 a.m. creating doubt about integrity | Irregularity in the raid - PW01 was not checked before leaving for the raid unlike other officers, suggesting possible planting of heroin | Contradiction between witnesses regarding place of arrest | Contradiction with regard to the place of incident - PW01 said Appellant was walking along untarred road while PW08 said he was standing at beginning of road | Contradiction with regard to the colour of the bag - witnesses gave different colours for the bag | Alteration of the Government Analyst Report - correction made to remove word 'not' was highly irregular
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The COA found that PW01 IP Ariyaruwan and PW08 RPC Lal Kumara properly corroborated each other's evidence regarding the arrest and recovery of heroin without material contradictions. IP Rajakaruna confirmed receiving the production and handing it to the Government Analyst with seals intact. The Government Analyst's evidence confirming 2.45 grams of pure heroin was reliable despite the correction made in open court.
probability_assessment_by_court: Court found prosecution proved the case beyond reasonable doubt with cogent evidence. Defence evidence from witnesses testifying 13 years after incident failed to create reasonable doubt in prosecution case.
defence_version_summary: The Appellant pleaded not guilty and gave evidence from the witness box. He took the position that he was arrested at his home and nothing was found in his possession, contradicting the prosecution's version that he was arrested on an untarred road. He called two defence witnesses including his sister Premalatha who supported his version. The court found the defence evidence lacked credibility and failed to create doubt in the prosecution case.
