court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal systematically addressed all six grounds of appeal and found them without merit. Regarding chain of custody, the COA held that PW01 properly explained keeping the production in his personal locker until 11.16 a.m. because the reserve officer was not present when the raiding party returned. On the raid irregularity, the court found no evidence of motive to plant drugs as the Appellant was a stranger to PW01. The alleged contradictions about the place of arrest were not material as both witnesses consistently stated arrest occurred on an untarred road. The colour discrepancy regarding the bag was dismissed as the defence had admitted the productions under Section 420. The correction to the Government Analyst Report was properly done in open court with permission and the defence had opportunity to cross-examine. The COA found the HC properly evaluated defence witnesses who testified 13 years after the incident and correctly concluded their evidence failed to create reasonable doubt. The court affirmed that the prosecution proved the case beyond reasonable doubt.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The Appellant Godakandage Nihal Perera was convicted by the High Court of Colombo for possession of 2.45 grams of heroin on 02/11/2004 under Section 54(A)(d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance and sentenced to life imprisonment on 08/09/2017. This was a re-trial ordered after his first conviction in 2009 was set aside by the Court of Appeal. The Appellant appealed raising six grounds including chain of custody irregularities, contradictions in prosecution evidence, and improper alteration of the Government Analyst Report. The Court of Appeal systematically examined all grounds and found them without merit, holding that the prosecution proved the case beyond reasonable doubt with proper chain of custody, corroborated eyewitness testimony, and reliable forensic evidence. The appeal was dismissed and the conviction and sentence were affirmed on 11/03/2022.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court properly evaluated the evidence and convicted the Appellant finding the prosecution proved the case beyond reasonable doubt. The HC adequately considered the defence witnesses' evidence and applied necessary tests to conclude their evidence failed to create doubt in prosecution evidence. The COA found no errors in the HC's analysis and judgment dated 08/09/2017.
precedents_cited_list: Mohamed Nimnaz v. Attorney General CA/95/94 | Perera v. Attorney General [1998] 1 Sri.L.R 378 | Witharana Doli Nona v. The Republic of Sri Lanka CA/19/99 | Bharwada Bhoginbhai Hirjigibhai v State of Gujarat 1983 AIR SC 753 | CA/293/2009
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal scrutinized the evidence and judgment to determine whether the High Court properly found the prosecution proved the case beyond reasonable doubt and whether the conviction was safe.

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna, J. | R. Gurusinghe, J.
offence_sections: Section 364 (I) of the Penal Code as amended by Act No. 22 of 1995 and Act No. 29 of 1998
brief_facts_summary: The accused-appellant was charged with raping his former wife on 20/03/2009. The prosecutrix testified that she went to the Gramasevaka office to collect a certificate, the accused took it and went to his hardware shop, she followed him, and he tied her hands and raped her inside the shop. The accused pleaded not guilty and claimed the sexual intercourse was consensual, stating they had an ongoing sexual relationship after their divorce.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 364 (I) of the Penal Code as amended by Act No. 22 of 1995 and Act No. 29 of 1998
hc_sentence_type: 12 years of rigorous imprisonment and a fine of Rs. 1,000 with default sentence of 2 weeks simple imprisonment, and Rs. 200,000 compensation with default sentence of 1 year simple imprisonment
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Gampaha convicted the accused-appellant of rape and sentenced him to 12 years rigorous imprisonment. The trial judge found the accused guilty and imposed a fine of Rs. 1,000 with a default sentence and ordered compensation of Rs. 200,000 to the prosecutrix with a default sentence of 1 year simple imprisonment.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The accused-appellant challenged the conviction on multiple grounds including that the prosecutrix was not a reliable witness, the High Court Judge failed to fairly evaluate the defence evidence, the conviction should not stand with inadequate and unreliable evidence, the Judge failed to evaluate the entirety of evidence and give reasons for his decision, and the rejection of the investigating officer's observations was erroneous.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The prosecutrix is not a reliable witness | The learned High Court Judge failed to fairly evaluate the evidence of the defence | The conviction of the appellant should not be allowed to stand with inadequate and unreliable evidence | The learned High Court Judge has failed to evaluate the entirety of the evidence and give reasons for his decision | The rejection of the investigating officer's observation is erroneous
gnd_other_description: Failure to evaluate entirety of evidence and give reasons for decision
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The prosecution called six witnesses including the prosecutrix, Gramasevaka, medical officer, police officers, and court interpreter. The Gramasevaka was treated as an adverse witness but his evidence corroborated the defence position. The investigating officer's observations created doubt about the prosecution version, noting no signs of struggle at the crime scene and expressing doubt about whether the sexual intercourse was consensual.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal found the prosecutrix's version improbable and the defence version more likely to have happened, noting multiple infirmities in the prosecution case and that the prosecution failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt.
defence_version_summary: The accused pleaded not guilty and testified that the sexual intercourse was consensual. He claimed he and the prosecutrix frequently had sex after their divorce, they were in contact by telephone, and she had informed him the day before that she was coming. He suggested she falsely implicated him either because they were seen by a friend of her father or because she was involved with another person at the time. The court found his version credible despite some omissions in his police statement.
legal_errors_description: The trial judge failed to evaluate the entirety of the evidence, failed to give adequate reasons for the decision, failed to fairly evaluate the defence evidence, and erroneously rejected the investigating officer's observations without valid reason thereby misdirecting himself and erring in law.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found multiple serious defects in the prosecution case and the trial judge's evaluation. The prosecutrix's behavior was deemed improbable - she did not question why the Gramasevaka allowed the accused to take her certificate, she followed the accused to multiple locations, and her claim of being frightened by a mere threat was implausible. The Gramasevaka, though treated as hostile, provided evidence corroborating the defence that the parties had an ongoing relationship. The investigating officer's observations noting no signs of struggle at the crime scene and expressing doubt about consent were erroneously rejected by the trial judge. The Court held that conviction on uncorroborated testimony of a sexual offence victim is dangerous when the evidence lacks credibility. The trial judge failed to properly evaluate the defence evidence and failed to adequately explain why he believed the prosecutrix. Applying precedents on credibility assessment and reasonable doubt, the Court of Appeal concluded the prosecution failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt and the conviction could not stand on unreliable evidence.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This is an appeal against a High Court of Gampaha conviction for rape of the accused-appellant's former wife on 20/03/2009. The High Court sentenced the accused to 12 years rigorous imprisonment, a fine of Rs. 1,000, and Rs. 200,000 compensation. The appeal challenged the reliability of the prosecutrix's evidence and the trial judge's evaluation. The Court of Appeal found the prosecutrix's testimony improbable and uncreditworthy, noting her unexplained behavior of following the accused to multiple locations and the lack of physical evidence of struggle. The Gramasevaka corroborated the defence version of an ongoing relationship, and the investigating officer's observations expressed doubt about the non-consensual nature of the act. The Court held the trial judge failed to properly evaluate all evidence and give adequate reasons. Applying precedents on the danger of convicting on uncorroborated testimony in sexual offences when credibility is doubtful, the Court of Appeal allowed the appeal, quashed the conviction, and ordered the accused-appellant's release.
defence_counsel: Saliya Pieris PC with Manujay de Silva
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court convicted the accused based on the prosecutrix's testimony. However, the Court of Appeal found the trial judge failed to evaluate the entirety of the evidence, failed to give adequate reasons for believing the prosecutrix, failed to fairly evaluate the defence evidence, and erroneously rejected the investigating officer's observations without valid reason.
precedents_cited_list: King v. Fernando 46 NLR 254 | Keshoram Bora v. The State of Assam 1978 AIR 65-SC-1096 | K. Padmatillake Alias Sergeant Elpitiya Vs. The Director-General, Commissioner to Investigate Allegations of Bribery and Corporation 2010 BLR 67 | Sunil and Another v. The Attorney General 1986 (1) SLR 230 | Ariyadasa Vs Queen 68 CLW 97 | Martin vs Queen 69 CLW 21 | Wijepala vs The Attorney General 2001 (2) SLR 45 | Rex v. Manning (1969) 53 Criminal Appeal reports 150 | Inoka Gallage Vs. Kamal Addararachchi and Other 2002 (1) SLR 307 | R Vs R.E.M 2008 SCC 51 | Ibrahim Thayagarajah Vs Inspector of Police, Rathnapura 59 NRL 335 | Moses vs State 1999 (3) SLR 401
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the prosecution proved the case beyond reasonable doubt and whether the trial judge properly evaluated all evidence and gave adequate reasons for the decision.

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna J. | R. Gurusinghe J.
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: On 01.01.2008, the accused-appellant shot and killed Member of Parliament Thangaraja Maheshwaran at Ponnambalam Vaneshwara Kovil, Kotahena, while the deceased was worshipping. The personal security officer of the deceased, PC Dharmasiri Perera, heard gunshots, returned fire, and chased the assailant who dropped a weapon while trying to escape. The accused-appellant was arrested at the hospital after being shot during the chase, wearing a pink shirt and found with injuries consistent with gunshot wounds.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Colombo convicted the accused-appellant on Count 1 for the murder of Thangaraja Maheshwaran and imposed the death sentence. The accused-appellant was acquitted on Count 2 concerning the death of Chandrakumar Mahindan. The court relied on the testimony of eyewitnesses, forensic evidence linking the accused to the weapon, and DNA evidence showing the accused's blood on the trigger of the recovered firearm.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The accused-appellant argued that the finding of guilt was contrary to the weight of evidence and that the prosecution failed to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt. The appellant contended the trial judge overlooked favorable evidence, failed to properly assess contradictions in eyewitness testimony, and gave undue weight to the prosecution case. The appellant also argued that the identification evidence was weak and the trial judge misdirected on the burden of proof.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The finding of guilt is contrary to the weight of evidence led in this case | The prosecution failed to prove the case beyond a reasonable doubt | The learned High Court Judge appears to have overlooked several items of evidence favourable to the accused-appellant | The learned High Court Judge in relying on the evidence of P.C. 52152 Dharmasiri Perera to find the accused-appellant guilty of murder, appears to have overlooked his evidence when he stated that he was unable to mention how the deceased Maheshwaran was murdered on 01.01.2008 | The learned High Court Judge failed to take into account contradictions in the evidence of eyewitness Gananathan Nithiyanandan | The learned High Court Judge failed to observe that neither a gun nor a pistol was shown to witnesses to prove identity of the weapon | The learned High Court Judge failed to observe that factual evidence given by Gananathan Nithiyanandan conflicts with evidence of Dr Ananda Samarasekara regarding shooting distance | The learned High Court Judge failed to observe that bloodstains found on the firearm and clothes worn by the accused-appellant were not linked to the bloodstains of the deceased | The learned High Court Judge, by giving undue importance to the prosecution case, misdirected on the burden of proof in criminal cases
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found that the eyewitness testimony of PC Dharmasiri Perera and Gananathan Nithiyanandan was credible and corroborated by forensic evidence. The court noted that witness PW3 had been threatened by persons to prevent him from testifying, which explained his initial reluctance to identify the accused. The court distinguished between core contradictions and minor inconsistencies, finding that the contradictions did not damage the core of the prosecution case.
probability_assessment_by_court: The court concluded that the prosecution proved the case beyond reasonable doubt through both direct and circumstantial evidence, including eyewitness testimony, DNA evidence linking the accused's blood to the weapon trigger, forensic matching of bullets to the recovered weapon, and the accused's own evidence corroborating key aspects of the prosecution case.
defence_version_summary: The accused-appellant pleaded not guilty and gave evidence from the witness box. He testified that being a Catholic, he went to the Hindu temple on 01.01.2008 around 8.30 am, heard gunshots, started running with other people, was injured, came out of the Kovil and fainted near Jampetta Street, Kotahena, and was taken to hospital where he was arrested. The court found that the accused's own evidence corroborated the prosecution case regarding his presence, injury inside the Kovil, and identification of the shirt he wore.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal comprehensively reviewed the evidence and found that the prosecution established guilt beyond reasonable doubt. The court held that PC Dharmasiri Perera's evidence was credible despite the appellant's challenges, noting that as a police officer he would have recognized gunshots and responded appropriately. The court found witness Gananathan Nithiyanandan's initial failure to identify the shooter to police was explained by fear and threats he received. The court rejected arguments regarding contradictions in evidence about shooting distance, noting the witness never testified about distance. The court found the forensic evidence compelling, including DNA evidence linking the accused's blood to the weapon trigger and ballistic evidence matching bullets from the deceased to the recovered weapon. The court held that the trial judge properly considered both prosecution and defence cases, correctly evaluated direct and circumstantial evidence, and did not misdirect on the burden of proof. The court emphasized that the accused's own testimony corroborated key prosecution evidence regarding his presence, injury location, and clothing. The court applied principles from Attorney-General vs Segulebbe Latheef regarding fair trial rights and found no violations. The court distinguished between material contradictions and minor inconsistencies, holding that contradictions marked did not harm the core prosecution case given the witness's mental state and surrounding circumstances.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused-appellant Jonson Collin Valentino was convicted by the High Court of Colombo on 27.08.2012 for murdering Member of Parliament Thangaraja Maheshwaran on 01.01.2008 at Ponnambalam Vaneshwara Kovil, Kotahena, and sentenced to death under Section 296 of the Penal Code (acquitted on second murder count). The appellant appealed arguing the conviction was against the weight of evidence, citing contradictions in eyewitness testimony, inadequate identification evidence, and misdirection on burden of proof. The Court of Appeal comprehensively reviewed the evidence including testimony from 18 prosecution witnesses, forensic evidence linking the accused's DNA to the weapon, ballistic matching, and found the prosecution proved guilt beyond reasonable doubt through both direct and circumstantial evidence. The court held that contradictions did not damage the core case, the trial judge properly evaluated evidence from both sides without procedural violations or denying fair trial rights, and the accused's own testimony corroborated key prosecution facts. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal and affirmed both conviction and death sentence on 10.03.2022.
defence_counsel: Dr Ranjith Fernando with Champika Monarawila AAL
cause_of_death_exact_text: gunshot injuries
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court evaluated the evidence from 18 prosecution witnesses and the accused-appellant's testimony, finding the prosecution proved guilt beyond reasonable doubt. The trial judge found the accused guilty on Count 1 for murder of Thangaraja Maheshwaran and imposed the death sentence, while acquitting on Count 2. The Court of Appeal found that the High Court judge properly considered both prosecution and defence cases, evaluated direct and circumstantial evidence appropriately, and committed no misdirection on burden of proof or other legal errors.
precedents_cited_list: The Attorney-General vs Segulebbe Latheef and Another 2008 (1) SLR 225
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the prosecution proved the case beyond reasonable doubt, evaluated the credibility and reliability of witness testimony, assessed whether contradictions were material or harmless, and examined whether the trial judge properly considered evidence from both parties and correctly applied principles of burden of proof in criminal cases.

judges: K. Priyantha Fernando, J (P/CA) | Wickum A. Kaluarachchi, J
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code read with Section 32 (Count 4 - Murder) and Section 380 of the Penal Code read with Section 32 (Count 5 - Robbery)
location_of_offence: Sporting Times betting center, first floor of a building
brief_facts_summary: Four persons entered a betting center named 'Sporting Times' on 20.04.2005, forced open the safe in the manager's room and took money. One person shot at the deceased who was inside the room. The telephone receiver was pulled out and thrown away. The accused-appellant's fingerprints and palm print were found on the telephone receiver. The appellant made a dock statement claiming he kept the receiver on the table after a commotion, but the receiver was found under the cashier's table.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code read with Section 32 (Count 4 - Murder) and Section 380 of the Penal Code read with Section 32 (Count 5 - Robbery)
hc_sentence_type: Death sentence for Count 4 (murder) and 10 years rigorous imprisonment for Count 5 (robbery)
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court acquitted the 2nd accused from all five charges. The 1st accused-appellant was convicted for the 4th count (murder) and 5th count (robbery). The High Court imposed death sentence for the murder count and ten-year rigorous imprisonment with a fine of Rs. 10,000 for the robbery count.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant's grounds were based on three main contentions: (1) common murderous intention on the part of the appellant had not been proved by the prosecution; (2) the circumstantial evidence against the appellant was not properly evaluated; (3) the learned High Court judge failed to evaluate the contradictions in prosecution witnesses.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: A common murderous intention on the part of the appellant had not been proved by the prosecution | The circumstantial evidence against the appellant was not properly evaluated | The learned High Court judge failed to evaluate the contradictions in prosecution witnesses
gnd_other_description: Dock statement not properly considered by the High Court
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: PW3 identified the appellant in an identification parade but stated the person had a similar face. Expert fingerprint evidence established the appellant's fingerprints and palm print on the telephone receiver. PW3 testified the appellant shot at the television, which was never challenged in cross-examination. The Court held that combining fingerprint evidence with PW3's identification established the appellant's identity and presence at the crime scene.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court held that the circumstances relied upon by the prosecution were consistent only with the guilt of the accused-appellant and inconsistent with any reasonable hypothesis of his innocence. The only inference that could be drawn was that the appellant committed both robbery and murder.
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty. He made a dock statement claiming that on the date, he went to the betting center, there was a commotion, the telephone fell down, and he kept it on the table. No defence witnesses were called. The Court rejected the dock statement as unbelievable because the receiver was found under the table, not on the table.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal examined whether the conviction for robbery and murder was correct. The COA found that the unchallenged fingerprint evidence proved the appellant pulled out the telephone receiver, establishing his participation in the robbery. The identification parade identification by PW3, though not precise, was corroborated by fingerprint evidence. PW3's testimony that the appellant shot at the television was never challenged in cross-examination. The Court concluded that as the appellant was the only one with a gun and the deceased died of gunshot injuries, the only inference was that the appellant shot the deceased. Even if the shot at the television accidentally hit the deceased, the appellant would be guilty under the fourth limb of Section 294 of the Penal Code, as firing a gun in a room with several people created imminent danger of death. The Court held the circumstantial evidence was consistent only with guilt and inconsistent with innocence. The conviction and sentence were upheld.
coa_sentence_type: Death sentence for Count 4 (murder) and 10 years rigorous imprisonment for Count 5 (robbery)
brief_judgment_file_summary: Andra Hendige Upul Nilantha alias Rana was convicted by the High Court of Kegalle for murder and robbery committed on 20.04.2005 at a betting center. Four persons entered the premises, forced open a safe, took money, and one person shot the deceased. The appellant's fingerprints were found on the telephone receiver that was pulled out during the robbery. He appealed arguing that common murderous intention was not proved, circumstantial evidence was not properly evaluated, and contradictions were not considered. The Court of Appeal held that fingerprint evidence combined with eyewitness testimony established the appellant's participation in the robbery. The Court found the appellant was the person who shot at the television and, being the only one armed with a gun, must have shot the deceased. Even if accidental, it would fall under the fourth limb of Section 294. The appeal was dismissed and the conviction and sentence upheld.
defence_counsel: Saliya Peiris PC with Pasindu Thilakarathne
cause_of_death_exact_text: Gunshot injuries
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court convicted the appellant for murder and robbery based on circumstantial evidence. The Court of Appeal noted that the High Court took a slightly different view in convicting the appellant for murder, but held that the High Court's conclusion that the appellant was guilty of murder was correct in law.
precedents_cited_list: Podisingho Vs. King - 53 NLR 49 | Gunawardane Vs. The Republic of Sri Lanka - (1981) 2 Sri L.R 315 | Don Sunny Vs. Attorney General – (1998) 2 Sri L.R 1
standard_of_review_applied: The Court applied the standard for circumstantial evidence that the circumstances relied upon must be consistent only with the guilt of the accused and inconsistent with any reasonable hypothesis of innocence
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court held that the sentence imposed by the High Court was neither illegal nor wrong in principle. When considering the nature of the crime, the sentence was not excessive. For robbery under Section 380 of the Penal Code, rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and a fine is prescribed.

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna J. | R. Gurusinghe J.
offence_sections: Section 365 b(2)b of The Penal Code as Amended by Act No 22 of 1995, 29 of 1998 and 16 of 2006
brief_facts_summary: The accused-appellant was indicted for grave sexual abuse of a child under sixteen years of age named Herath Mudiyanselage Akila Sanduni Kumari. The offences occurred between July 2007 and June 2008 at Naragala. The accused committed cruel sex by shaking his male genitals keeping them between the victim's thighs on multiple occasions.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 365 b(2)b of The Penal Code as Amended by Act No 22 of 1995, 29 of 1998 and 16 of 2006
hc_sentence_type: 30 years Rigorous Imprisonment (10 years per count, consecutive), Fine Rs. 7,500 total, Compensation Rs. 225,000 total
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Chilaw convicted the accused-appellant on all three counts of grave sexual abuse. The court sentenced him to 10 years rigorous imprisonment on each count with fines and compensation totaling Rs. 225,000 to be paid to the prosecutrix. The sentences were to run consecutively totaling 30 years imprisonment.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The accused-appellant did not challenge the conviction but only appealed against the sentence imposed by the High Court. The defence argued that the accused was 63 years old with no previous convictions and a father of two children. Defence counsel requested the court to impose 10 years rigorous imprisonment on each count to run concurrently instead of consecutively.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The sentence of 30 years consecutive imprisonment is excessive | The accused is 63 years of age with no previous convictions | The accused is a father of 2 children | Request for concurrent sentences of 10 years rigorous imprisonment on each count
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The judgment does not discuss witness evidence or evidence evaluation in detail. The appeal focused solely on sentencing and not on conviction or evidentiary issues.
defence_version_summary: The accused did not challenge the conviction. Defence counsel informed the court that the accused is 63 years of age with no previous convictions and is a father of 2 children. The defence requested concurrent rather than consecutive sentences.
legal_errors_description: The High Court failed to apply the single transaction rule requiring concurrent sentences when multiple offences arise from the same transaction. The court imposed consecutive sentences without justification.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal analyzed the case based on the single transaction rule established in precedent. The court found that the three offences were committed under the same transaction and therefore consecutive sentences were inappropriate. The court applied the principle from MOHD. Akhtar Hussain case that when a given transaction constitutes offences, concurrent sentences are proper and legitimate. The prosecution did not object to the application for concurrent sentences. Considering the circumstances including the accused's age of 63 years, absence of previous convictions, and family status, the Court of Appeal modified the sentence to run concurrently while maintaining the individual sentence lengths and fine and compensation amounts.
coa_sentence_type: 10 years Rigorous Imprisonment on each count to run concurrently with effect from 08.11.2018, Fine Rs. 7,500 total, Compensation Rs. 225,000 total
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused-appellant Heen Kenda Mudalige Chandrarathne was convicted by the High Court of Chilaw for three counts of grave sexual abuse of a child under sixteen years at Naragala between July 2007 and June 2008. The High Court sentenced him to 30 years rigorous imprisonment consecutive, with fines and Rs. 225,000 compensation. The appellant challenged only the sentence, arguing he was 63 years old with no prior convictions and a father of two children. The Court of Appeal found the High Court erred by imposing consecutive sentences when the offences arose from the same transaction. Applying the single transaction rule, the Court of Appeal modified the sentence to 10 years rigorous imprisonment on each count to run concurrently, while maintaining the fines and compensation.
defence_counsel: Mahinda Jayawardena AAL with Champika Monarawila AAL
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court convicted the accused on all three counts and imposed consecutive sentences totaling 30 years. The Court of Appeal found this was an error because the High Court failed to consider that the three offences were committed under the same transaction, which required concurrent rather than consecutive sentences under established legal principles.
precedents_cited_list: MOHD. Akhtar Hussain Alias Ibrahim Ahmed Bhatti Vs. Assistant Collector of Customs (Prevention) Ahmedabad & ORS (1988) 1988 AIR 2143, 1988 SCC (4) 183, 1988 SCALE (2)552, 1988 SCR Supl. (2) 747 and JT 1988 (3) 586
standard_of_review_applied: Appeal in terms of section 331 of the Code of Criminal procedure (amendment) Act No 21 of 1988 read with article 138 of the Constitution
sentencing_principles_applied: The single transaction rule for concurrent sentences as established in MOHD. Akhtar Hussain case. When a given transaction constitutes multiple offences, it is proper and legitimate to have concurrent sentences rather than consecutive sentences.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera, J. | Neil Iddawala, J.
offence_sections: Section 67(C) of the Electricity Act No 19 of 1950
brief_facts_summary: The two accused were charged in Magistrate Court of Kandy under separate charge sheets for fraudulently consuming electricity. After pleading not guilty, they were initially discharged on 05.05.2014 due to complainant's lackadaisical conduct. The case was reopened on 27.10.2014, trial proceeded in absentia, and prosecution closed their case. The accused pointed to errors in the charge sheet and requested discharge under Section 186 CPC. The Magistrate discharged both accused on 30.12.2015 on the basis that charges could not be maintained.
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court overruled a preliminary objection raised by the accused-respondents on 05.06.2018, which contended that the appeal filed by the Attorney General against the Magistrate Court order dated 30.12.2015 was improperly constituted. The High Court directed both parties to proceed with the appeal.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The accused-respondents filed a revision application against the High Court order dated 05.06.2018. They argued that the appeal filed by the Attorney General on 01.02.2016 was improperly constituted because the Attorney General was not named as a party initially. They contended the appeal was without written sanction of the Attorney General as required under Section 318 CPC and that the subsequent amendment adding the Attorney General as a party on 07.07.2016 meant the appeal was time-barred.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The appeal filed by the Attorney General against the Magistrate Court order dated 30.12.2015 was improperly constituted as the Attorney General was not initially named as a party | The appeal was filed without written sanction of the Attorney General as required under Section 318 of the CPC | The amendment on 07.07.2016 adding the Attorney General as a party constituted a fresh appeal and was therefore time-barred
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court analyzed whether the Magistrate's order of 30.12.2015 was a discharge or an acquittal. Although the Magistrate used the term 'discharge', the Court found that the words employed were identical to the proviso to Section 186 CPC which speaks of acquittal. The prosecution had closed their case and the Magistrate recorded reasons that further proceedings would not result in conviction.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Magistrate determined that further proceedings would not result in conviction of the accused based on insufficient evidence presented by the prosecution.
defence_version_summary: The accused pleaded not guilty initially. During trial, the accused were represented and pointed to an error in the charge sheet, requesting discharge under Section 186 of CPC. No specific details about defence witnesses or alibi are mentioned in the judgment.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal analyzed two key issues: whether the Magistrate's order was a discharge or acquittal, and whether the appeal was properly constituted. The COA held that despite the Magistrate using the terminology 'discharge', the order was tantamount to an acquittal under Section 186 CPC proviso, citing Dyson v Khan 31 NLR 136. On the second issue, the COA agreed with the High Court's analysis that the Attorney General filed the appeal under Section 320(2) CPC, which empowers the Attorney General to file appeals despite not being named as a party in the Magistrate Court. The COA found that the State Counsel appearing in official capacity was presumed to be acting under the Attorney General's direction. The amendment adding the Attorney General to the caption on 07.07.2016 was merely to clarify the caption and did not constitute a fresh appeal. The COA held the appeal was filed within 27 days and not time-barred. The Court emphasized that it is a court of law, not an academy of law, and should not be trammeled by technical and frivolous objections, citing Velupillai v Chairman U. C. Jaffna 39 NLR 464.
brief_judgment_file_summary: This is a revision application filed by two accused against a High Court order that dismissed their preliminary objection. The accused were charged in Magistrate Court for fraudulently consuming electricity under Section 67(C) of the Electricity Act. The Magistrate discharged them on 30.12.2015 after finding insufficient evidence. The Attorney General filed an appeal, but the accused objected claiming it was improperly constituted. The Court of Appeal held that the Magistrate's order was an acquittal, not merely a discharge, and that the Attorney General's appeal was properly constituted under Section 320(2) CPC despite the initial caption not naming the Attorney General. The COA dismissed the revision application and directed expeditious conclusion of the pending High Court inquiry.
defence_counsel: Nalin Ladduwahetty PC with Thusitha Ranasinghe, Hafeel Fariz and Ms L Ubeysekera
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court analyzed whether the appeal was filed under Section 320(1) or Section 320(2) CPC. The High Court held that the Attorney General filed the appeal under Section 320(2), which grants distinct powers to the Attorney General to appeal despite not being named as a party in the Magistrate Court. The High Court found the appeal was filed within 28 days and was not time-barred. The amendment adding the Attorney General to the caption was merely to clarify matters and did not affect the prescription period or the validity of the appeal.
precedents_cited_list: Dyson v Khan 31 NLR 136 | Velupillai v Chairman U. C. Jaffna 39 NLR 464
standard_of_review_applied: Revisionary jurisdiction under Article 138 of the Constitution to ascertain whether the impugned High Court order may be intervened with.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Sections 380 and 383 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The accused respondents were armed with firearms and entered a place of business around 3 in the afternoon. They tied the sales girls and robbed electronic items belonging to the place. Some of the robbed items were later recovered and returned to the owners.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Sections 380 and 383 of the Penal Code
hc_sentence_type: 1st respondent: 12 months RI suspended for 10 years with fine of Rs 2500 on each charge; 2nd and 3rd respondents: 2 years RI suspended for 10 years with compensation
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court Judge accepted the guilty plea tendered midway through the trial. The Judge considered the age, previous convictions of the 1st and 3rd accused, and the fact that some robbed items were recovered and returned to owners. Sentences were suspended imprisonment terms with fines and compensation orders.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner Attorney General challenged the sentence imposed by the High Court Judge on the ground that it was grossly inadequate given the gravity of the offence. The revision application sought to set aside the judgment on the basis that the sentence was too lenient.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The sentence imposed by the learned High Court Judge is grossly inadequate
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court noted that lay witnesses and police evidence were led, but the lay witnesses only referred to identification of the accused at a parade and not in Court. The identification of the accused was solely based on the identification parade, raising questions about whether this was sufficient to find the accused guilty.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court considered whether identification at parade alone without in-court identification was sufficient evidence
defence_version_summary: The accused initially pleaded not guilty and trial commenced, but halfway through the trial they tendered a plea of guilty which was accepted by the High Court Judge. No specific defence version or alibi was presented as they changed their plea to guilty.
procedural_defects_description: The witnesses only identified the accused at an identification parade and not in Court during trial, which the Court of Appeal found problematic
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal reviewed the revision application filed by the Attorney General challenging the adequacy of the sentence. While acknowledging the prosecution's argument that the sentence was grossly inadequate given the gravity of the offence involving armed robbery, the Court found significant evidentiary weaknesses. The Court held that witnesses only identified the accused at an identification parade and not in Court, citing precedents that identification parade results do not constitute substantive evidence. The Court distinguished between evidence at trial identifying the accused versus evidence at parade. Considering the non-identification of accused in Court, the time elapsed of nearly 16 years from the date of offence, and the period in remand, the Court found no reason to interfere with the trial judge's sentence despite the gravity of the armed robbery. The revision application was dismissed.
coa_sentence_type: Original High Court sentence upheld: 1st respondent 12 months RI suspended for 10 years with fine Rs 2500 per charge and Rs 100000 compensation; 2nd and 3rd respondents 2 years RI suspended for 10 years with compensation
brief_judgment_file_summary: This revision application by the Attorney General challenged the sentence imposed on three accused convicted under Sections 380 and 383 of the Penal Code for armed robbery. The accused initially pleaded not guilty but changed their plea to guilty midway through trial, and the High Court imposed suspended sentences with fines and compensation. The Attorney General argued the sentence was grossly inadequate given the gravity of the armed robbery. The Court of Appeal dismissed the revision, finding that witnesses only identified the accused at a parade and not in Court, which raised evidentiary concerns. Considering the identification issue, the 16-year lapse since the offence, and the remand period, the Court found no reason to interfere with the trial judge's sentence.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court Judge considered the age and previous convictions of the 1st and 3rd accused as mitigating factors, along with the recovery and return of some robbed items to owners. The Court of Appeal acknowledged the High Court's sentencing rationale but did not find it erroneous, instead declining to interfere based on evidentiary weaknesses in identification and delay considerations.
precedents_cited_list: The Attorney General vs. Joseph Aloysius and others Sri Lanka Law Reports 1992 PAGE 264 | Keerthi Bandara vs. Attorney General 2000 2 Sri L.R. 266
standard_of_review_applied: Revision application standard requiring instances of grave miscarriage of justice which shocks the conscience of the Court
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court considered the gravity of the offence, the evidentiary weaknesses regarding identification, the time period elapsed of nearly 16 years from the date of offence, and the period in remand of the accused

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
offence_sections: Animals Act No. 29 of 1953 sections 3D, 6, 7 and 8(2)
brief_facts_summary: The 1st and 2nd respondents were arrested on 1.12.2015 for allegedly transporting 7 buffaloes without a license and were produced before the Magistrate of Bandarawela. Both pleaded guilty and were convicted and sentenced by the Magistrate. The 4th respondent claimed the vehicle as the purported registered owner, and the Magistrate released the vehicle to him. The 5th respondent was handed over the animals based on documents marked E2 (certificate from Gramasevaka) and E3 (voucher from previous owner).
hc_judgment_summary: The learned High Court Judge dismissed the revision application filed by the petitioners on the basis that there was delay which was not explained and that the petitioners had no locus standi to intervene in the matter.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioners filed a revision application claiming that the learned High Court Judge had followed the wrong procedure in releasing the buffaloes because the order was based on documents E2 and E3 produced by the 5th respondent, which were in contravention of the Animals Act No. 29 of 1953 sections 3D, 6, 7 and 8(2). They cited a gazette dated 26.11.2009 stating that a license is needed to transport cattle.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The High Court Judge followed wrong procedure in releasing the buffaloes | The order was based on documents E2 and E3 in contravention of the Animals Act | The gazette notification requires a license to transport cattle | The petitioners have locus standi as they were given custody of the animals
gnd_other_description: Locus standi issue and improper release of animals in contravention of statutory provisions
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court analyzed documentary evidence including documents E2 and E3 produced by the 5th respondent. The Court found that the voucher E3 did not comply with the form specified in the Animals Act and gazette notification, and thus the 5th respondent had not established ownership as per the provisions of the relevant act.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court assessed that the 5th respondent failed to establish ownership according to statutory requirements, and the Magistrate was misled by improper documentation.
defence_version_summary: The 1st and 2nd respondents pleaded guilty before the Magistrate. The State Counsel appearing for the Attorney General argued that the petitioners did not have locus standi to appear in the matter.
legal_errors_description: The Magistrate founded his order on improper procedure by releasing the buffaloes based on documents that did not comply with the Animals Act provisions and gazette notification requirements. The High Court Judge erred in refusing the petitioners' application despite the procedural violations.
procedural_defects_description: The Magistrate released the animals based on documents E2 and E3 which did not comply with sections 8(1) and 8(2) of the Animals Act No. 29 of 1953 and the gazette notification specifying the form for cattle vouchers.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal noted that while delay in filing revision applications is generally fatal and must be explained, in this case the welfare of animals was in question and the petitioners were canvassing a legal issue fit for consideration despite the delay of nearly 5 months. The Court examined the Animals Act provisions and found that under sections 8(1) and 8(2), it is clearly stated how a cattle voucher should be prepared. The gazette notification also specifies the required form. The Court determined that document E3 produced by the 5th respondent did not fall into that category, and thus the 5th respondent had not established ownership as per the provisions of the relevant act. Regarding locus standi, the Court applied the principles from cited judgments and held that since the petitioners were given custody of the animals and are a welfare organization for animals, they had sufficient interest in the matter and were an aggrieved party. The Court concluded that the Magistrate's order was based on improper procedure making it illegal, and the High Court Judge in refusing the application caused a grave miscarriage of justice.
coa_sentence_type: The instant application for revision is allowed and the orders of the Magistrate and High Court are set aside
brief_judgment_file_summary: This is a revision application filed by office bearers of the International Animal Welfare Trust challenging the High Court's dismissal of their revision application against a Magistrate's order. The 1st and 2nd respondents were arrested on 1.12.2015 for transporting 7 buffaloes without a license, pleaded guilty, and were convicted. The Magistrate released the animals to the 5th respondent based on documents E2 and E3. The petitioners argued this violated the Animals Act provisions. The High Court dismissed their revision application citing delay and lack of locus standi. The Court of Appeal allowed the revision application, finding that the Magistrate's order was based on improper procedure as the documents did not comply with statutory requirements, the petitioners had locus standi as they were given custody of the animals and are a welfare organization, and the High Court's refusal caused a grave miscarriage of justice.
prosecution_counsel: Chathurangi Mahawaduge SC for the State
defence_counsel: Leslie J. Siriweera with A. H. Senevirathna for the Petitioners
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court dismissed the petitioners' revision application on the grounds of delay and lack of locus standi. The Court of Appeal found this to be erroneous, holding that despite the delay, the legal issue warranted consideration given the welfare of animals was at stake, and the petitioners had sufficient interest as they had custody of the animals and were a welfare organization.
precedents_cited_list: CA/PHC/APN 78/2021 | CA(PHC)APN 144/2016 (Dehideniya J) | R.V.Paddington Valuation Office (1996) 1 QB 380 at 401 (Lord Denning) | A.R.Perera and Others vs Central Freight Bureau of Sri Lanka and another (2006) 1 SLR 83
standard_of_review_applied: Revisionary power under Articles 138 and 145 of the Constitution whereby the Court of Appeal can correct any error in fact or in law committed by the High Court or lower Court in the interest of justice

judges: K. Priyantha Fernando, J. (P/CA) | Wickum A. Kaluarachchi, J.
offence_sections: Section 54A (b) and Section 54A (d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance
location_of_offence: Strace Road, slum area near Kamkarupura flats, Colombo
brief_facts_summary: IP Mahendra Ranasinghe and other officers from Colombo Crimes Division conducted a raid in a slum area along Strace Road. The appellant, wearing a sarong and yellow shirt, suddenly appeared from a nearby house. Upon search, police found a pink cellophane bag containing 3.31 grams of heroin inside his shirt pocket. The appellant was arrested and charged with trafficking and possession of heroin.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 54A (d) of the Poisons, Opium and Dangerous Drugs Ordinance
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the appellant on count 2 (possession of heroin) and acquitted him on count 1 (trafficking). The appellant was sentenced to life imprisonment for possession of 3.31 grams of heroin.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant challenged the conviction on three main grounds: the prosecution version fails the test of probability, there were serious infirmities in the chain of production, and the defence evidence was rejected on unreasonable grounds. The defence argued it was improbable for the appellant to approach uniformed police officers while carrying heroin.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The version of the prosecution fails the test of probability | There were serious infirmities in the chain of production | The evidence of the defence has been rejected on unreasonable grounds
gnd_other_description: Unreasonable rejection of defence evidence and failure to properly assess probability of defence version
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The prosecution relied on police witnesses who conducted the raid, including IP Mahendra Ranasinghe. The defence presented sworn testimony from the appellant and his sixteen-year-old son who claimed police found drugs on a clothesline and arrested the appellant when he came home. The Court of Appeal found the trial judge failed to give equal consideration to the defence evidence.
probability_assessment_by_court: Court of Appeal found it improbable for appellant to approach police with heroin if he knew they were present, but considered it possible he did not know. Trial judge rejected defence version on flimsy grounds without proper consideration of probability.
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty. Defence version was that police came to appellant's house and found drugs on a nearby clothesline, detained his sixteen-year-old son, and arrested the appellant when he came home. Both the appellant and his son gave sworn evidence. The Court of Appeal found the trial judge rejected this evidence unreasonably and failed to apply equal standards in assessing defence testimony.
legal_errors_description: The trial judge failed to consider the defence evidence with the same yardstick as the prosecution evidence and took a squint-eyed look at the defence evidence. The trial judge improperly rejected the defence version based on the accused's failure to preemptively present facts to the Magistrate, which violated the presumption of innocence. The trial judge also failed to consider that the defence witness was a sixteen-year-old boy who may have feared police harassment.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal accepted that the chain of custody was properly established as evidence showed clear handling from police to Government Analyst, with reports admitted under Section 420. However, the COA found the trial judge committed grave errors in evaluating the defence evidence. The trial judge improperly expected the accused to preemptively prove his innocence to the Magistrate and rejected the defence version on flimsy grounds. The COA cited James Silva v The Republic of Sri Lanka holding that examining accused's evidence in light of prosecution evidence reverses the presumption of innocence. The COA also cited Dudh Nath Panday stating defence witnesses are entitled to equal treatment. The trial judge unfairly rejected the son's testimony without considering he was only sixteen years old and may have feared police harassment. The COA found merit in the third ground of appeal regarding unreasonable rejection of defence evidence and allowed the appeal.
brief_judgment_file_summary: Kankanam Pathiranage Suraj Kumara was convicted by the High Court of Colombo for possession of 3.31 grams of heroin under Section 54A(d) and sentenced to life imprisonment, while being acquitted on the trafficking charge. The appellant challenged the conviction arguing the prosecution version was improbable, chain of custody had infirmities, and defence evidence was unreasonably rejected. The Court of Appeal found the trial judge committed serious errors by not giving equal consideration to defence evidence and expecting the accused to preemptively prove innocence. The COA held that the trial judge rejected the testimony of the appellant and his sixteen-year-old son on flimsy grounds without proper consideration of probability and the son's age and fear of police. The appeal was allowed and the accused was acquitted.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court convicted the appellant on count 2 for possession and sentenced him to life imprisonment. The Court of Appeal found the trial judge made serious errors by rejecting the defence evidence on flimsy grounds, including expecting the accused to show his shirt to the Magistrate to prove it had no pocket and criticizing the sixteen-year-old son for not reporting to higher authorities without considering his age and fear of police harassment.
precedents_cited_list: James Silva v The Republic of Sri Lanka [1980] 2 SLR 167 | Dudh Nath Panday v. State of Uttar Pradesh (1981) AIR 911 | Don Samantha Jude Anthony Jayamaha v The Attorney General, C.A. 303/2006
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the trial judge properly evaluated defence evidence with the same standard as prosecution evidence and whether the conviction was safe considering the totality of evidence

judges: Sampath B Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 300 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The appellant attacked his mother-in-law Godawatte Liyanage Gnanawathi on 06-07-2010 with an axe from behind, causing injuries to her neck area. The appellant had visited her house and informed her that his wife had eloped with another person, after which the victim showed sympathy and provided him meals before the attack. The attack left the victim unable to work as a manual labourer and dependent on her husband for daily affairs.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 300 of the Penal Code
hc_sentence_type: Four years rigorous imprisonment, fine of Rs 5000 in default 3 months simple imprisonment, and Rs 150000 compensation in default 12 months simple imprisonment
hc_judgment_summary: After trial, the High Court found the appellant guilty as charged for attempted murder. The court imposed four years rigorous imprisonment, a fine of Rs 5000 with default imprisonment of 3 months, and Rs 150000 as compensation to the victim with default imprisonment of 12 months.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant did not challenge the conviction and only appealed against the sentence. The defence counsel pleaded that the sentence should be suspended considering mitigatory circumstances including that the appellant was 60 years old with three children, a farmer by profession, and had no previous convictions.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Sentence should be suspended given mitigatory circumstances | Appellant is 60 years old with three children | Appellant is a farmer by profession with no previous convictions
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The victim PW-01 testified about the attack and its consequences. The evidence revealed that the victim suffered for a long time and was unable to work or earn a living, remaining dependent on her husband for daily affairs even at the time of giving evidence.
defence_version_summary: The appellant did not canvass the conviction and only challenged the sentence. Defence counsel pleaded mitigatory circumstances stating the appellant was 60 years old with three children, a farmer with no previous convictions, and requested suspension of sentence.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal considered the appellant's mitigation plea but held that the nature and gravity of the crime are germane for consideration of appropriate punishment, not just the criminal's circumstances. The court noted that for attempted murder causing serious injuries, the offender could face imprisonment up to 20 years and a fine. The COA found that the High Court Judge had properly considered both mitigatory circumstances and the gravity of the offence. The court emphasized the victim's long-term suffering and inability to work, and found the four-year imprisonment term to be very much adequate. The COA applied precedents on sentencing principles and concluded there was no reason to interfere with the High Court's sentence.
coa_sentence_type: Four years rigorous imprisonment, fine of Rs 5000 in default 3 months simple imprisonment, and Rs 150000 compensation in default 12 months simple imprisonment
brief_judgment_file_summary: Ratnayaka Arachchilage Wijesinghe was convicted by the High Court of Embilipitiya for attempted murder of his mother-in-law on 06-07-2010, having attacked her from behind with an axe causing neck injuries. The High Court sentenced him to four years rigorous imprisonment, Rs 5000 fine, and Rs 150000 compensation. The appellant appealed only against the sentence, not the conviction, pleading for suspension based on mitigatory circumstances. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal, holding that the nature and gravity of the crime warranted the sentence imposed, and that the High Court Judge had properly balanced mitigatory circumstances with the seriousness of the offence.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court Judge properly considered both the mitigatory circumstances presented by the defence and the gravity of the offence in imposing the sentence. The COA found the High Court's analysis and sentencing decision to be appropriate and adequate given the facts and circumstances of the case.
precedents_cited_list: Ravij Vs. State of Rajastan (1996) 2 SCC 175 | M. Gomes Vs. W.V.D Leelaratne 66 NLR 233
standard_of_review_applied: Appellate review of sentence to determine if it was excessive or inadequate considering the gravity of the offence and mitigatory circumstances
sentencing_principles_applied: The nature and gravity of the crime are germane for consideration of appropriate punishment, not just the circumstances of the criminal. A judge should first consider the gravity of the offence as it appears from the nature of the act itself and have regard to the punishment provided in the Penal Code or relevant statute.

judges: K. Priyantha Fernando, J (P/CA) | Wickum A. Kaluarachchi, J
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The Accused-Appellant, a Lance Corporal of the Sri Lanka Army attached to Palali Camp, was charged with shooting and causing the death of Serjeant Major U. V. Wickrmasekara on 21.01.2006. The appellant was seen following the deceased behind a building, and soon after gunshots were heard along with shouting 'බුදු අම්මෝ'. Witnesses saw the deceased lying with blood flowing from chest, hand and the appellant was seen in a firing position with a weapon. The appellant then handed over his weapon to a higher officer.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The learned High Court Judge of Anuradhapura convicted the accused-appellant for the charge of murder by his Judgment dated 12.02.2019. The trial was conducted without a jury as elected by the accused. Eight witnesses gave evidence for the prosecution, and the accused made a dock statement for the defence.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant challenged the conviction on six main grounds including non-compliance with Section 195(ee) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act and Section 48 of the Judicature Act, admission of inadmissible confessionary evidence denying a fair trial, misdirection by casting burden of proof on the appellant, improper assessment of witness credibility and failure to properly evaluate the dock statement, and failure to follow vital rules pertaining to circumstantial evidence.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The requirement of subsection 195(ee) of the Code of Criminal Procedure act has not been sufficiently/adequately complied with | The requirements of the provision of Section 48 of the Judicature Act have not been properly complied with | Inadmissible and prejudicial/damaging evidence to the accused of a confession has been permitted to be recorded and thus denied a fair trial | The learned trial judge has misdirected himself by casting the burden of creating a reasonable doubt on the evidence of the prosecution upon the appellant | The credibility of the prosecution witnesses has not been properly assessed, dock statement has not been properly considered and failed to evaluate the evidence properly | Vital rules pertaining to the circumstantial evidence have not been followed by the learned high court judge
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: Eight witnesses testified for the prosecution. Witness No. 4 was treated as an adverse witness and his evidence was disregarded. The entirety of evidence of witness No. 5 was not considered due to confessionary statement. Witnesses No. 1 (Major Chathura Ranaweera) and No. 3 (Sarath Udawithana) were key witnesses who saw the appellant following the deceased and being in a firing position after gunshots were heard.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court held that considering the entirety of the circumstantial evidence, the only inference that could be drawn is that no one else but the accused-appellant committed the murder. The chain of events was sufficient to exclude third person involvement.
defence_version_summary: The accused pleaded not guilty. After the prosecution case was closed, defence was called and the accused-appellant made a dock statement only without calling witnesses. In his dock statement, he denied the shooting and claimed he went to the barrack to inform about the shooting incident. The court found his dock statement unbelievable and not creating any doubt in the prosecution case.
legal_errors_description: The Court found some observations of the learned High Court Judge were not accurate. The observation that it is essential to create a reasonable doubt on the prosecution evidence by the dock statement was not correct in law. The learned judge also incorrectly observed that the accused-appellant had failed to challenge the prosecution case, which was not correct because the law presumes the innocence of the accused until proved his guilt.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal examined all six grounds of appeal systematically. The COA found that the jury option requirement under Section 195(ee) and Section 48 of the Judicature Act were properly complied with. Although inadmissible confessionary evidence was recorded, the HC judge properly disregarded it, so no prejudice occurred. The COA acknowledged the HC judge made errors in observations about burden of proof but held these did not affect the ultimate findings. The COA thoroughly analyzed the circumstantial evidence and held that the chain of events - the appellant following the deceased, gunshots being heard, the deceased falling with gunshot injuries, and the appellant being seen in a firing position and then handing over his weapon - was sufficient to exclude third person involvement and establish guilt beyond reasonable doubt. The COA also independently analyzed the dock statement and found it unbelievable and inconsistent. Applying the principle from Gunawardena v Republic that circumstantial evidence is like a rope composed of several cords, the COA held the cumulative circumstantial evidence was sufficient to sustain conviction.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The Accused-Appellant, a Lance Corporal in the Sri Lanka Army, was convicted by the High Court of Anuradhapura for murdering Serjeant Major U. V. Wickrmasekara on 21.01.2006 at Palali Camp and sentenced to death. The case was based entirely on circumstantial evidence with no eyewitnesses to the actual shooting. The appellant appealed on six grounds including procedural non-compliance, admission of inadmissible confessionary evidence, misdirection on burden of proof, improper evaluation of evidence, and failure to follow rules on circumstantial evidence. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal, holding that although the High Court Judge made some incorrect observations about burden of proof, these did not affect the ultimate findings. The COA found that the chain of circumstantial evidence - the appellant following the deceased, gunshots and shouting, the deceased falling with gunshot injuries, the appellant being seen in firing position, and handing over his weapon - was sufficient to exclude third person involvement and establish guilt beyond reasonable doubt. The conviction and death sentence were affirmed.
defence_counsel: D. Weerasuriya, PC with Pasan Malinda
cause_of_death_exact_text: fatal brain laceration due to discharged from a rifled firearm
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court based its conviction on circumstantial evidence showing the appellant followed the deceased, was seen in a firing position after gunshots, and handed over his weapon. The COA found the HC judge made incorrect observations about burden of proof - stating it was essential for the dock statement to create reasonable doubt and that the accused failed to challenge the prosecution case - but held these errors did not affect the correctness of the ultimate findings since the circumstantial evidence was sufficient to establish guilt.
precedents_cited_list: Appuhamy Vs. Palis - (1917) 4 CWR 355 | Junaiden Mohamed Haaris Vs. Hon. Attorney General - SC Appeal 118/17 | King Vs. Abyewickrama - 44 NLR 254 | King Vs. Appuhamy - 46 NLR 128 | Podisingho Vs. King - 53 NLR 49 | Gunawardena Vs. The Republic of Sri Lanka - (1981) 2 Sri L.R. 315 | Don Sunny Vs. Attorney General (Amarapala murder case) - (1998) 2 Sri L.R. 1 | The Queen Vs. D.G. de S. Kularatne and 2 others - 71 NLR 529
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal examined whether the infirmities in the judgment affected the findings of the learned High Court Judge and whether the conclusion could be substantiated by the evidence adduced in the case

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna, J. | R. Gurusinghe, J.
offence_sections: Section 140 of the Penal Code; Section 296 of the Penal Code read with Section 146; Section 296 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: A mob including the appellant took the deceased from PW2's house and attacked him. The deceased was made to sit near a kottan tree where PW1 and PW3 claim the appellant stabbed him on the shoulder. Multiple accused allegedly assaulted the deceased at several locations. The deceased died from stab injuries including a fatal chest wound labeled injury 'A' in the post-mortem report.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court discharged all remaining accused except the appellant from all charges. The HC Judge relied on evidence of PW1 and PW3 that the appellant stabbed the deceased on the back near the shoulder causing death. The HC found insufficient evidence that other accused contributed to the death beyond reasonable doubt.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appeal challenged the reliability of eyewitness testimony, particularly highlighting contradictions between PW1's evidence and the Judicial Medical Officer's testimony. The defense argued PW1 saw the deceased walking 20-30 minutes after the stabbing, which contradicted medical evidence that the fatal injury would cause death within minutes. The appeal also challenged PW3's credibility due to a 26-day delay in making a statement and contradictions with medical evidence.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Evidence of PW1 contradicts JMO evidence - PW1 claims deceased crossed road 20-30 minutes after stabbing but JMO states fatal injury would cause death within minutes | Injury 'D' allegedly inflicted by appellant is not necessarily fatal per JMO - fatal injury 'A' has no evidence of who inflicted it | PW3's evidence unreliable - 26 day delay in making statement not reasonably explained | PW3's evidence contradicts JMO - claimed injury to hip but JMO found no such injury | PW1 is interested witness as accused in related murder case involving relative of accused | Evidence of PW1 and PW3 contradicts each other regarding timeline and events | Prosecution failed to establish beyond reasonable doubt which specific accused inflicted fatal injury 'A' | No evidence of common intention among accused
gnd_other_description: Prosecution failed to prove which accused inflicted fatal injury in multi-accused trial
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: Court found significant contradictions between eyewitnesses PW1 and PW3 and medical evidence. PW1's claim that deceased walked 20-30 minutes after stabbing contradicted JMO's evidence that fatal injury causes death within minutes. PW3's 26-day delay in making statement undermined credibility. PW1 identified as interested witness being accused in related murder case.
probability_assessment_by_court: Court found prosecution failed to establish beyond reasonable doubt which accused inflicted fatal injury 'A'. Evidence only showed appellant inflicted non-fatal injury 'D'. Contradictions between eyewitness and medical evidence created reasonable doubt.
defence_version_summary: Plea not explicitly stated. Defense challenged credibility of prosecution witnesses through cross-examination, highlighting contradictions with medical evidence and inconsistencies between eyewitness accounts. Court found defense successful in establishing reasonable doubt regarding which accused inflicted fatal injury.
legal_errors_description: High Court erred in relying on evidence of PW1 and PW3 when their testimony contradicted medical evidence and each other. HC failed to identify which accused inflicted fatal injury 'A' and convicted appellant based only on evidence of non-fatal injury 'D'.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal conducted a detailed analysis of contradictions between eyewitness and medical evidence. The COA found PW1's evidence that the deceased walked 20-30 minutes after stabbing was incompatible with JMO's testimony that the fatal chest injury 'A' would cause death within 5-10 minutes and victim could not sit or walk. The appellant was only connected to injury 'D' on the shoulder, which JMO classified as grievous but not necessarily fatal. The fatal injury 'A' on the chest had no evidence linking it to the appellant. The COA applied the principle from Karupiah Servai vs. The King that in multi-accused trials, prosecution must establish beyond reasonable doubt that the convicted accused was responsible for the offense. The COA found PW1 was an interested witness being accused in a related murder case. PW3's 26-day delay in making a statement failed tests of spontaneity and contemporaneity per Haramanis vs. Somalatha and Jayawardena vs. The State. The respondent's Additional Solicitor General conceded no evidence of common intention and acquittal of other accused was correct. The COA concluded charges not proven beyond reasonable doubt.
coa_sentence_type: Conviction and sentence set aside - appellant acquitted
brief_judgment_file_summary: The fifth accused-appellant was convicted by the High Court of Gampaha for murder under Section 296 of the Penal Code on charges relating to the death of Mohamad Usman Mohomad Housun on April 23, 1994. The HC discharged all other accused but convicted the appellant based on eyewitness testimony from PW1 and PW3 that he stabbed the deceased on the shoulder. The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal on March 3, 2022, finding fatal contradictions between the eyewitness testimony and medical evidence. The JMO testified the fatal chest injury would cause death within minutes and the victim could not walk, contradicting PW1's claim of seeing the deceased walking 20-30 minutes later. The appellant was linked only to injury 'D' which was not fatal, while no evidence identified who inflicted the fatal injury 'A'. The COA acquitted the appellant.
defence_counsel: Anuja Premarathne, PC with N. Rajapaksha and T Muthucumarana
cause_of_death_exact_text: Stab injury to the heart (Injury 'A') causing immediate pressure drop and death within minutes
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court relied on evidence of PW1 and PW3 to conclude the appellant stabbed the deceased causing death. The COA found this analysis flawed as the HC failed to reconcile contradictions between eyewitness and medical evidence and did not identify who inflicted the fatal injury 'A'. The HC incorrectly convicted based on evidence linking appellant only to non-fatal injury 'D'.
precedents_cited_list: Karupiah Servai vs. The King 52 NLR 227 | Haramanis vs. Somalatha [1998] 3 Sri LR 365 | Jayawardena and others vs The State [2000] 3 Sri LR 192
standard_of_review_applied: Beyond reasonable doubt standard - prosecution must establish which specific accused inflicted fatal injury when multiple accused tried together

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna, J. | R. Gurusinghe, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The deceased was transporting wood in a hand tractor along Sanasa Road when two men covering their faces and armed with swords ambushed him. The deceased's daughter PW1 and two other children were in the tractor. PW1 witnessed the appellants chase and strike her father on the neck with a sword. She recognized them as neighbors she had known since childhood. The deceased was taken to hospital where he was pronounced dead.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: After trial, the High Court found both appellants guilty of murder under section 296 of the Penal Code. The appellants were sentenced to death by the learned High Court Judge.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellants challenged the conviction on three main grounds. First, they argued the identification evidence suffered from serious infirmities rendering the conviction unsafe. Second, they contended the Trial Judge failed to evaluate the identification evidence in its correct judicial perspective and did not address inherent weaknesses. Third, they claimed the government analyst report was inadmissible.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The evidence relating to identification suffers from serious infirmities, which renders the conviction unsafe | The Learned Trial Judge has failed to evaluate the evidence relating to the identification in its correct judicial perspective, addressing her mind to the inherent weaknesses in the evidence relating to the identification | The evidence relating to the government analyst report is inadmissible
gnd_other_description: Inadmissibility of government analyst report
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found PW1's evidence reliable despite her being a child witness. She was cross-examined at length and nothing elicited could attribute to mistaken identity. She gave her statement to police on the same day and consistently identified the appellants as neighbors known since childhood. The defence failed to suggest their positions to the witness.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court found the identities of the appellants were proved beyond reasonable doubt. PW1's evidence did not suffer from any infirmity that warranted doubt. The Court rejected arguments about inability to identify due to face coverings, noting the first appellant removed his covering and both were known neighbors.
defence_version_summary: Both appellants pleaded not guilty and made dock statements. The first appellant claimed he was at his wife's parents' house on the day of incident and that police forced him to furnish the knife. The second appellant stated he had worked with the deceased and lost a finger in an accident, that the deceased had enemies, and he had nothing to do with the murder. The Court found these defences were not put to prosecution witnesses and were afterthoughts that did not create reasonable doubt.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal comprehensively analyzed the identification evidence and found PW1's testimony reliable despite her being thirteen years old at the time of incident. The Court cited Tehal Singh case holding that a thirteen-year-old's common sense may be equivalent to an adult. The Court rejected arguments about face coverings, noting the first appellant removed his covering and both were known neighbors. The Court found contradictions were not vital and did not go to the root of the case. The defence's failure to suggest their positions to PW1 indicated false defences. Arguments about medical evidence conflicts were rejected as PW1's testimony was consistent with multiple injuries. The Court upheld the admissibility of the government analyst report under section 414 of the Criminal Procedure Code as amended. The Court concluded identification was proved beyond reasonable doubt.
brief_judgment_file_summary: Two accused-appellants were convicted by the High Court of Gampaha for murdering Paularachchige Mahinda Gerard on May 19, 2009 at Udugampola and sentenced to death under section 296 of the Penal Code. The deceased's thirteen-year-old daughter PW1 witnessed the attack and identified the appellants as neighbors known since childhood. The appellants appealed arguing identification evidence was unreliable due to face coverings and that the government analyst report was inadmissible. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal, finding PW1's evidence reliable and credible despite her age, noting contradictions were not vital, the defence failed to challenge her testimony properly, and the government analyst report was properly admitted under section 414 of the Criminal Procedure Code.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court convicted both appellants and sentenced them to death. The Court of Appeal noted the Learned Trial Judge gave reasons for relying on PW1's evidence and observed that the names of assailants were made known to hospital police post and Gampaha police station at 6.50 pm on the same day with no delay.
precedents_cited_list: Tehal Singh and others vs the state of Punjab AIR 1979 SC 1347 | Gunasiri and two others vs Republic of Sri Lanka 2009 1 SRI LR 39 | Sarwan Singh vs. State of Punjab | Bobby Mathew vs. State of Kamatakal
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the identification evidence was reliable and whether the conviction was safe, examining the circumstances under which identification was made to exclude all probabilities of mistaken identity
