judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: PW01 Premasiri and the deceased left a gambling den around 6.30pm on a motorbike when two persons wearing helmets jumped onto the road near a Kumbuk tree and one attacked the deceased with a sword-like weapon. PW01 ran away due to fear and saw the deceased fall into a paddy field. The deceased sustained six injuries and died from haemorrhagic shock following multiple deep cut wounds. PW01 initially told police the attacker looked like Michael Mudalali's 2nd son, and there was a prior dispute as the deceased had cut Michael Mudalali one and half years before.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Kuliyapitiya convicted the Appellant as charged under Section 296 of the Penal Code for murder and sentenced him to death on 02/05/2017. The trial was conducted as a non-jury trial as the Appellant opted for it, and after considering evidence from both prosecution and defence, the judge found the Appellant guilty.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Appellant challenged the conviction arguing that the prosecution witness Premasiri had several weaknesses in identifying the Appellant, the trial judge failed to consider poor light conditions at the time of incident, the judge accepted prosecution evidence while slighting defence evidence, the judge did not address the unreliable nature of Premasiri's evidence, the judge failed to address contradictions between prosecution witnesses Premasiri and IP Vijitha Kumara, and the judgment was delivered three and half months after conclusion of the case.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The Learned trial Judge has held that the prosecution witness Premasiri had identified the Appellant despite several weaknesses pertaining to the identity of the Appellant | The Learned Trial Judge did not address his mind regarding the light condition that prevailed at the time of the incident | The Learned Trial Judge instead of considering the entirety of the evidence led before him to decide the guilt or innocence of the Appellant, has decided to accept the evidence of prosecution witness Premasiri thereby slighting the defence evidence led at the trial | The Learned Trial Judge did not address his mind regarding the unreliable nature of evidence of prosecution witness Premasiri | The Learned trial Judge has failed to address inter-se contradiction between prosecution witnesses Premasiri and IP Vijitha Kumara | The learned High Court judge had delivered the judgment after three and half months of the conclusion of the case
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found that PW01 Premasiri's identification evidence was weak as the incident occurred during dusk with fading light under a Kumbuk tree, the assailants wore helmets with vizors covering their faces, PW01 ran away immediately when the assault began, and he initially only said the attacker resembled Michael Mudalali's 2nd son. The High Court judge failed to properly consider these circumstances and the poor light conditions when evaluating the identification evidence.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal held that considering the weak identification evidence, particularly the poor light conditions and limited observation time, the identity of the Appellant was highly doubted and the benefit of doubt should be given to the Appellant
defence_version_summary: The Appellant pleaded not guilty, gave evidence from the witness box and called four witnesses on his behalf. The defence challenged the identification evidence arguing poor light conditions and weaknesses in the sole eyewitness testimony made the identification unreliable.
legal_errors_description: The High Court judge failed to properly evaluate the identification evidence, did not adequately consider the poor light conditions described by PW01, failed to assess how long PW01 had observed the Appellant in fading light, and did not properly apply caution required in cases dependent on disputed identification evidence
procedural_defects_description: No identification parade was held. The judgment was delivered three and half months after conclusion of the case.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal extensively analyzed identification evidence principles citing Phipson on Evidence, Alexander v R, Visveswaran v State, and Turnbull guidelines requiring special caution in cases dependent on identification evidence. The COA found that PW01's evidence was insufficient to establish identity beyond reasonable doubt because the incident occurred in fading light under a Kumbuk tree, assailants wore helmets with vizors, PW01 ran away immediately, initially only stated the attacker resembled Michael Mudalali's 2nd son, and the motorbike had a weak headlight. The COA held the High Court judge failed to properly consider the light conditions, duration of observation, and other critical identification factors. The COA found grounds 1 to 5 had merit as the learned High Court judge failed to appreciate the weak identification evidence. Given the highly doubted identity, the benefit of doubt accrued to the Appellant, resulting in acquittal.
brief_judgment_file_summary: Kanugalawatte Shamila Harsha Perera was convicted by the High Court of Kuliyapitiya under Section 296 of the Penal Code for murdering Mudiyanselage Susil Premasiriwardene on 20th April 2014 and sentenced to death. The victim was attacked with a sword-like weapon by two helmeted assailants near a Kumbuk tree during dusk while riding a motorbike. The Appellant appealed challenging the identification evidence, arguing the sole eyewitness PW01 Premasiri's testimony was unreliable due to poor light conditions, helmets covering the assailants' faces, and weak initial identification. The Court of Appeal analyzed identification evidence principles and found the High Court judge failed to properly evaluate the light conditions and limited observation circumstances. Finding the Appellant's identity highly doubted, the COA allowed the appeal and acquitted the Appellant from the murder charge.
defence_counsel: Rienzie Arseculeratne, PC with Eranga Yahandawala
cause_of_death_exact_text: haemorrhagic shock following multiple deep cut wounds to the body
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court accepted PW01 Premasiri's identification evidence and convicted the Appellant of murder sentencing him to death. However, the Court of Appeal found the High Court judge failed to adequately consider the poor light conditions, the fact that assailants wore helmets covering their faces, PW01's limited observation time before fleeing, and the weak initial identification stating the attacker only resembled Michael Mudalali's 2nd son.
precedents_cited_list: Phipson on Evidence (Sweet & Maxwell Thomson Reuters, 17th Edn. 2015) | Alexander v. R (1981) 145 CLR 395 | Visveswaran v State (2003) 6 SCC 73 | Turnbull [1977] QB 224
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal applied the standard requiring that in cases dependent wholly or substantially on disputed identification evidence, courts must exercise special caution and consider all circumstances affecting the reliability of identification including light conditions, duration of observation, distance, and time elapsed

judges: Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 19(b) and Section 19(c) of the Bribery Act
brief_facts_summary: The Appellant, Principal of Wickramaseela Madya Maha Vidyalaya in Giriulla, allegedly solicited and accepted Rs.40,000 from PW01 Deepthi Dammika to enrol her daughter in Grade 01. After PW01's husband lodged a complaint with the Bribery Commission, a detection operation was organized on 18/08/2006. PW01 handed over the money to the Appellant in his office, who placed it in a table drawer, and Bribery officials immediately recovered the money.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 19(b) and Section 19(c) of the Bribery Act
hc_sentence_type: Fine of Rs.5,000 per count with default sentence of 3 months simple imprisonment; 4 months rigorous imprisonment per count (16 months total) suspended for 7 years
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court found the Appellant guilty under all four counts of soliciting and accepting a gratification under sections 19(b) and 19(c) of the Bribery Act. The trial judge imposed a fine of Rs.5,000 per count and 16 months rigorous imprisonment suspended for 7 years. The High Court judgment was delivered on 07/03/2018.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The Appellant contended that the High Court Judge convicted him based on prejudice of 'Bad Character' unsupported by evidence, rejected the evidence given by the Appellant on an incorrect legal basis, failed to consider the evidence given by four defence witnesses, failed to appreciate the defence position, and failed to consider the reasonable doubt that arose in the prosecution case itself.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The Learned High Court Judge has convicted the Appellant based on the prejudice of 'Bad Character' unsupported by evidence | The Learned High Court Judge has rejected the evidence given by the Appellant on an incorrect legal basis | The Learned High Court Judge has failed to consider the evidence given by four defence witnesses | The trial judge has failed to appreciate the defence position | The Learned High Court Judge has failed to consider the 'reasonable doubt' that has arisen in the prosecution case itself
gnd_other_description: Failure to properly consider defence evidence and failure to consider reasonable doubt in the prosecution case
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: PW01 Deepthi Dammika testified that the Appellant solicited Rs.40,000 for her daughter's school admission, and she handed over the money during a detection operation. PW02 WPS Surangani acted as the decoy, and all prosecution witnesses corroborated PW01's evidence. PW07 Mohideen Salahaudeen confirmed that principals were not allowed to take money apart from facility fees for school admissions.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court of Appeal found that the trial judge made adverse character references unsupported by evidence, which affected the fair trial and created prejudice against the Appellant
defence_version_summary: The Appellant pleaded not guilty and gave evidence from the witness box along with four defence witnesses. He claimed he had powers to accept donations for the school under Education Ministry circulars and that he accepted Rs.40,000 as a donation, intending to issue a receipt and deposit it in the school development account. He stated PW01 was in a hurry and could not go to the bank herself.
legal_errors_description: The trial judge made adverse references to the Appellant's character in his judgment, describing him as a person who as a practice requests and obtains money to admit students, and made assumptions about his spending on his children's education abroad. These comments were unsupported by evidence and violated sections 53 and 54 of the Evidence Ordinance regarding bad character evidence.
procedural_defects_description: The trial judge admitted inadmissible bad character evidence in his judgment, which was not part of the evidence adduced by either party and was not put in issue during the trial, causing serious miscarriage of justice
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal held that the trial judge made adverse character references against the Appellant in his judgment that were completely outside the evidence adduced by both parties. These comments violated sections 53 and 54 of the Evidence Ordinance and caused serious miscarriage of justice by prejudicing the Appellant's right to a fair trial. The Court cited Maxwell v. Director of Public Prosecution, The King v. Pila, and Moses v. Queen to establish that bad character evidence, when inadmissible, greatly prejudices the accused and affects the right to a fair trial. The Court further determined that this was not a fit case for re-trial given that 16 years had passed since the incident, the first trial took 12 years, the Appellant had been in remand for 2 months, and the prosecution failed to produce the document supporting cancellation of the Education Ministry circular that the Appellant claimed gave him authority to accept donations.
coa_sentence_type: Conviction and sentence set aside; Appellant acquitted from all charges
brief_judgment_file_summary: The Appellant, Jayasinghe Arachchilage Karunathilaka Jayasinghe, Principal of Wickramaseela Madya Maha Vidyalaya in Giriulla, was indicted for soliciting and accepting Rs.40,000 as a bribe for school admission under sections 19(b) and 19(c) of the Bribery Act. The High Court convicted him on all four counts and imposed a fine of Rs.5,000 per count and 16 months rigorous imprisonment suspended for 7 years. On appeal, the Court of Appeal found that the trial judge made adverse character references against the Appellant in his judgment that were unsupported by evidence and violated sections 53 and 54 of the Evidence Ordinance. This caused serious miscarriage of justice and prejudiced the Appellant's right to a fair trial. Considering the 16-year lapse since the incident, the Court of Appeal allowed the appeal, set aside the conviction and sentence, and acquitted the Appellant from all charges.
prosecution_counsel: Asitha Anthony, Assistant Director General of Bribery Commission
defence_counsel: Amila Palliyage with Duminda De Alwis
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court convicted the Appellant and stated in its judgment that the Appellant was a person who as a practice requests and obtains money to admit students, and that the defence evidence revealed he spent lots of money on his children's costly education abroad which goes over and above earnings from a usual government salary. The Court of Appeal found these adverse character references were completely unsupported by evidence and were assumptions of the trial judge that caused serious miscarriage of justice.
precedents_cited_list: Maxwell v. Director of Public Prosecution (1935) A.C. 309 | The King v. Pila 15 NLR 453 | Moses v. Queen 75 NLR 121 | Nandana v. The Attorney General 2008 (1) SLR 51 | The Attorney General v. Bimbirigodage Sujith Lal SC Appeal 14/2016 | Queen v. G. K. Jayasinghe 69 NLR 314
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the trial judge's admission of inadmissible bad character evidence caused serious miscarriage of justice and prejudiced the Appellant's right to a fair trial
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court considered principles regarding re-trial including the nature of evidence available, time duration since the appeal, period of incarceration suffered, trauma of a second trial for no fault of the accused, and traumatic effect on family members. The Court determined that nearly 16 years had passed since the offence and it was not a fit case for re-trial.

judges: Menaka Wijesundera J. | Neil Iddawala J.
brief_facts_summary: The petitioner was indicted in the High Court of Kuliyapitiya along with seven others for robbery and unlawful assembly. The petitioner's counsel made an application to recall prosecution witnesses numbers 3, 4, and 5 under section 48 of the Judicature Act. The learned High Court Judge refused this application on 16.1.2020.
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court Judge refused the petitioner's application to recall witnesses numbers 3, 4, and 5 under section 48 of the Judicature Act after hearing submissions from both parties.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The petitioner contended that he had made an initial application to recall witnesses before the previous High Court Judge on 14.12.2017 but no order was pronounced before that judge was transferred. The petitioner argued that he should be allowed to recall the witnesses under section 48 of the Judicature Act.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Application to recall witnesses numbers 3, 4, and 5 under section 48 of the Judicature Act was improperly refused | Previous application made on 14.12.2017 before earlier High Court Judge but no order was pronounced before transfer
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The prosecution called at least 5 witnesses in the trial. The petitioner had been represented by counsel throughout and cross-examination of witnesses had taken place. The current counsel came late to the case.
defence_version_summary: Not guilty. The petitioner was represented by counsel throughout the trial. Cross-examination of prosecution witnesses had taken place. The current defence counsel came late to the case and sought to recall witnesses 3, 4, and 5.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal examined section 48 of the Judicature Act which allows an accused to demand that witnesses be resummoned and reheard, but gives the judge discretion to decide 'if he thinks fit'. The Court noted that the petitioner had been represented by counsel throughout and cross-examination had taken place. The current counsel came late to the case but made an application in 2017 which was not ruled upon. The petitioner then waited two years before making another application in 2020. The Court found that the respondent's objection that this was done to delay trial had merit. The Court held that the prime duty of the judiciary is to safeguard rights but section 48 gives discretion to the judge to decide whether recalling witnesses is needed. The Court found no exceptional reason to invoke revisionary powers and concluded that the High Court Judge's exercise of discretion in refusing the application was proper.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The 8th accused-petitioner Rathnayake Arachchilage Amila Priyankara was indicted in the High Court of Kuliyapitiya along with seven others for robbery and unlawful assembly. The petitioner's counsel applied to recall prosecution witnesses 3, 4, and 5 under section 48 of the Judicature Act, but the High Court Judge refused this application on 16.1.2020. The petitioner filed a revision application arguing that a previous application made in 2017 was not ruled upon. The Court of Appeal held that section 48 gives the trial judge discretion to recall witnesses and found no exceptional circumstances warranting interference. The revision application was dismissed on 02.03.2022.
prosecution_counsel: Erandi Dassanaike SC on the instruction of Riyaz Barry SC
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court Judge exercised discretion under section 48 of the Judicature Act and refused the application to recall witnesses after hearing submissions from both parties. The Court of Appeal found no error in this exercise of discretion.
standard_of_review_applied: Revisionary jurisdiction under Article 138(1) of the Constitution - whether there is an exceptional reason which shocks the conscience of the Court to invoke revisionary powers

judges: K. PRIYANTHA FERNANDO, J (P/CA) | WICKUM A. KALUARACHCHI, J
offence_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The accused-appellant was married to PW1 and they had a baby girl named Thinithi Sadithya Sasathmi, born on 26th April 2013. On 15th January 2014, the appellant took the nine-month-old baby from PW1's house on his motorbike around 8.15 p.m. Later that night around 12.15-12.20 a.m., he called PW1 to come to Thekkawatte water tank to collect the baby. The baby was found dead the next morning with her neck almost severed. The appellant had been living separately from PW1 due to constant fights, but visited the baby daily.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court of Tangalle convicted the accused-appellant of murder and imposed the death sentence. The learned High Court Judge considered all relevant aspects of the case and concluded based on circumstantial evidence that the appellant was responsible for the death of the nine-month-old baby.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The defence argued that it was impossible for the appellant to commit such a brutal murder as he loved the baby and visited her daily. The President's Counsel contended that the appellant may have been intoxicated or not in a proper state of mind. Arguments were raised about contradictions in SMS evidence between PW6 and PW1, improper admission of evidence regarding a rape case showing bad character, and that the trial judge pre-judged the case by remanding the appellant mid-trial.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: The impossibility of committing such a brutal murder by the accused-appellant who loved the baby girl | The appellant was intoxicated or not in a normal state of mind | Contradiction between evidence of PW6 and PW1 regarding SMS message | Improper admission of evidence regarding bad character (rape case) | Pre-judgment of the case by remanding appellant during trial | Daughter of PW6 not called as witness and mobile phone details not produced
gnd_other_description: Defence of intoxication raised; improper admission of bad character evidence
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The prosecution case relied entirely on circumstantial evidence with no eyewitnesses to the incident. Key witnesses included PW1 (the mother), PW2 (loku Thaththa who searched for and found the baby dead), PW3 (Anula), and PW6 (who saw someone running with the baby). The JMO provided medical evidence on cause of death. The court found the circumstantial evidence consistent only with the appellant's guilt.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court held that the circumstances proved were consistent only with the guilt of the accused-appellant and not with any other hypothesis. The court found no evidence of third party involvement and concluded that since the appellant took custody of the baby and she was found dead, the only irresistible inference was that the appellant committed the murder.
defence_version_summary: The accused-appellant pleaded not guilty and gave evidence claiming he handed over the baby back to PW2 when an unknown person rushed out with a manna knife. He also claimed he consumed liquor heavily that day. The court rejected this defence as PW2 was never asked in cross-examination whether the baby was handed back to him, and this defence was contradicted by the appellant's own statement to police (contradiction X2) where he said he did not consume alcohol as it was a Poya day. The court found his evidence not credible.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal carefully analyzed all grounds raised by the appellant. On the intoxication defence, the court rejected it due to contradiction X2 showing the appellant told police he did not consume alcohol on Poya day. Regarding the loving father argument, the court noted unchallenged evidence that the appellant asked PW1 to 'cut off her nipples' when she wanted to nurse the baby, showing he was not a loving father. The court found the contradiction in SMS evidence immaterial since the appellant himself called PW1 to come to Thekkawatte to collect the baby, which was unchallenged evidence. On bad character evidence regarding the rape case, the court held it was admissible under Section 9 of the Evidence Ordinance as necessary to explain the quarrels between parties, and it did not prejudice the case as the judge was not influenced by it. The court rejected the pre-judgment argument, holding that remanding under Section 263 on reasonable suspicion was lawful. Most significantly, the court emphasized that the appellant's defence of handing the baby to PW2 was never put to PW2 in cross-examination, and citing Indian precedents, held that failure to challenge this in cross-examination meant the defence could not be raised later. The court concluded that the circumstantial evidence pointed irresistibly to the appellant's guilt with no evidence of third party involvement.
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused-appellant Thotamune Patabadige Chathura Madusanka was convicted by the High Court of Tangalle for murdering his nine-month-old daughter Thinithi Sadithya Sasathmi on 16th January 2014 by severing her neck, and was sentenced to death under Section 296 of the Penal Code. He appealed both conviction and sentence, arguing he loved the baby, was intoxicated, and that there were evidentiary contradictions and procedural errors. The Court of Appeal, in a judgment delivered on 31st May 2022, rejected all grounds of appeal. The court found the intoxication defence contradicted by the appellant's own police statement, rejected the loving father argument based on unchallenged evidence of his behavior, held that contradictions in SMS evidence were immaterial, ruled that bad character evidence was properly admitted, and found no pre-judgment in the remand order. Critically, the court held that the appellant's defence of handing the baby to PW2 could not be accepted as it was never put to PW2 in cross-examination. The court affirmed the conviction and death sentence, holding that the circumstantial evidence pointed irresistibly and exclusively to the appellant's guilt.
defence_counsel: Shavindra Fernando, PC with Umayangi Indatissa and Natasha Wijesekara
cause_of_death_exact_text: Death was due to a fatal cut injury caused to the neck. The main artery that supplies blood to the brain has been cut and the head was almost severed from the neck.
hc_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal noted that the learned President's Counsel fairly acknowledged that he did not intend to point out defects or shortcomings in the High Court judgment because the learned High Court Judge had considered all relevant aspects of the case. The COA found that the High Court Judge carefully evaluated and extensively dealt with all relevant circumstances in a well-considered judgment and correctly concluded that the accused-appellant killed the baby girl.
precedents_cited_list: Junaiden Mohamed Haaris Vs. Hon. Attorney General - SC Appeal 118/17, decided 09.11.2017 | King Vs. Abyewickrama - 44 NLR 254 | King Vs. Appuhamy - 46 NLR 128 | Don Sunny Vs. Attorney General (Amarapala murder case) - (1998) 2 Sri L.R. 1 | Arumugam alias Podithambi V. Range Forest Officer - (1986) 2 Sri L.R. 398 | Jagath Premawardena V. Attorney General - C.A. Appeal 173/2005, decided on 19.03.2009 | Sarvan Singh V. State of Punjab - (2002 AIR SC (iii) 3652 at 3655 and 3656) | Ratnayake Mudiyanselage Premachandra V. The Hon. Attorney General - C.A. Case No. 79/2011, decided on 04.04.2017 | Himachal Pradesh V. Thakur Dass - (1983) 2 Cri. L. J. 1694 at 1701 | Motilal V. State of Madhya Pradesh - (1990) Criminal Law Journal NOC 125 MP
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the circumstantial evidence proved by the prosecution was consistent only with the guilt of the accused and not with any other hypothesis, in accordance with established precedent

judges: Sampath B Abayakoon, J. | P. Kumararatnam, J.
offence_sections: Section 296 read with section 32 of the Penal Code (Count 1) and Section 380 read with section 32 of the Penal Code (Count 2)
brief_facts_summary: The deceased Monika Rasika Kumari Gunathilaka was the pregnant wife of the 1st accused-appellant. On 4 July 1999, the appellant and deceased travelled by bus to Warakathenna bus stop where they were followed by the 2nd and 3rd accused along with accomplice PW-01. The 3rd accused strangled the deceased with a rope and stabbed her with a knife, with the appellant assisting by holding the rope, then they pushed her body into the waterway. The accused then robbed her gold jewellery including a ring, two ear studs and a wrist watch which were sold.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 read with section 32 of the Penal Code (Count 1) and Section 380 read with section 32 of the Penal Code (Count 2)
hc_sentence_type: Death on Count 1 and 3 years rigorous imprisonment and fine on Count 2
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court Judge of Kegalle by judgment dated 03-10-2017 found all three accused including the appellant guilty as charged after a trial in absentia for the 1st and 2nd accused. The court convicted them based on the evidence of accomplice PW-01 who received conditional pardon and circumstantial evidence. They were sentenced to death on the murder count and 3 years rigorous imprisonment with fine on the robbery count.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellant argued that the High Court Judge convicted him for robbery and murder without considering doubts in the prosecution evidence. The defence contended that PW-01 was an unreliable accomplice witness and that material contradictions were not properly considered. The appellant claimed he had no motive to kill his wife with whom he had a happy married life and that the conviction was unsafe.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: When there were doubts in the prosecution evidence, the learned High Court Judge without considering those doubts, has convicted the 1st accused for robbery and murder
gnd_other_description: Unreliability of accomplice evidence and failure to establish motive
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The Court found PW-01's evidence as accomplice was sufficiently corroborated by circumstantial evidence including PW-02 who saw the appellant with deceased before the incident, PW-15 the bus conductor who confirmed they travelled together, and medical evidence confirming strangulation and stabbing. The contradictions marked were found to be immaterial as they related to disposal of robbed items not the actual killing.
probability_assessment_by_court: The Court found that when strong prima facie case has been established with evidence only the accused can explain, the accused must provide reasonable explanation of highly incriminating circumstances. The appellant failed to explain why PW-02 saw him with deceased when he claimed he never met her that day.
defence_version_summary: The appellant pleaded not guilty and made a lengthy dock statement claiming he was never at the scene of crime and was unaware of the murder. He claimed PW-01's evidence was totally false and that the other accused misled an innocent woman. He stated he only learned his wife left home that evening when he returned home at night and that he had a peaceful married life with no reason to kill her. The Court found he failed to explain highly incriminating evidence and did not call any defence witnesses.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal comprehensively analyzed the law relating to accomplice evidence, noting that while conviction on uncorroborated accomplice testimony is legal, it is dangerous practice. The Court found PW-01's evidence was sufficiently corroborated by PW-02 who saw appellant with deceased before the incident, PW-15 the bus conductor, the sister of deceased, and medical evidence confirming manner of death. The contradictions marked were found immaterial as they related to disposal of stolen items not the killing itself. The Court found strong circumstantial evidence including appellant's subsequent conduct in requesting PW-02 not to reveal seeing them together, his failure to explain being seen with deceased when he claimed ignorance, and his extramarital affair with PW-12 establishing motive. The Court held that when strong prima facie case is established with evidence only accused can explain, failure to cross-examine witnesses on material points and failure to provide reasonable explanation justified conviction. The Court applied the Ellenborough dictum and found common intention was established beyond reasonable doubt.
coa_sentence_type: Death on Count 1 and 3 years rigorous imprisonment and fine on Count 2
brief_judgment_file_summary: The 1st accused-appellant Gamage Chandrakumara was convicted along with two co-accused by the High Court of Kegalle for the murder and robbery of his pregnant wife Monika Rasika Kumari Gunathilaka on 4 July 1999. The High Court sentenced them to death for murder and 3 years rigorous imprisonment with fine for robbery. The appellant appealed arguing the accomplice PW-01 was unreliable and contradictions were not considered. The Court of Appeal found PW-01's evidence was sufficiently corroborated by circumstantial evidence, medical evidence, and testimony of witnesses who saw the appellant with deceased before her death. The Court found the appellant failed to explain highly incriminating evidence including being seen with the victim when he claimed ignorance, his request to a witness not to reveal seeing them together, and his extramarital affair establishing motive. The appeal was dismissed and conviction and sentence affirmed.
cause_of_death_exact_text: shock following strangulation and cut injuries
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court Judge properly considered the law relating to accomplice evidence and found PW-01's testimony was sufficiently corroborated. The Court of Appeal found the High Court Judge correctly evaluated the evidence in its totality considering both direct and circumstantial evidence and properly applied relevant legal principles before finding the appellant guilty as charged.
precedents_cited_list: The Queen Vs. Liyanage (1965) 67 NLR 193 | Illanganthilaka and Others Vs. The Republic of Sri Lanka (1984) 2 SLR 38 | P. Sarawanamuththu Vs. R. A. De Mel (1948) 49 NLR 529 | Pantis Vs. The Attorney General (1998) 2 SLR 148 | Sumanasena Vs. The Attorney General (1999) 3 SLR 137 | Rex Vs. Lord Cochrane and others (1814) Gurneys Reports 479 | Rex Vs. Burdett (1820) B & Ald 161 | Sarwansingh Vs. State of Punjab (2002) AIR Supreme Court (iii) 3652 | State of Himachalpradesh Vs. Thakur Dass (1983) 2 Criminal LJ 1694 | King Vs. Assappu 50 NLR 324
standard_of_review_applied: Appellate review of conviction and sentence examining whether High Court properly evaluated accomplice evidence with corroboration, circumstantial evidence, and whether accused provided reasonable explanation for incriminating circumstances

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna J. | R. Gurusinghe J.
offence_sections: Section 296 read with section 32 of the Penal Code
brief_facts_summary: The two accused-appellants (brothers) had an altercation with their brother-in-law Rupasinghe over a land dispute involving their mother. They assaulted Rupasinghe and his wife with a mamoty. The deceased (Rupasinghe's brother) arrived at the scene with eyewitness Piyarathne. The 1st accused assaulted the deceased, and the 2nd accused stabbed him, causing his death.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 296 read with section 32 of the Penal Code
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the 1st and 2nd accused-appellants and sentenced them to death for murder under section 296 of the Penal Code on 03.08.2016. The 3rd accused was acquitted. The HC found the eyewitness testimony credible and concluded the accused had committed murder with common murderous intention.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The appellants argued that the learned High Court Judge failed to properly evaluate contradictions and omissions in the eyewitness and dying declaration evidence. They contended the trial judge misdirected himself on how to consider contradictions, wrongly found witnesses impartial, failed to properly consider medical evidence, and erred in finding a pre-planned murder with common murderous intention when there was no evidence of premeditation.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Failure to evaluate contradictions and omissions in eyewitness evidence | Failure to consider inherent weaknesses and principles relating to dying declarations | Failure to analyse evidence in a fair and just manner | Misdirection on how contradictions and omissions should be considered | Misdirection in finding witnesses were impartial when they were related to the deceased | Failure to consider medical evidence properly regarding timing of death | Error in stating defence made suggestion only to eyewitness at late stage | Error in finding pre-planned murder with common murderous intention without evidence | Failure to provide reasons whether accused committed murder or culpable homicide not amounting to murder
gnd_other_description: Failure to determine under which limb of Section 294 the accused was convicted for murder
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: The prosecution led eight witnesses, with A.R.M Piyarathne (PW 1) as the sole eyewitness and A.M Kumarasiri (PW 8) providing dying declaration evidence. Two contradictions and two omissions were marked in the eyewitness evidence. Three contradictions and one omission were highlighted in Kumarasiri's evidence, which also contradicted the eyewitness on material points.
probability_assessment_by_court: COA found the HC failed to give benefit of doubt to the appellants as required in criminal cases and went out of the way to find excuses for contradictions and omissions
defence_version_summary: All three accused persons gave dock statements and merely stated they were innocent of the charges. The defence suggested the deceased and Piyarathne first attacked the appellants. No defence witnesses were called. The court found this defence version was not properly considered by the trial judge.
legal_errors_description: The HC failed to properly evaluate contradictions and omissions, misdirected itself on dying declaration principles, failed to distinguish between murder and culpable homicide not amounting to murder, failed to provide reasons per Section 283 of the Criminal Procedure Act, and failed to specify under which limb of Section 294 the conviction was based. The HC also erred in finding pre-planned murder without evidence and wrongly applied principles regarding defence suggestions to witnesses.
procedural_defects_description: The HC failed to provide reasons as per Section 283 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act to explain whether the accused committed murder or culpable homicide not amounting to murder. The prosecution failed to ask the Judicial Medical Officer whether the injuries were sufficient to cause death in the ordinary course of nature.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal found multiple legal errors by the High Court. The COA held that the HC went out of the way to find excuses for contradictions and omissions, failing to give the benefit of doubt to the appellants. The medical evidence showing death would result in 3-4 minutes contradicted the dying declaration evidence, which was not properly considered. The HC erroneously found pre-planned murder with common murderous intention when there was no evidence of premeditation. Only two injuries were found (one head cut and one fatal chest stab), and if the accused intended to kill, they could have inflicted more injuries. The prosecution failed to prove the offence of murder under Section 294, as they did not establish whether the injuries were sufficient to cause death in the ordinary course of nature. The HC failed to specify under which limb of Section 294 the conviction was based and failed to provide reasons distinguishing murder from culpable homicide. The COA concluded the case should be culpable homicide not amounting to murder based on sudden fight, and reduced the sentence to 7 years rigorous imprisonment plus a fine of Rs. 5,000.
coa_sentence_type: Conviction set aside and replaced with conviction for culpable homicide not amounting to murder under section 297 of the Penal Code based on sudden fight. Sentence of rigorous imprisonment for 7 years imposed, with fine of Rs. 5,000 and in default 3 months simple imprisonment
brief_judgment_file_summary: Two accused-appellants (brothers) were convicted by the High Court of Kuliyapitiya and sentenced to death for murdering their brother-in-law on 18.08.2005 following a land dispute. The deceased was stabbed during a fight. The appellants appealed, arguing the HC failed to properly evaluate contradictions in eyewitness and dying declaration evidence, and erred in finding pre-planned murder. The Court of Appeal found the HC made multiple legal errors, including failure to distinguish murder from culpable homicide and failure to properly assess medical evidence. The COA set aside the murder conviction and death sentence, replacing it with culpable homicide not amounting to murder based on sudden fight, and imposed 7 years rigorous imprisonment plus a fine of Rs. 5,000. The appeal was partly allowed.
defence_counsel: Shavindra Fernando PC with Tharani Mayadunne AAL and Ranjith Rajapathirana AAL for the 01st accused-appellant; Ranjith Rajapathirana AAL for the 02nd Accused-Appellant
hc_analysis_summary: The HC concluded the accused had common murderous intention with pre-plan and premeditation, found the eyewitness credible and witnesses impartial, and accepted Section 27 recoveries as corroborative. The COA found these conclusions erroneous, noting the HC went out of the way to find excuses for contradictions, failed to properly consider medical evidence timing, and wrongly concluded there was pre-planned murder without evidence.
precedents_cited_list: Ranasinghe vs. Attorney General 2007 (1) SLR 216 | Ajith Samarakoon vs. State (Kobeigane Murder) 2004 (2) SLR 209 | Farook vs. AG 2006 (3) SLR 174 | Ariyadasa vs. AG 2012 (1) SLR 84 | Vithana and another v. Republic 2007 (1) SLR 169 | R v Chandrasekera 44 NLR 97
standard_of_review_applied: The Court of Appeal reviewed whether the High Court properly evaluated evidence, applied correct legal principles in distinguishing murder from culpable homicide, and whether the prosecution proved all necessary elements of murder under Section 294 of the Penal Code
sentencing_principles_applied: The COA considered the circumstances of sudden fight, the nature and number of injuries inflicted, the lack of evidence of premeditation, and determined that culpable homicide not amounting to murder was the appropriate conviction. A sentence of 7 years rigorous imprisonment was imposed taking effect from the date of original HC conviction (03.08.2016).
final_charge_after_appeal: Culpable homicide not amounting to murder under section 297 of the Penal Code based on sudden fight (modified from murder under section 296)

judges: N. Bandula Karunarathna J. | R. Gurusinghe J.
offence_sections: Section 354 of the Penal Code (Count 01); Section 365b(2)b of The Penal Code as Amended by Act No 22 of 1995 (Counts 02 and 03)
brief_facts_summary: The accused-appellant kidnapped Colambage Salin Naveen Dewishan, a child under 16 years of age, from his lawful guardian between 01.07.2016 and 31.07.2016. During the same period and in a separate incident, the accused-appellant committed cruel sex on the victim, constituting grave sexual abuse on two separate counts. The indictment was amended on 04.03.2020, and the accused pleaded guilty to all three counts.
hc_offence_of_conviction_sections: Section 354 of the Penal Code (Count 01); Section 365b(2)b of The Penal Code as Amended by Act No 22 of 1995 (Counts 02 and 03)
hc_sentence_type: 18 years rigorous imprisonment (Counts 02 and 03 concurrent); 2 years rigorous imprisonment (Count 01); Fine Rs. 25,000 total with default imprisonment; Compensation Rs. 600,000 with default imprisonment
hc_judgment_summary: The High Court convicted the accused-appellant on all three counts after he pleaded guilty to the amended indictment. The court imposed 18 years rigorous imprisonment each for counts 2 and 3 (to run concurrently), 2 years rigorous imprisonment for count 1, fines totaling Rs. 25,000, and compensation of Rs. 600,000 to the victim.
grounds_of_appeal_raw_text_summary: The accused-appellant appealed only against the sentence imposed by the High Court, not challenging the conviction. The defence counsel requested that the sentence be reduced and that mandatory imprisonment be imposed from the date of the Court of Appeal judgment, arguing for leniency given that the accused pleaded guilty and saved valuable court time.
grounds_of_appeal_structured_notes: Sentence is excessive | Request for reduction of imprisonment term | Request for backdating imprisonment from date of COA judgment
witness_evidence_analysis_summary: This was a guilty plea case with no trial on the merits. The accused pleaded guilty to all three counts in the amended indictment. No witnesses were called or evidence presented as the conviction was based solely on the guilty plea.
probability_assessment_by_court: Not applicable as this was a guilty plea case
defence_version_summary: The accused pleaded guilty to all three counts in the amended indictment. No witnesses were called by the defence. The Court of Appeal noted that the accused was an unmarried person without previous convictions who pleaded guilty and saved valuable court time.
court_of_appeal_analysis_summary: The Court of Appeal acknowledged that the accused-appellant was technically guilty of the offence under Section 365b(2)b of the Penal Code but found the 18-year sentence excessive. The Court noted mitigating factors including that the accused was unmarried, had no previous convictions, pleaded guilty to the amended charges, and saved valuable court time. The Court applied the principle that an accused person who pleads guilty should be treated leniently and given a chance to rectify his mistakes and return to society as a good person. After considering the facts, circumstances, and submissions, the Court held that this was not a case warranting the maximum custodial sentence and reduced the imprisonment from 18 years to 8 years for counts 2 and 3, while affirming all fines, compensation, and default terms.
coa_sentence_type: 8 years rigorous imprisonment each for Counts 02 and 03 (concurrent); 2 years rigorous imprisonment for Count 01; Fine Rs. 25,000 total with default imprisonment; Compensation Rs. 600,000 with default imprisonment; sentences backdated to 14.12.2020
brief_judgment_file_summary: The accused-appellant, Chakrawarthige Akila Risinath Dabarera, was convicted by the High Court of Chilaw on three counts: kidnapping a child under 16 years (Section 354 Penal Code) and grave sexual abuse on two separate occasions (Section 365b(2)b Penal Code as amended). He pleaded guilty to the amended indictment and was sentenced to 18 years rigorous imprisonment (concurrent for counts 2 and 3), 2 years for count 1, fines of Rs. 25,000, and compensation of Rs. 600,000. The accused appealed only the sentence. The Court of Appeal, considering mitigating factors including his guilty plea, no previous convictions, and the principle of treating guilty pleas leniently, reduced the sentence from 18 years to 8 years rigorous imprisonment for counts 2 and 3, while affirming all other aspects of the sentence.
hc_analysis_summary: The High Court acted under Section 197 of the Criminal Procedure Code and convicted the accused based on his guilty plea to the amended charges. The Court of Appeal found no legal errors in the conviction process but determined that the 18-year sentence was excessive given the circumstances and mitigating factors.
standard_of_review_applied: Sentencing appeal review considering mitigating factors and the principle of leniency for guilty pleas
sentencing_principles_applied: The Court applied the principle that an accused person who pleads guilty to counts in an indictment should be treated leniently and given a chance to rectify his mistakes and return to society as a good person. The Court considered mitigating factors including lack of previous convictions, guilty plea, and saving court time.
